title: The Woman Who Waters Dead Plants
title_zh: 给枯萎植物浇水的女人
text_zh: 对面阳台上住着一位六十多岁的女人，每天傍晚都会准时出现，手里端着一只掉了漆的铁皮水壶，慢条斯理地给阳台上那一排花盆浇水。问题在于，那些植物早就枯死了。干瘪的茎秆像被时间遗弃的骨架，土壤龟裂成不规则的碎片，连一片嫩芽都不曾冒出来。邻居们私下议论过这件事，有人觉得她精神不太正常，有人猜测她只是懒得清理。我起初也持类似的看法，直到有一天黄昏，我碰巧在楼道里遇见她。她主动跟我搭话，语气平淡却清醒：「你是不是也觉得我在做无用功？」我不好意思否认。她笑了笑说：「这些花是我丈夫在世的时候种的。他走了以后我试过重新种，可怎么也养不活。后来我想，浇水这个动作本身就够了——它让我每天傍晚还能站在这里，做一件跟他有关的事。」那天之后，我再也没有觉得那些花是枯死的。
text_pinyin: Duìmiàn yángtái shàng zhù zhe yí wèi liùshí duō suì de nǚrén, měi tiān bàngwǎn dōu huì zhǔnshí chūxiàn, shǒu lǐ duān zhe yì zhī diào le qī de tiěpí shuǐhú, màntiáosīlǐ de gěi yángtái shàng nà yí pái huāpén jiāo shuǐ. Wèntí zàiyú, nàxiē zhíwù zǎo jiù kūsǐ le. Gānbiě de jīnggǎn xiàng bèi shíjiān yíqì de gǔjià, tǔrǎng guīliè chéng bù guīzé de suìpiàn, lián yí piàn nènyá dōu bù céng mào chūlái. Línjū men sīxià yìlùn guò zhè jiàn shì, yǒu rén juéde tā jīngshén bú tài zhèngcháng, yǒu rén cāicè tā zhǐ shì lǎn de qīnglǐ. Wǒ qǐchū yě chí lèisì de kànfǎ, zhídào yǒu yì tiān huánghūn, wǒ pèngqiǎo zài lóudào lǐ yùjiàn tā. Tā zhǔdòng gēn wǒ dā huà, yǔqì píngdàn què qīngxǐng: 「Nǐ shì bú shì yě juéde wǒ zài zuò wúyòng gōng?」Wǒ bù hǎoyìsi fǒurèn. Tā xiào le xiào shuō: 「Zhèxiē huā shì wǒ zhàngfu zàishì de shíhou zhòng de. Tā zǒu le yǐhòu wǒ shì guò chóngxīn zhòng, kě zěnme yě yǎng bù huó. Hòulái wǒ xiǎng, jiāo shuǐ zhège dòngzuò běnshēn jiù gòu le——tā ràng wǒ měi tiān bàngwǎn hái néng zhàn zài zhèlǐ, zuò yí jiàn gēn tā yǒuguān de shì.」Wǒ hūrán míngbai, yíshì de yìyì cónglái bù qǔjué yú jiéguǒ. Kūwěi de huāpén lǐ chéngfàng de bú shì zhíwù, ér shì yí duàn bù kěn sànchǎng de jìyì. Shuǐ jiāo xiàqù, zhēngfā le yě hǎo, shèn jìn tǔ lǐ yě bà, nàge wānyāo, qīng hú, děngdài de zīshì cái shì zhēnzhèng zài shēngzhǎng de dōngxi.
text_en: A woman in her sixties lives on the balcony across from mine and appears punctually every evening, holding a chipped tin watering can, methodically watering the row of flower pots on her balcony. The problem is that those plants died long ago. The shriveled stems stand like skeletons abandoned by time, the soil cracked into irregular fragments—not a single sprout has ever emerged. The neighbors have discussed this privately; some think she's mentally unwell, others guess she's simply too lazy to clean up. I initially held a similar view, until one evening I happened to run into her in the hallway. She struck up a conversation, her tone flat but lucid: 'Do you also think I'm doing something pointless?' I was too embarrassed to deny it. She smiled slightly and said: 'These flowers were planted by my husband when he was alive. After he passed, I tried replanting, but nothing would grow. Eventually I realized the act of watering itself is enough—it lets me stand here every evening and do something connected to him.' I suddenly understood that the meaning of ritual never depends on outcome. What the withered pots hold is not plants but a memory that refuses to leave. The water pours down—evaporating or seeping into soil—but the posture of bending, tilting the can, and waiting is what's truly growing.

questions.q_zh: 邻居们对这位女人浇水的行为有什么看法？
questions.q_en: What do the neighbors think of the woman's watering behavior?

options.text: 都很感动，觉得她深爱丈夫
options.pinyin: dōu hěn gǎndòng, juéde tā shēn ài zhàngfu
options.text_en: They are all moved and think she deeply loves her husband

options.text: 有人觉得她精神有问题，有人觉得她只是懒
options.pinyin: yǒu rén juéde tā jīngshén yǒu wèntí, yǒu rén juéde tā zhǐ shì lǎn
options.text_en: Some think she's mentally unwell, others think she's just lazy

options.pinyin: dàjiā dōu lǐjiě tā de zuòfǎ
options.text_en: Everyone understands her behavior

options.pinyin: méiyǒu rén zhùyì dào zhè jiàn shì
options.text_en: No one has noticed

questions.explanation: The passage explicitly states neighbors split into two camps: some questioned her mental state, others assumed laziness. No one understood the ritual's emotional purpose.

questions.q_zh: 女人为什么坚持给枯死的植物浇水？
questions.q_en: Why does the woman persist in watering the dead plants?

options.pinyin: tā xiāngxìn zhíwù hái néng fùhuó
options.text_en: She believes the plants can be revived

options.text: 浇水这个动作让她与去世的丈夫保持联系
options.pinyin: jiāo shuǐ zhège dòngzuò ràng tā yǔ qùshì de zhàngfu bǎochí liánxì
options.text_en: The act of watering keeps her connected to her deceased husband

options.text: 她不知道植物已经枯死了
options.pinyin: tā bù zhīdào zhíwù yǐjīng kūsǐ le
options.text_en: She doesn't know the plants are dead

options.text: 她想给邻居留下勤劳的印象
options.pinyin: tā xiǎng gěi línjū liúxià qínláo de yìnxiàng
options.text_en: She wants to appear hardworking to neighbors

questions.explanation: She explains directly: her husband planted the flowers, and watering lets her 'do something connected to him' every evening. The act substitutes for the relationship, not the botany.

questions.q_zh: 作者最终领悟到了什么道理？
questions.q_en: What truth does the author ultimately realize?

options.pinyin: rén yīnggāi xuéhuì fàngxià guòqù
options.text_en: People should learn to let go of the past

options.text: 植物需要特别的照顾才能存活
options.pinyin: zhíwù xūyào tèbié de zhàogù cái néng cúnhuó
options.text_en: Plants need special care to survive

options.text: 仪式的意义不在于结果，而在于动作本身
options.pinyin: yíshì de yìyì bú zàiyú jiéguǒ, ér zàiyú dòngzuò běnshēn
options.text_en: The meaning of ritual lies not in outcome but in the act itself

options.text: 孤独的人容易做出奇怪的事
options.pinyin: gūdú de rén róngyì zuò chū qíguài de shì
options.text_en: Lonely people tend to do strange things

questions.explanation: The narrator's epiphany—'the meaning of ritual never depends on outcome'—reframes watering dead plants as meaningful through repetition alone, not results.

title: The Bench Nobody Sits On
text_zh: 小区花园里有十二把长椅，其中十一把都被居民们用得油光锃亮，唯独靠近东墙的那一把永远空着。椅面上落满了银杏叶，下雨天积着浅浅的水洼，仿佛一块被集体遗忘的领地。我在这个小区住了三年才注意到这个现象，随后开始留意，发现确实没有人愿意坐那把椅子。遛狗的大爷绕着走，带孩子的妈妈宁可站着也不过去。我问过物业，他们说那把椅子和其他的完全一样，没有任何质量问题。后来一位住了二十年的老住户告诉我原委：十几年前，一个独居老人经常坐在那里晒太阳，某天早上被人发现在椅子上过世了，表情很安详。从此以后，那把椅子就被赋予了某种禁忌的意味。理性上大家都清楚这不过是巧合，但身体会诚实地绕开它。我有时故意走过去坐一会儿，阳光和别处没有两样，风穿过银杏树的声音甚至更清晰。也许真正让人回避的不是死亡本身，而是一个具体的、可被指认的死亡现场——我们能接受抽象的终结，却害怕它落在某一把椅子、某一个下午。
text_pinyin: Xiǎoqū huāyuán lǐ yǒu shí'èr bǎ chángyǐ, qízhōng shíyī bǎ dōu bèi jūmín men yòng de yóuguāng zèngliàng, wéidú kàojìn dōng qiáng de nà yì bǎ yǒngyuǎn kōng zhe. Yǐ miàn shàng luò mǎn le yínxìng yè, xiàyǔ tiān jī zhe qiǎnqiǎn de shuǐwā, fǎngfú yí kuài bèi jítǐ yíwàng de lǐngdì. Wǒ zài zhège xiǎoqū zhù le sān nián cái zhùyì dào zhège xiànxiàng, suíhòu kāishǐ liúyì, fāxiàn quèshí méiyǒu rén yuànyì zuò nà bǎ yǐzi. Liú gǒu de dàyé rào zhe zǒu, dài háizi de māma nìngkě zhàn zhe yě bú guòqù. Wǒ wèn guò wùyè, tāmen shuō nà bǎ yǐzi hé qítā de wánquán yíyàng, méiyǒu rènhé zhìliàng wèntí. Hòulái yí wèi zhù le èrshí nián de lǎo zhùhù gàosù wǒ yuánwěi: shí jǐ nián qián, yí gè dújū lǎorén jīngcháng zuò zài nàlǐ shài tàiyáng, mǒu tiān zǎoshang bèi rén fāxiàn zài yǐzi shàng guòshì le, biǎoqíng hěn ānxiáng. Cóngcǐ yǐhòu, nà bǎ yǐzi jiù bèi fùyǔ le mǒu zhǒng jìnjì de yìwèi. Lǐxìng shàng dàjiā dōu qīngchu zhè búguò shì qiǎohé, dàn shēntǐ huì chéngshí de ràokāi tā. Wǒ yǒushí gùyì zǒu guòqù zuò yíhuìr, yángguāng hé biéchù méiyǒu liǎngyàng, fēng chuānguò yínxìng shù de shēngyīn shènzhì gèng qīngxī. Yěxǔ zhēnzhèng ràng rén huíbì de bú shì sǐwáng běnshēn, ér shì yí gè jùtǐ de, kě bèi zhǐrèn de sǐwáng xiànchǎng——wǒmen néng jiēshòu chōuxiàng de zhōngjié, què hàipà tā luò zài mǒu yì bǎ yǐzi, mǒu yí gè xiàwǔ.
text_en: There are twelve benches in the community garden; eleven of them have been polished glossy by residents' use, but the one near the east wall is perpetually empty. Its surface is covered with ginkgo leaves, collecting shallow puddles on rainy days, like a territory collectively forgotten. I lived in this community for three years before noticing this phenomenon, then began paying attention and confirmed that indeed no one would sit there. The old man walking his dog detours around it; mothers with children would rather stand than go near. I asked the property management and they said the bench is identical to the others with no quality issues. Later a resident of twenty years told me the story: over a decade ago, an elderly man who lived alone used to sit there sunbathing, and one morning was found to have passed away on the bench, his expression peaceful. From then on, the bench acquired a kind of taboo meaning. Rationally everyone knows it's mere coincidence, but the body honestly avoids it. I sometimes deliberately go sit there for a while—the sunlight is no different from elsewhere, and the sound of wind through the ginkgo trees is even clearer.

questions.q_zh: 那把长椅为什么没人坐？
questions.q_en: Why does no one sit on that bench?

options.text: 椅子有质量问题，坐着不舒服
options.pinyin: yǐzi yǒu zhìliàng wèntí, zuò zhe bù shūfu
options.text_en: The bench has quality issues and is uncomfortable

options.pinyin: wèizhi bù hǎo, shài bú dào tàiyáng
options.text_en: It's poorly located with no sunlight

options.text: 曾有老人在上面去世，大家觉得不吉利
options.pinyin: céng yǒu lǎorén zài shàngmiàn qùshì, dàjiā juéde bú jílì
options.text_en: An elderly person once died on it and people find it unlucky

options.text: 物业规定不能坐那把椅子
options.pinyin: wùyè guīdìng bù néng zuò nà bǎ yǐzi
options.text_en: Property management forbids sitting there

questions.explanation: The passage traces avoidance to a specific death: an elderly person died on that bench, and residents collectively treated it as unlucky—a taboo without formal declaration.

questions.q_zh: 作者坐在那把椅子上时感受如何？
questions.q_en: How does the author feel sitting on that bench?

options.pinyin: gǎndào hàipà hé bù'ān
options.text_en: Feels afraid and uneasy

options.text: 阳光和风声跟别处没有区别，甚至更好
options.pinyin: yángguāng hé fēngshēng gēn biéchù méiyǒu qūbié, shènzhì gèng hǎo
options.text_en: The sunlight and wind sound no different, even better

options.text: 觉得椅子确实有些奇怪
options.pinyin: juéde yǐzi quèshí yǒuxiē qíguài
options.text_en: Feels the bench is indeed somewhat strange

options.text: 想起了自己去世的亲人
options.pinyin: xiǎngqǐ le zìjǐ qùshì de qīnrén
options.text_en: Thinks of his own deceased relatives

questions.explanation: The author sits down and reports identical sensory experience—same sunlight, same wind—undermining the neighbors' aversion with direct physical evidence.

questions.q_zh: 文章最后表达了什么观点？
questions.q_en: What viewpoint does the article express at the end?

options.text: 人们应该克服对死亡的恐惧
options.pinyin: rénmen yīnggāi kèfú duì sǐwáng de kǒngjù
options.text_en: People should overcome their fear of death

options.text: 小区应该搬走那把椅子
options.pinyin: xiǎoqū yīnggāi bān zǒu nà bǎ yǐzi
options.text_en: The community should remove the bench

options.text: 人能接受抽象的死亡，却害怕具体可辨认的死亡现场
options.pinyin: rén néng jiēshòu chōuxiàng de sǐwáng, què hàipà jùtǐ kě biànrèn de sǐwáng xiànchǎng
options.text_en: People can accept abstract death but fear a specific identifiable death scene

options.text: 迷信思想在现代社会依然根深蒂固
options.pinyin: míxìn sīxiǎng zài xiàndài shèhuì yīrán gēnshēndìgù
options.text_en: Superstitious thinking remains deep-rooted in modern society

questions.explanation: The passage contrasts accepting 'everyone dies' abstractly versus recoiling from a bench where death has a known face and location. Proximity, not mortality, triggers avoidance.

title: The Repairman's Hands
text_zh: 楼下修自行车的老张有一双让人过目不忘的手。指关节粗大得不成比例，指甲缝里永远嵌着洗不掉的黑色油污，掌心的纹路深得像干涸的河床。我第一次注意到他的手，是看他拆卸一个极其精密的变速器——那双粗糙的手忽然变得异常灵巧，每一个动作都恰到好处，像钢琴家在键盘上滑行。他跟我说他修了四十年自行车，从二八大杠修到山地车再到如今的共享单车。他说手是有记忆的，有些零件他闭着眼睛都能装回去。我问他有没有想过退休，他低头看了看自己的手，说：「这双手离开了扳手和螺丝就不知道往哪儿放。」这句话让我想了很久。我们常说一个人的身份是由思想或职位决定的，但对老张来说，身份就长在手上——那些伤疤、老茧、变形的指节，每一处都是四十年劳作的化石记录。他的手已经不是工具，而是他本人。如果有一天他不得不放下扳手，失去的不只是一份工作，而是一整套关于自我的叙事。
text_pinyin: Lóu xià xiū zìxíngchē de Lǎo Zhāng yǒu yì shuāng ràng rén guòmùbúwàng de shǒu. Zhǐ guānjié cūdà de bù chéng bǐlì, zhǐjia fèng lǐ yǒngyuǎn qiàn zhe xǐ bú diào de hēisè yóuwū, zhǎngxīn de wénlù shēn de xiàng gānhé de héchuáng. Wǒ dì yī cì zhùyì dào tā de shǒu, shì kàn tā chāixiè yí gè jíqí jīngmì de biànsùqì——nà shuāng cūcāo de shǒu hūrán biàn de yìcháng língqiǎo, měi yí gè dòngzuò dōu qiàdàohǎochù, xiàng gāngqínjiā zài jiànpán shàng huáxíng. Tā gēn wǒ shuō tā xiū le sìshí nián zìxíngchē, cóng èrbā dàgàng xiū dào shāndìchē zài dào rújīn de gòngxiǎng dānchē. Tā shuō shǒu shì yǒu jìyì de, yǒuxiē língjiàn tā bì zhe yǎnjing dōu néng zhuāng huíqù. Wǒ wèn tā yǒu méi yǒu xiǎng guò tuìxiū, tā dītóu kàn le kàn zìjǐ de shǒu, shuō: 「Zhè shuāng shǒu líkāi le bānshǒu hé luósī jiù bù zhīdào wǎng nǎr fàng.」Zhè jù huà ràng wǒ xiǎng le hěn jiǔ. Wǒmen cháng shuō yí gè rén de shēnfèn shì yóu sīxiǎng huò zhíwèi juédìng de, dàn duì Lǎo Zhāng lái shuō, shēnfèn jiù zhǎng zài shǒu shàng——nàxiē shāngbā, lǎojiǎn, biànxíng de zhǐjié, měi yí chù dōu shì sìshí nián láozuò de huàshí jìlù. Tā de shǒu yǐjīng bú shì gōngjù, ér shì tā běnrén. Rúguǒ yǒu yì tiān tā bùdébù fàngxià bānshǒu, shīqù de bù zhǐ shì yí fèn gōngzuò, ér shì yí zhěng tào guānyú zìwǒ de xùshì.
text_en: Old Zhang, the bicycle repairman downstairs, has a pair of unforgettable hands. His finger joints are disproportionately large, the nail crevices permanently embedded with indelible black grease, the palm lines deep as dried riverbeds. The first time I noticed his hands was watching him disassemble an extremely precise derailleur—those rough hands suddenly became extraordinarily deft, every movement perfectly calibrated, like a pianist gliding across keys. He told me he'd been repairing bicycles for forty years, from the old Flying Pigeon models to mountain bikes to today's bike-shares. He said hands have memory; some parts he can reassemble with his eyes closed. I asked if he'd ever thought about retiring. He looked down at his hands and said: 'These hands wouldn't know where to go without wrenches and screws.' That line stayed with me for a long time. We often say a person's identity is determined by thoughts or position, but for Old Zhang, identity grows right on the hands—every scar, callus, and deformed knuckle is a fossil record of forty years of labor. His hands are no longer tools; they are him.

questions.q_zh: 老张的手有什么特点？
questions.q_en: What is distinctive about Old Zhang's hands?

options.pinyin: fēicháng báijìng xìnèn
options.text_en: Very clean and delicate

options.text: 粗大、有油污和深纹，但异常灵巧
options.pinyin: cūdà, yǒu yóuwū hé shēn wén, dàn yìcháng língqiǎo
options.text_en: Large, greasy with deep lines, but extraordinarily deft

options.text: 因为受伤已经无法正常工作
options.pinyin: yīnwèi shòushāng yǐjīng wúfǎ zhèngcháng gōngzuò
options.text_en: Injured and unable to work normally

options.text: 跟普通人的手没什么区别
options.pinyin: gēn pǔtōng rén de shǒu méi shénme qūbié
options.text_en: No different from ordinary hands

questions.explanation: The passage describes oversized joints, grease-filled nail crevices, and palm lines 'deep as dried riverbeds'—yet those same rough hands disassemble derailleurs with pianist-like precision.

questions.q_zh: 老张说「这双手离开了扳手和螺丝就不知道往哪儿放」是什么意思？
questions.q_en: What does Old Zhang mean by 'these hands wouldn't know where to go without wrenches and screws'?

options.text: 他的手已经受损，只能做修理工作
options.pinyin: tā de shǒu yǐjīng shòusǔn, zhǐ néng zuò xiūlǐ gōngzuò
options.text_en: His hands are damaged and can only do repair work

options.text: 修车已经成为他身份的一部分，无法割舍
options.pinyin: xiū chē yǐjīng chéngwéi tā shēnfèn de yí bùfèn, wúfǎ gēshě
options.text_en: Repairing bikes has become part of his identity and is inseparable

options.text: 他除了修车没有其他技能
options.pinyin: tā chúle xiū chē méiyǒu qítā jìnéng
options.text_en: He has no other skills besides bike repair

options.text_en: He's joking

title: Rain on the Overpass
text_zh: 我有个不太说得出口的习惯：下雨天喜欢站在天桥上看底下的人流。这不是什么诗意的行为，只是一种近乎贪婪的观察欲。雨把城市的节奏打乱了，人们被迫暴露出某种日常中隐藏的性格。你能看到一种人撑开伞以后步速丝毫不变，仿佛雨根本不存在，世界依然按照他的日程运转。另一种人会加速奔跑，左手护着公文包，右手举着报纸遮头，姿态狼狈却充满求生本能的活力。最有意思的是第三种人——他们既不加速也不减速，任凭雨水浇在身上，脸上的表情介于认命和享受之间。我总觉得第三种人身上藏着某种哲学，一种对不可控事物的温柔投降。天桥上视角好，还能看到雨滴砸在不同材质上的反应——柏油路溅起细碎的水花，铁皮棚顶震动着发出密集的鼓点，梧桐树叶接住雨水然后弯腰释放。整座城市在雨中变成了一个巨大的乐器，而我是唯一愿意站在指挥台上的傻瓜。
text_pinyin: Wǒ yǒu gè bú tài shuō de chūkǒu de xíguàn: xiàyǔ tiān xǐhuan zhàn zài tiānqiáo shàng kàn dǐxia de rénliú. Zhè bú shì shénme shīyì de xíngwéi, zhǐ shì yì zhǒng jìnhū tānlán de guānchá yù. Yǔ bǎ chéngshì de jiézòu dǎluàn le, rénmen bèi pò bàolù chū mǒu zhǒng rìcháng zhōng yǐncáng de xìnggé. Nǐ néng kàn dào yì zhǒng rén chēng kāi sǎn yǐhòu bùsù sīháo bú biàn, fǎngfú yǔ gēnběn bù cúnzài, shìjiè yīrán ànzhào tā de rìchéng yùnzhuǎn. Lìng yì zhǒng rén huì jiāsù bēnpǎo, zuǒ shǒu hù zhe gōngwénbāo, yòu shǒu jǔ zhe bàozhǐ zhē tóu, zītài lángbèi què chōngmǎn qiúshēng běnnéng de huólì. Zuì yǒu yìsi de shì dì sān zhǒng rén——tāmen jì bù jiāsù yě bù jiǎnsù, rènpíng yǔshuǐ jiāo zài shēn shàng, liǎn shàng de biǎoqíng jièyú rènmìng hé xiǎngshòu zhījiān. Wǒ zǒng juéde dì sān zhǒng rén shēn shàng cáng zhe mǒu zhǒng zhéxué, yì zhǒng duì bùkě kòng shìwù de wēnróu tóuxiáng. Tiānqiáo shàng shìjiǎo hǎo, hái néng kàn dào yǔdī zá zài bùtóng cáizhì shàng de fǎnyìng——bǎiyóu lù jiàn qǐ xìsuì de shuǐhuā, tiěpí péng dǐng zhèndòng zhe fāchū mìjí de gǔdiǎn, wútóng shùyè jiē zhù yǔshuǐ ránhòu wānyāo shìfàng. Zhěng zuò chéngshì zài yǔ zhōng biàn chéng le yí gè jùdà de yuèqì, ér wǒ shì wéiyī yuànyì zhàn zài zhǐhuī tái shàng de shǎguā.
text_en: I have a habit I'm not quite comfortable admitting: on rainy days I like to stand on the overpass and watch the flow of people below. This isn't some poetic act, just a nearly greedy desire to observe. Rain disrupts the city's rhythm, forcing people to reveal character traits hidden in daily life. You can see one type who, after opening their umbrella, doesn't change pace at all, as if rain simply doesn't exist and the world still runs on their schedule. Another type breaks into a sprint, left hand guarding their briefcase, right hand holding a newspaper over their head—a bedraggled posture yet bursting with survival instinct. The most interesting is the third type—they neither speed up nor slow down, letting rain fall on them, their expressions hovering between resignation and enjoyment. I've always felt this third type harbors a certain philosophy: a gentle surrender to what cannot be controlled. The overpass offers a good vantage point; you can also see how raindrops react on different materials—asphalt splashing fine droplets, tin roofs vibrating with dense drumbeats, sycamore leaves catching water then bending to release it. The entire city becomes a vast instrument in the rain, and I'm the only fool willing to stand on the conductor's podium.

title: The Clockmaker's Silence
text_zh: 巷子深处有一家修钟表的小店，门面窄得只容一人侧身进出，里面却别有洞天——三面墙上挂满了各式各样的钟，有落地摆钟、布谷鸟钟、航海天文钟，甚至还有几只显然已经无法修复的古董怀表被装在玻璃罩里供着。店主姓沈，六十出头，说话极少。你把坏了的表递给他，他接过来翻看几秒，然后点点头或摇摇头，整个过程不超过三句话。我最初觉得他冷漠，后来才意识到他的沉默是职业塑造的——修钟表需要绝对的安静，任何多余的声响都可能掩盖齿轮咬合时的细微异响。他活在一个以秒为单位的声音世界里。有一次我等他修表，坐在店里听了将近一个小时的钟声交响：不同的钟在不同的时间发出不同音调的滴答声，偶尔某只钟报时，浑厚的钟声在狭小空间里产生奇异的共鸣。沈师傅抬头看了我一眼，似乎对我能安静坐着表示认可。他后来破例多说了几句：「现在没什么人修表了，都买新的。但一只好表是有脾气的，你得听它说话。」我想，在这个追求效率的年代，愿意倾听一只旧表心跳的人，本身就是一种正在消失的手艺。
text_pinyin: Xiàngzi shēnchù yǒu yì jiā xiū zhōngbiǎo de xiǎo diàn, ménmiàn zhǎi de zhǐ róng yì rén cèshēn jìnchū, lǐmiàn què biéyǒudòngtiān——sān miàn qiáng shàng guà mǎn le gèshìgèyàng de zhōng, yǒu luòdì bǎizhōng, bùgǔniǎo zhōng, hánghǎi tiānwén zhōng, shènzhì hái yǒu jǐ zhī xiǎnrán yǐjīng wúfǎ xiūfù de gǔdǒng huáibiǎo bèi zhuāng zài bōli zhào lǐ gòng zhe. Diànzhǔ xìng Shěn, liùshí chūtóu, shuōhuà jí shǎo. Nǐ bǎ huài le de biǎo dì gěi tā, tā jiē guòlái fānkàn jǐ miǎo, ránhòu diǎndiǎn tóu huò yáoyáo tóu, zhěnggè guòchéng bù chāoguò sān jù huà. Wǒ zuìchū juéde tā lěngmò, hòulái cái yìshí dào tā de chénmò shì zhíyè sùzào de——xiū zhōngbiǎo xūyào juéduì de ānjìng, rènhé duōyú de shēngxiǎng dōu kěnéng yǎngài chǐlún yǎohé shí de xìwēi yìxiǎng. Tā huó zài yí gè yǐ miǎo wéi dānwèi de shēngyīn shìjiè lǐ. Yǒu yí cì wǒ děng tā xiū biǎo, zuò zài diàn lǐ tīng le jiāngjìn yí gè xiǎoshí de zhōngshēng jiāoxiǎng: bùtóng de zhōng zài bùtóng de shíjiān fāchū bùtóng yīndiào de dīdā shēng, ǒu'ěr mǒu zhī zhōng bàoshí, húnhòu de zhōngshēng zài xiáxiǎo kōngjiān lǐ chǎnshēng qíyì de gòngmíng. Shěn shīfu táitóu kàn le wǒ yì yǎn, sìhū duì wǒ néng ānjìng zuò zhe biǎoshì rènkě. Tā hòulái pòlì duō shuō le jǐ jù: 「Xiànzài méi shénme rén xiū biǎo le, dōu mǎi xīn de. Dàn yì zhī hǎo biǎo shì yǒu píqi de, nǐ děi tīng tā shuōhuà.」Wǒ xiǎng, zài zhège zhuīqiú xiàolǜ de niándài, yuànyì qīngtīng yì zhī jiù biǎo xīntiào de rén, běnshēn jiù shì yì zhǒng zhèngzài xiāoshī de shǒuyì.
text_en: Deep in the alley there's a small watch repair shop, the storefront so narrow only one person can enter sideways, yet inside is a hidden world—three walls covered with clocks of every kind: grandfather clocks, cuckoo clocks, marine chronometers, even a few antique pocket watches clearly beyond repair, enshrined under glass domes. The owner, surnamed Shen, is in his early sixties and speaks very little. You hand him a broken watch, he takes it, examines it for a few seconds, then nods or shakes his head—the whole process takes no more than three sentences. I initially found him cold, but later realized his silence was shaped by profession—watch repair requires absolute quiet, and any extraneous sound might mask the subtle irregularities in gear meshing. He lives in a sonic world measured in seconds. Once while waiting for my watch, I sat in the shop and listened to nearly an hour of clock symphony: different clocks producing different-pitched ticking at different intervals, occasionally one striking the hour, its rich tone creating strange resonance in the tight space. Master Shen glanced up at me, seemingly approving of my ability to sit quietly. He then made a rare exception and said a few more words: 'Nobody repairs watches anymore, they all buy new ones.

title: Letters to No One
text_zh: 整理母亲遗物的时候，我在衣柜最底层发现了一只铁盒子，里面装着厚厚一沓信，全部写好、封好、贴好邮票，却从未寄出。收信人是我父亲——他在我十二岁那年因病去世。信的日期从父亲去世后的第三天一直延续到母亲住院前的一个月，跨度将近二十年。我拆开几封来读，内容出奇地平淡：今天买了一条新围巾，颜色你肯定喜欢；楼下的桂花开了，香味飘到五楼；儿子考上大学了，报的是你当年想学的建筑系。没有撕心裂肺的思念，没有对命运的控诉，只是一个人安安静静地向另一个人汇报日常，仿佛对方只是出了一趟远门。我坐在母亲的床边读了一整个下午，从头到尾没有掉一滴眼泪，只是胸口闷得厉害。那些信让我意识到，深沉的悲伤不一定是剧烈的，它可以是一种持续了二十年的低频震动，安静到周围没有任何人察觉。母亲从未在我面前提过这些信，她的日常表现得坚强而有条理。原来坚强有时候不是没有脆弱，而是把脆弱折好，装进信封，放在衣柜最底层。
text_pinyin: Zhěnglǐ mǔqīn yíwù de shíhou, wǒ zài yīguì zuì dǐcéng fāxiàn le yì zhī tiě hézi, lǐmiàn zhuāng zhe hòuhòu yí dá xìn, quánbù xiě hǎo, fēng hǎo, tiē hǎo yóupiào, què cóng wèi jì chū. Shōuxìn rén shì wǒ fùqīn——tā zài wǒ shí'èr suì nà nián yīn bìng qùshì. Xìn de rìqī cóng fùqīn qùshì hòu de dì sān tiān yìzhí yánxù dào mǔqīn zhùyuàn qián de yí gè yuè, kuàdù jiāngjìn èrshí nián. Wǒ chāi kāi jǐ fēng lái dú, nèiróng chūqí de píngdàn: jīntiān mǎi le yì tiáo xīn wéijīn, yánsè nǐ kěndìng xǐhuan; lóu xià de guìhuā kāi le, xiāngwèi piāo dào wǔ lóu; érzi kǎo shàng dàxué le, bào de shì nǐ dāngnián xiǎng xué de jiànzhù xì. Méiyǒu sīxīnlièfèi de sīniàn, méiyǒu duì mìngyùn de kòngsù, zhǐ shì yí gè rén ānānjìngjìng de xiàng lìng yí gè rén huìbào rìcháng, fǎngfú duìfāng zhǐ shì chū le yí tàng yuǎnmén. Wǒ zuò zài mǔqīn de chuáng biān dú le yí zhěnggè xiàwǔ, cóngtóu dào wěi méiyǒu diào yì dī yǎnlèi, zhǐ shì xiōngkǒu mèn de lìhai. Nàxiē xìn ràng wǒ yìshí dào, shēnchén de bēishāng bú yídìng shì jùliè de, tā kěyǐ shì yì zhǒng chíxù le èrshí nián de dīpín zhèndòng, ānjìng dào zhōuwéi méiyǒu rènhé rén chájué. Mǔqīn cóng wèi zài wǒ miànqián tí guò zhèxiē xìn, tā de rìcháng biǎoxiàn de jiānqiáng ér yǒu tiáolǐ. Yuánlái jiānqiáng yǒushíhou bú shì méiyǒu cuìruò, ér shì bǎ cuìruò zhé hǎo, zhuāng jìn xìnfēng, fàng zài yīguì zuì dǐcéng.
text_en: While sorting through my mother's belongings, I found a tin box at the very bottom of the wardrobe. Inside was a thick stack of letters—all written, sealed, stamped, yet never mailed. The addressee was my father, who died of illness when I was twelve. The dates spanned from three days after his death to a month before my mother was hospitalized—nearly twenty years. I opened a few to read, and the content was surprisingly mundane: bought a new scarf today, you'd definitely like the color; the osmanthus downstairs has bloomed, the fragrance drifts up to the fifth floor; our son got into college, enrolled in the architecture department you always wanted to study. No gut-wrenching longing, no accusations against fate—just one person quietly reporting daily life to another, as if the other had merely gone on a long trip. I sat by my mother's bed reading the entire afternoon, not shedding a single tear from start to finish, just a heavy tightness in my chest. Those letters made me realize that profound grief isn't necessarily violent; it can be a low-frequency vibration sustained for twenty years, so quiet that no one around ever notices. My mother never mentioned these letters to me; her daily demeanor was strong and orderly. It turns out that sometimes being strong isn't the absence of fragility, but folding fragility neatly, placing it in an envelope, and storing it at the very bottom of the wardrobe.

questions.q_zh: 这些信有什么特别之处？

options.text: 全部写好封好贴好邮票，但从未寄出

options.text_en: All written, sealed, and stamped, but never mailed

questions.explanation: The passage describes letters fully written, sealed, and stamped—complete in form—yet deliberately never mailed. The ritual of writing was the entire point.

title: The Last Screening
text_zh: 城南那家老电影院终于要拆了。消息传出来的时候，朋友圈里一片唏嘘，但真正去看最后一场放映的人并不多——只有稀稀拉拉二十来个人坐在能容纳三百人的大厅里。放的是一部八十年代的老片子，胶片画质模糊，色调偏黄，音响时不时发出刺耳的电流声。我坐在第七排中间，左边是一对头发花白的老夫妻，右边是一个穿校服的女孩，大概是逃课来的。老夫妻从头到尾没怎么看银幕，倒是一直在小声说话，指着这里那里回忆当年。女孩全程举着手机拍，大概觉得这是某种值得记录的废墟美学。我哪边都不属于——既没有可供怀旧的记忆，也没有把衰败当景观的兴致。我只是觉得，一个空间消失之前应该有人在场见证，就像葬礼需要有人出席一样。散场以后我在门口站了一会儿，看到售票窗口的玻璃上还贴着褪色的排片表。最后走出来的是那位放映员，他锁好门，把钥匙装进口袋，头也不回地走了。那个背影里没有伤感，只有一种干净利落的告别，像剪辑师手起刀落的最后一剪。
text_pinyin: Chéngnán nà jiā lǎo diànyǐngyuàn zhōngyú yào chāi le. Xiāoxi chuán chūlái de shíhou, péngyou quān lǐ yí piàn xīxū, dàn zhēnzhèng qù kàn zuìhòu yì chǎng fàngyìng de rén bìng bù duō——zhǐ yǒu xīxīlālā èrshí lái gè rén zuò zài néng róngnà sānbǎi rén de dàtīng lǐ. Fàng de shì yí bù bāshí niándài de lǎo piànzi, jiāopiàn huàzhì móhu, sèdiào piān huáng, yīnxiǎng shíbùshí fāchū cì'ěr de diànliú shēng. Wǒ zuò zài dì qī pái zhōngjiān, zuǒbiān shì yí duì tóufa huābái de lǎo fūqī, yòubiān shì yí gè chuān xiàofú de nǚhái, dàgài shì táokè lái de. Lǎo fūqī cóngtóu dào wěi méi zěnme kàn yínmù, dàoshì yìzhí zài xiǎoshēng shuōhuà, zhǐ zhe zhèlǐ nàlǐ huíyì dāngnián. Nǚhái quánchéng jǔ zhe shǒujī pāi, dàgài juéde zhè shì mǒu zhǒng zhídé jìlù de fèixū měixué. Wǒ nǎ biān dōu bù shǔyú——jì méiyǒu kě gōng huáijiù de jìyì, yě méiyǒu bǎ shuāibài dāng jǐngguān de xìngzhì. Wǒ zhǐ shì juéde, yí gè kōngjiān xiāoshī zhīqián yīnggāi yǒu rén zàichǎng jiànzhèng, jiù xiàng zànglǐ xūyào yǒu rén chūxí yíyàng. Sànchǎng yǐhòu wǒ zài ménkǒu zhàn le yíhuìr, kàn dào shòupiào chuāngkǒu de bōli shàng hái tiē zhe tuìsè de páipiàn biǎo. Zuìhòu zǒu chūlái de shì nà wèi fàngyìngyuán, tā suǒ hǎo mén, bǎ yàoshi zhuāng jìn kǒudai, tóu yě bù huí de zǒu le. Nàge bèiyǐng lǐ méiyǒu shānggǎn, zhǐ yǒu yì zhǒng gānjìnglìluò de gàobié, xiàng jiǎnjí shī shǒu qǐ dāo luò de zuìhòu yì jiǎn.
text_en: The old cinema in the south of the city is finally being demolished. When the news spread, social media was full of sighs, but not many actually came to the last screening—just a sparse twenty or so people in a hall that seats three hundred. They showed an old film from the eighties, the filmstrip quality blurry, the color tinted yellow, the speakers occasionally emitting harsh static. I sat in the middle of row seven. To my left was an elderly couple with graying hair; to my right a girl in a school uniform, probably skipping class. The old couple barely watched the screen the entire time, instead whispering to each other, pointing here and there, recalling the past. The girl held up her phone filming throughout, apparently viewing this as some kind of ruin aesthetic worth documenting. I belonged to neither side—I had no memories for nostalgia, nor any interest in treating decay as scenery. I just felt that before a space disappears, someone should be present to witness it, the way a funeral requires attendance. After the showing I stood by the entrance for a while, noticing a faded screening schedule still pasted on the ticket window glass. The last to emerge was the projectionist, who locked the door, pocketed the key, and walked away without looking back. In that departing figure there was no sentimentality, only a clean, decisive farewell—like an editor's final cut, the hand rising and the blade falling.

title: The 5:47 Train
text_zh: 每天早上五点四十七分，我都会坐上同一班地铁，三号线往北方向。这个时间点的车厢有一种特殊的生态：乘客大多是清洁工、厨师、保安、便利店店员——那些在城市正式醒来之前就必须就位的人。没有人看手机，不是因为没有信号，而是因为困意还没有完全散去，大家都闭着眼睛，身体随着列车的晃动微微摆动，像一排被同一股潮水推动的海草。我在这班车上坐了两年，渐渐认识了一些面孔。角落里总是坐着一个穿蓝色工作服的中年女人，她每天带一个铝饭盒，车开到第四站的时候准时睡着，第八站准时醒来，从未误过。我佩服这种被身体节律驯化的精确。另一位是个年轻的面包师傅，手上总有洗不掉的面粉味，他喜欢站在车门旁边，一只手扶着栏杆，另一只手在空气中做揉面的动作，也许是在为到店后的工作热身。五点四十七分的列车承载着一座城市最安静的勤劳，这些人没有被任何报道记录过，但他们是城市的底层代码——删掉任何一行，整个系统都会报错。
text_pinyin: Měi tiān zǎoshang wǔ diǎn sìshíqī fēn, wǒ dōu huì zuò shàng tóng yí bān dìtiě, sān hào xiàn wǎng běi fāngxiàng. Zhège shíjiān diǎn de chēxiāng yǒu yì zhǒng tèshū de shēngtài: chéngkè dàduō shì qīngjié gōng, chúshī, bǎo'ān, biànlì diàn diànyuán——nàxiē zài chéngshì zhèngshì xǐng lái zhīqián jiù bìxū jiùwèi de rén. Méiyǒu rén kàn shǒujī, bú shì yīnwèi méiyǒu xìnhào, ér shì yīnwèi kùnyì hái méiyǒu wánquán sàn qù, dàjiā dōu bì zhe yǎnjing, shēntǐ suí zhe lièchē de huàngdòng wēiwēi bǎidòng, xiàng yì pái bèi tóng yí gǔ cháoshuǐ tuīdòng de hǎicǎo. Wǒ zài zhè bān chē shàng zuò le liǎng nián, jiànjiàn rènshi le yìxiē miànkǒng. Jiǎoluò lǐ zǒngshì zuò zhe yí gè chuān lán sè gōngzuòfú de zhōngnián nǚrén, tā měi tiān dài yí gè lǚ fànhé, chē kāi dào dì sì zhàn de shíhou zhǔnshí shuì zháo, dì bā zhàn zhǔnshí xǐng lái, cóng wèi wù guò. Wǒ pèifú zhè zhǒng bèi shēntǐ jiélǜ xùnhuà de jīngquè. Lìng yí wèi shì gè niánqīng de miànbāo shīfu, shǒu shàng zǒng yǒu xǐ bú diào de miànfěn wèi, tā xǐhuan zhàn zài chēmén pángbiān, yì zhī shǒu fú zhe lángān, lìng yì zhī shǒu zài kōngqì zhōng zuò róu miàn de dòngzuò, yěxǔ shì zài wèi dào diàn hòu de gōngzuò rèshēn. Wǔ diǎn sìshíqī fēn de lièchē chéngzài zhe yí zuò chéngshì zuì ānjìng de qínláo, zhèxiē rén méiyǒu bèi rènhé bàodào jìlù guò, dàn tāmen shì chéngshì de dǐcéng dàimǎ——shāndiào rènhé yì háng, zhěnggè xìtǒng dōu huì bàocuò.
text_en: Every morning at 5:47, I take the same subway—Line 3, northbound. The carriage at this hour has a special ecology: most passengers are cleaners, cooks, security guards, convenience store clerks—people who must be in position before the city officially wakes. No one looks at their phone, not because there's no signal, but because drowsiness hasn't fully dispersed. Everyone sits with eyes closed, bodies swaying gently with the train's rocking, like a row of seaweed pushed by the same tide. I've ridden this train for two years and gradually come to recognize certain faces. In the corner always sits a middle-aged woman in blue overalls; she brings an aluminum lunch box every day, falls asleep precisely when the train reaches the fourth stop, and wakes precisely at the eighth—never missed. I admire this precision domesticated by bodily rhythm. Another is a young baker whose hands always carry an indelible flour scent. He likes standing by the door, one hand on the railing, the other kneading air—perhaps warming up for work once he arrives at the shop. The 5:47 train carries a city's quietest diligence. These people have never been covered in any report, but they are the city's base code—delete any line and the whole system throws an error.

title: The Convenience Store at 3 AM
text_zh: 如果你想了解一座城市的真实面孔，凌晨三点去便利店坐一会儿。白天的便利店是功能性的——人们进来买水、付账、离开，整个过程不超过九十秒。但凌晨三点的便利店是另一个物种。灯光惨白得有些失真，冰柜的嗡嗡声成了空间里唯一的底噪。这个时间点走进来的人往往不是为了购物，而是为了寻找一个还亮着灯的、不会赶你走的公共空间。我见过加班到崩溃的年轻人坐在靠窗的高脚凳上发呆，面前的关东煮早就凉透了；见过刚吵完架的情侣一前一后走进来，在货架的两端假装挑东西，其实都在等对方先开口；见过独居的老人来买一盒牛奶，然后站在收银台前跟店员聊了二十分钟的天气。便利店员大多是夜校学生或兼职的年轻人，他们对这一切见怪不怪，既不热情也不冷淡，恰到好处地维持着一种专业的漠然。我觉得这正是凌晨三点便利店的核心价值——它提供的不是商品，而是一种「不被追问」的庇护。在这个人人都要求你解释自己的世界里，一个不问你为什么在凌晨三点独自坐着的地方，本身就是一种温柔。
text_pinyin: Rúguǒ nǐ xiǎng liǎojiě yí zuò chéngshì de zhēnshí miànkǒng, língchén sān diǎn qù biànlì diàn zuò yíhuìr. Báitiān de biànlì diàn shì gōngnéng xìng de——rénmen jìnlái mǎi shuǐ, fù zhàng, líkāi, zhěnggè guòchéng bù chāoguò jiǔshí miǎo. Dàn língchén sān diǎn de biànlì diàn shì lìng yí gè wùzhǒng. Dēngguāng cǎnbái de yǒuxiē shīzhēn, bīngguì de wēngwēng shēng chéng le kōngjiān lǐ wéiyī de dǐzào. Zhège shíjiān diǎn zǒu jìnlái de rén wǎngwǎng bú shì wèile gòuwù, ér shì wèile xúnzhǎo yí gè hái liàng zhe dēng de, bú huì gǎn nǐ zǒu de gōnggòng kōngjiān. Wǒ jiàn guò jiābān dào bēngkuì de niánqīng rén zuò zài kào chuāng de gāojiǎo dèng shàng fādāi, miànqián de guāndōng zhǔ zǎo jiù liáng tòu le; jiàn guò gāng chǎo wán jià de qínglǚ yì qián yí hòu zǒu jìnlái, zài huòjià de liǎng duān jiǎzhuāng tiāo dōngxi, qíshí dōu zài děng duìfāng xiān kāikǒu; jiàn guò dújū de lǎorén lái mǎi yì hé niúnǎi, ránhòu zhàn zài shōuyín tái qián gēn diànyuán liáo le èrshí fēnzhōng de tiānqì. Biànlì diàn yuán dàduō shì yèxiào xuéshēng huò jiānzhí de niánqīng rén, tāmen duì zhè yíqiè jiànguàibùguài, jì bù rèqíng yě bù lěngdàn, qiàdàohǎochù de wéichí zhe yì zhǒng zhuānyè de mòrán. Wǒ juéde zhè zhèng shì língchén sān diǎn biànlì diàn de héxīn jiàzhí——tā tígōng de bú shì shāngpǐn, ér shì yì zhǒng「bú bèi zhuīwèn」de bìhù. Zài zhège rénrén dōu yāoqiú nǐ jiěshì zìjǐ de shìjiè lǐ, yí gè bú wèn nǐ wèishénme zài língchén sān diǎn dúzì zuò zhe de dìfāng, běnshēn jiù shì yì zhǒng wēnróu.
text_en: If you want to understand a city's true face, sit in a convenience store at 3 AM. Daytime convenience stores are functional — buy water, pay, leave, under ninety seconds. But a 3 AM convenience store is a different species. Fluorescent light harsh to distortion, the refrigerator hum the only ambient noise. People at this hour are often not here to shop, but to find a public space still lit that won't chase them away. I've seen young workers, pushed past breaking point by overtime, zoning out on window stools with long-cold oden before them. I've seen couples post-fight walk in one after the other, pretending to browse at opposite ends, both waiting for the other to speak first. I've seen elderly people living alone buy milk, then chat with the clerk about weather for twenty minutes. The clerks — mostly night school students — take all this in stride, maintaining calibrated professional indifference. This is the core value of a 3 AM convenience store: not merchandise, but a shelter of 'not being questioned.' In a world where everyone demands you explain yourself, a place that doesn't ask why you're sitting alone at 3 AM is itself a form of tenderness.

title: Rooftop Vocabulary
text_zh: 搬进这栋老公寓以后，我发现天台是整栋楼隐秘的社交中心。白天它属于晾衣服的阿姨们，万国旗一样的被单和衣物在风里猎猎作响，阿姨们一边收衣服一边交换小区里最新的情报——谁家装修噪音太大，谁家的孩子考上了重点中学，菜市场哪个摊位的虾最新鲜。这是一套独立于互联网之外的信息系统，传播效率惊人且从不需要事实核查。傍晚时分天台换了一批人，多是下班回来的年轻租户，三三两两坐在水泥矮墙上抽烟或喝啤酒，聊的内容大多是工作的不顺和房租的离谱。深夜的天台最安静，偶尔有失眠的人独自上来吹风，站在边缘往下看灯火渐次熄灭的街区。我在天台上待得最久的一次是某个夏夜，空气湿热得像一块拧不干的毛巾。楼下马路上传来零星的车声，远处某栋楼里有人在练萨克斯，断断续续的旋律顺着夜风飘上来。一栋楼就是一个垂直的村庄，天台是村口的老槐树——不适合在室内说的话，都到这里来找听众。
text_pinyin: Bān jìn zhè dòng lǎo gōngyù yǐhòu, wǒ fāxiàn tiāntái shì zhěng dòng lóu yǐnmì de shèjiāo zhōngxīn. Báitiān tā shǔyú liàng yīfu de āyí men, wànguóqí yíyàng de bèidān hé yīwù zài fēng lǐ lièliè zuòxiǎng, āyí men yìbiān shōu yīfu yìbiān jiāohuàn xiǎoqū lǐ zuìxīn de qíngbào——shéi jiā zhuāngxiū zàoyīn tài dà, shéi jiā de háizi kǎo shàng le zhòngdiǎn zhōngxué, càishìchǎng nǎge tānwèi de xiā zuì xīnxiān. Zhè shì yí tào dúlì yú hùliánwǎng zhī wài de xìnxī xìtǒng, chuánbō xiàolǜ jīngrén qiě cóng bù xūyào shìshí héchá. Bàngwǎn shífēn tiāntái huàn le yí pī rén, duō shì xiàbān huílái de niánqīng zūhù, sānsānliǎngliǎng zuò zài shuǐní ǎi qiáng shàng chōuyān huò hē píjiǔ, liáo de nèiróng dàduō shì gōngzuò de bú shùn hé fángzū de lípǔ. Shēnyè de tiāntái zuì ānjìng, ǒu'ěr yǒu shīmián de rén dúzì shànglái chuī fēng, zhàn zài biānyuán wǎng xià kàn dēnghuǒ jiàncì xīmiè de jiēqū. Wǒ zài tiāntái shàng dāi de zuì jiǔ de yí cì shì mǒu gè xià yè, kōngqì shīrè de xiàng yí kuài nǐng bù gān de máojīn. Lóu xià mǎlù shàng chuán lái língxīng de chē shēng, yuǎnchù mǒu dòng lóu lǐ yǒu rén zài liàn sàkèsī, duànduànxùxù de xuánlǜ shùn zhe yè fēng piāo shànglái. Nà yí kè wǒ tūrán juéde, yí dòng lóu jiù shì yí gè chuízhí de cūnzhuāng, ér tiāntái shì cūnkǒu de lǎo huáishù——suǒyǒu bú shìhé zài shìnèi shuō de huà, dōu huì zài zhèlǐ zhǎodào tā de tīngzhòng.
text_en: After moving into this old apartment building, I discovered the rooftop is the building's secret social center. During the day it belongs to the laundry-hanging aunties—sheets and clothes flapping in the wind like flags of all nations, the aunties exchanging the community's latest intelligence while collecting laundry: whose renovation is too noisy, whose kid got into a key middle school, which market stall has the freshest shrimp. It's an information system independent of the internet, with astonishing transmission efficiency and never requiring fact-checking. At dusk the rooftop changes crowds, mostly young tenants back from work, sitting in clusters on the low concrete wall smoking or drinking beer, talking mostly about work frustrations and absurd rent. The late-night rooftop is quietest—occasionally an insomniac comes up alone for air, standing at the edge looking down at neighborhoods where lights extinguish one by one. My longest stay on the rooftop was one summer night when the air was humid and hot like an un-wringable towel. Sporadic traffic sounds drifted up from the street below; somewhere in a distant building someone was practicing saxophone, the intermittent melody floating up on the night breeze.

title: The Retired Postman's Route
text_zh: 老赵退休三年了，但他每天早上还是六点半出门，骑着那辆绿色的自行车，沿着他送了三十年信的路线转一圈。邻居们一开始以为他是在锻炼身体，后来才发现他是真的在「巡视」——看看谁家的信箱满了没人取，哪栋楼的门锁坏了，路边的行道树是不是需要修剪。居委会的小李说，老赵比他们还清楚这片区域的情况。有一次，老赵发现三号楼的张奶奶连续三天没取报纸，就去敲门，发现张奶奶摔倒了，起不来。要不是老赵，后果不堪设想。我问老赵，退休了为什么还要每天走这条路。他想了一会儿说：「走了三十年，这条路上的每棵树、每个信箱、每扇门我都认识。突然不走了，就好像把它们丢下了。」他顿了顿又说：「其实也不是它们需要我，是我需要它们。每天走这一圈，我才觉得自己还有点用处。」说完他站起来，朝左边的巷子走去。那是他邮递路线的第一段。
text_pinyin: Lǎo Zhào tuìxiū sān nián le, dàn tā měi tiān zǎoshang háishi liù diǎn bàn chūmén, qí zhe nà liàng lǜsè de zìxíngchē, yánzhe tā sòng le sānshí nián xìn de lùxiàn zhuǎn yī quān. Línjūmen yī kāishǐ yǐwéi tā shì zài duànliàn shēntǐ, hòulái cái fāxiàn tā shì zhēn de zài 「xúnshì」—— kàn kan shuí jiā de xìnxiāng mǎn le méi rén qǔ, nǎ dòng lóu de ménsuǒ huài le, lùbiān de xíngdàoshù shì bú shì xūyào xiūjiǎn. Jūwěihuì de xiǎo Lǐ shuō, lǎo Zhào bǐ tāmen hái qīngchu zhè piàn qūyù de qíngkuàng. Yǒu yī cì, lǎo Zhào fāxiàn sān hào lóu de Zhāng nǎinai liánxù sān tiān méi qǔ bàozhǐ, jiù qù qiāo mén, fāxiàn Zhāng nǎinai shuāi dǎo le, qǐ bù lái. Yào búshi lǎo Zhào, hòuguǒ bùkān shèxiǎng. Wǒ wèn lǎo Zhào, tuìxiū le wèishéme hái yào měi tiān zǒu zhè tiáo lù. Tā xiǎng le yīhuìr shuō: 「Zǒu le sānshí nián, zhè tiáo lù shàng de měi kē shù, měi gè xìnxiāng, měi shàn mén wǒ dōu rènshi. Tūrán bù zǒu le, jiù hǎoxiàng bǎ tāmen diū xià le.」Tā dùn le dùn yòu shuō: 「Qíshí yě bú shì tāmen xūyào wǒ, shì wǒ xūyào tāmen. Měi tiān zǒu zhè yī quān, wǒ cái juéde zìjǐ hái yǒu diǎn yòngchu.」Nà yī kè wǒ tūrán míngbai le, yǒuxiē rén de jiàzhígǎn bú shì lái zì tóuxián huò shōurù, ér shì lái zì yī tiáo zǒu le wúshù biàn de lù.
text_en: Old Zhao retired three years ago, but every morning he still goes out at 6:30, riding that green bicycle of his, making one loop along the route where he delivered mail for thirty years. The neighbors first assumed he was exercising, but later realized he was genuinely 'patrolling'—checking whether anyone's mailbox was overflowing, whether any building's door lock was broken, whether the roadside trees needed trimming. Xiao Li from the neighborhood committee said Old Zhao knows the state of this area better than they do. Once, Old Zhao noticed that Grandma Zhang in Building 3 hadn't collected her newspaper for three days straight, so he went and knocked on her door—and found she had fallen and couldn't get up. If not for Old Zhao, the consequences would have been unthinkable. I asked Old Zhao why he still walks this route every day after retirement. He thought for a moment and said: 'I walked it for thirty years. Every tree on this road, every mailbox, every door—I know them all. To suddenly stop would feel like abandoning them.' He paused, then added: 'Actually, it's not that they need me. It's that I need them. Walking this loop every day is the only way I feel I still have some use.' Then I suddenly understood: some people's sense of worth comes not from titles or income, but from a road they've walked countless times.

title: My Grandmother's Two Accents
text_zh: 外婆在上海住了五十多年，普通话说得已经很标准了，带一点上海味道，但不仔细听的话根本听不出来。可每次接到老家湖南亲戚的电话，她的口音会在几秒钟之内切换回湖南话，语速变快，声调也完全不一样。小时候我觉得这很有趣，像看一个人变魔术。长大以后我慢慢理解了：语言不只是交流的工具，它还是一种情感的容器。外婆用普通话过她在上海的日常生活——买菜、跟邻居聊天、看电视新闻。但她用湖南话装着另一个自己——那个十八岁离开家乡、坐了三天火车来到上海的女孩。有一年过年，外婆喝了点酒，突然用湖南话唱了一首歌，谁也听不懂歌词，但那个旋律让她哭了。后来她擦了擦眼泪说：「这是我妈教我的，我怕哪天忘了。」那一刻我理解了一件事：有些人并不是在两种口音之间切换，而是在两个自己之间来回走动。而连接这两个自己的，不过是一条看不见的声音的线。
text_pinyin: Wàipó zài Shànghǎi zhù le wǔshí duō nián, pǔtōnghuà shuō de yǐjīng hěn biāozhǔn le, dài yīdiǎn Shànghǎi wèidao, dàn bù zǐxì tīng de huà gēnběn tīng bù chūlái. Kě měi cì jiē dào lǎojiā Húnán qīnqi de diànhuà, tā de kǒuyīn huì zài jǐ miǎozhōng zhī nèi qiēhuàn huí Húnánhuà, yǔsù biàn kuài, shēngdiào yě wánquán bù yīyàng. Xiǎo shíhou wǒ juéde zhè hěn yǒuqù, xiàng kàn yī gè rén biàn móshù. Zhǎng dà yǐhòu wǒ mànmàn lǐjiě le: yǔyán bù zhǐ shì jiāoliú de gōngjù, tā hái shì yī zhǒng qínggǎn de róngqì. Wàipó yòng pǔtōnghuà guò tā zài Shànghǎi de rìcháng shēnghuó——mǎi cài, gēn línjū liáotiān, kàn diànshì xīnwén. Dàn tā yòng Húnánhuà zhuāng zhe lìng yī gè zìjǐ——nàge shíbā suì líkāi jiāxiāng, zuò le sān tiān huǒchē lái dào Shànghǎi de nǚhái. Yǒu yī nián guònián, wàipó hē le diǎn jiǔ, tūrán yòng Húnánhuà chàng le yī shǒu gē, shuí yě tīng bù dǒng gēcí, dàn nàge xuánlǜ ràng tā kū le. Hòulái tā cā le cā yǎnlèi shuō: 「Zhè shì wǒ mā jiào wǒ de, wǒ pà nǎ tiān wàng le.」Nà yī kè wǒ lǐjiě le yī jiàn shì: yǒuxiē rén bìng bú shì zài liǎng zhǒng kǒuyīn zhī jiān qiēhuàn, ér shì zài liǎng gè zìjǐ zhī jiān láihuí zǒudòng. Ér liánjiē zhè liǎng gè zìjǐ de, búguò shì yī tiáo kàn bú jiàn de shēngyīn de xiàn.
text_en: My grandmother lived in Shanghai for over fifty years. Her Mandarin had become quite standard, with a slight Shanghai flavor—but unless you listened carefully, you wouldn't notice. Yet every time she received a call from relatives back in Hunan, her accent would switch back to Hunanese within seconds, her speech speeding up, the tones entirely different. As a child I found this amusing, like watching someone perform a magic trick. Growing up, I gradually understood: language is not just a tool for communication—it's also a vessel for emotion. Grandmother used Mandarin for her daily life in Shanghai—buying vegetables, chatting with neighbors, watching the news. But she kept another self inside Hunanese—the girl who left her hometown at eighteen and rode a train for three days to reach Shanghai. One Spring Festival, after drinking a bit of wine, she suddenly sang a song in Hunanese. Nobody could understand the lyrics, but the melody made her cry. Afterward she wiped her tears and said: 'My mother taught me this one.

title: The Office Fridge War
text_zh: 我们办公室的冰箱曾经引发过一场持续了三个月的「战争」。起因很简单：有人吃了别人的酸奶。被吃酸奶的同事小王贴了一张纸条在冰箱上：「请不要动我的酸奶，谢谢。」第二天，酸奶又少了一盒。小王又贴了一张：「我的酸奶上面有名字，请认清再拿。」第三天，有人在纸条下面回了一句：「你的酸奶太好喝了，下次请买两盒。」从此，冰箱门成了整个办公室的留言板。有人抱怨剩饭味道太大，有人要求把臭豆腐扔掉，有人提议大家AA制买水果。最精彩的是，行政部的张姐每周五下班前会把冰箱里超过三天的东西全部清理掉，她管这叫「冰箱审判日」。有一次她不小心扔了老板的咖啡豆，全办公室都替她捏了一把汗。结果老板什么都没说，只是第二天又买了一袋咖啡豆，上面用记号笔写了四个大字：「请勿扔掉」。
text_pinyin: Wǒmen bàngōngshì de bīngxiāng céngjīng yǐnfā guò yī chǎng chíxù le sān gè yuè de 「zhànzhēng」. Qǐyīn hěn jiǎndān: yǒu rén chī le biéren de suānnǎi. Bèi chī suānnǎi de tóngshì xiǎo Wáng tiē le yī zhāng zhǐtiáo zài bīngxiāng shàng: 「Qǐng búyào dòng wǒ de suānnǎi, xièxie.」Dì'èr tiān, suānnǎi yòu shǎo le yī hé. Xiǎo Wáng yòu tiē le yī zhāng: 「Wǒ de suānnǎi shàngmiàn yǒu míngzì, qǐng rèn qīng zài ná.」Dì sān tiān, yǒu rén zài zhǐtiáo xiàmiàn huí le yī jù: 「Nǐ de suānnǎi tài hǎo hē le, xià cì qǐng mǎi liǎng hé.」Cóngcǐ, bīngxiāng mén chéng le zhěnggè bàngōngshì de liúyánbǎn. Yǒu rén bàoyuàn shèngfàn wèidao tài dà, yǒu rén yāoqiú bǎ chòu dòufu rēng diào, yǒu rén tíyì dàjiā AA zhì mǎi shuǐguǒ. Zuì jīngcǎi de shì, xíngzhèng bù de Zhāng jiě měi zhōuwǔ xiàbān qián huì bǎ bīngxiāng lǐ chāoguò sān tiān de dōngxi quánbù qīnglǐ diào, tā guǎn zhè jiào 「bīngxiāng shěnpàn rì」. Yǒu yī cì tā bù xiǎoxīn rēng le lǎobǎn de kāfēidòu, quán bàngōngshì dōu tì tā niē le yī bǎ hàn. Jiéguǒ lǎobǎn shénme dōu méi shuō, zhǐshì dì'èr tiān yòu mǎi le yī dài kāfēidòu, shàngmiàn yòng jìhaobǐ xiě le sì gè dà zì: 「Qǐng wù rēng diào」. Cóng nà yǐhòu, bīngxiāng shàng de zhǐtiáo yuè lái yuè shǎo le——bú shì wèntí jiějué le, ér shì dàjiā dōu xuéhuì le zài fàng dōngxi zhīqián xiān xiě shàng zìjǐ de míngzì hé rìqī. Yǒu shíhou, yī chǎng hùnluàn zuìzhōng néng cuīshēng chū zhìxù, zhǐshì guòchéng bú tài tǐmiàn.
text_en: The fridge in our office once sparked a 'war' that lasted three months. The cause was simple: someone ate someone else's yogurt. Xiao Wang, the victim, stuck a note on the fridge: 'Please don't touch my yogurt. Thank you.' The next day, another carton was missing. Xiao Wang posted another: 'My yogurt has my name on it. Please check before taking.' On the third day, someone replied beneath the note: 'Your yogurt is really delicious. Next time please buy two.' From then on, the fridge door became the entire office's message board. People complained about leftover food smelling too strong, demanded that stinky tofu be thrown out, and proposed everyone chip in for fruit. The best part was Sister Zhang from the admin department, who every Friday before leaving would clear out everything in the fridge older than three days. She called this 'Fridge Judgment Day.' Once she accidentally threw out the boss's coffee beans, and the whole office broke into a cold sweat on her behalf. But the boss said nothing — just bought another bag the next day with four large characters written in marker: 'DO NOT THROW AWAY.'

title: The Missing Cup
text_zh: 我们办公室有一个谜团，持续了半年才解开。事情是这样的：茶水间的柜子里本来有十二个白色陶瓷杯子，是公司统一买的。但从某一天起，杯子开始一个一个消失。一周少一个，非常有规律。到第八周的时候，只剩下四个了。大家开始互相猜测：是有人拿回家了？还是被打碎了但没人承认？行政部的小刘甚至在柜子旁边贴了一张告示：「请归还公共杯子。」没有任何效果。后来我无意中发现了答案。有一天加班很晚，我路过保洁阿姨的休息室，看见架子上整整齐齐地放着八个白色陶瓷杯子，每个杯子上都贴着一小条胶带，写着不同的名字——都是保洁阿姨们自己的名字。原来她们一直用纸杯喝水，觉得浪费，就每人拿了一个陶瓷杯。她们不知道这些杯子是公司为员工买的，以为是没人要的。我站在那里犹豫了很久，最后什么都没说，回去跟小刘说：「别找了，那些杯子在该在的地方。」
text_pinyin: Wǒmen bàngōngshì yǒu yī gè mítuán, chíxù le bànnián cái jiě kāi. Shìqíng shì zhèyàng de: cháshuǐjiān de guìzi lǐ běnlái yǒu shí'èr gè báisè táocí bēizi, shì gōngsī tǒngyī mǎi de. Dàn cóng mǒu yī tiān qǐ, bēizi kāishǐ yī gè yī gè xiāoshī. Yī zhōu shǎo yī gè, fēicháng yǒu guīlǜ. Dào dì bā zhōu de shíhou, zhǐ shèng xià sì gè le. Dàjiā kāishǐ hùxiāng cāicè: shì yǒu rén ná huí jiā le? Háishi bèi dǎsuì le dàn méi rén chéngrèn? Xíngzhèng bù de xiǎo Liú shènzhì zài guìzi pángbiān tiē le yī zhāng gàoshi: 「Qǐng guīhuán gōnggòng bēizi.」Méiyǒu rènhé xiàoguǒ. Hòulái wǒ wúyìzhōng fāxiàn le dá'àn. Yǒu yī tiān jiābān hěn wǎn, wǒ lùguò bǎojié āyí de xiūxīshì, kànjiàn jiàzi shàng zhěngzhěng qíqí de fàng zhe bā gè báisè táocí bēizi, měi gè bēizi shàng dōu tiē zhe yī xiǎo tiáo jiāodài, xiě zhe bùtóng de míngzì——dōu shì bǎojié āyímen zìjǐ de míngzì. Yuánlái tāmen yīzhí yòng zhǐbēi hē shuǐ, juéde làngfèi, jiù měi rén ná le yī gè táocí bēi. Tāmen bù zhīdào zhèxiē bēizi shì gōngsī wèi yuángōng mǎi de, yǐwéi shì méi rén yào de. Wǒ zhàn zài nàlǐ yóuyù le hěn jiǔ, zuìhòu shénme dōu méi shuō, huíqù gēn xiǎo Liú shuō: 「Bié zhǎo le, nàxiē bēizi zài gāi zài de dìfang.」
text_en: Our office had a mystery that took half a year to solve. Here's what happened: the cabinet in the tea room originally had twelve white ceramic cups, purchased by the company. But starting from a certain day, the cups began disappearing one by one. One per week, very regularly. By the eighth week, only four remained. Everyone speculated: had someone taken them home? Or were they broken but nobody confessed? Xiao Liu from admin even posted a notice by the cabinet: 'Please return the communal cups.' It had no effect. Later I discovered the answer by accident. Working late one night, I passed by the cleaning ladies' break room and saw eight white ceramic cups arranged neatly on a shelf, each with a small strip of tape bearing a different name—the cleaning ladies' own names. It turned out they'd always been drinking water from paper cups and felt it was wasteful, so each took one ceramic cup. They didn't know the cups were bought for employees—they assumed nobody wanted them. I stood there hesitating for a long time. In the end I said nothing, went back, and told Xiao Liu: 'Stop looking. Those cups are where they should be.'

title: The Night Shift Security Guard's Notebook
text_zh: 我们写字楼的夜班保安姓孙，五十多岁，每天晚上六点到早上六点值班。大部分时间他就坐在大厅的前台后面，面前放着一个蓝色的笔记本。我一直以为那是值班记录，直到有一天深夜我加班出来，好奇地问了一句。老孙有点不好意思地把笔记本翻给我看。里面记的全是诗——不是什么名人的诗，是他自己写的。有的写夜里大厅的灯光，有的写凌晨三点的安静，有的写窗外的月亮。字迹不太好看，但每一首都认认真真地写了日期。他说他初中毕业就出来打工了，一直喜欢写东西但从来没有机会。当了夜班保安以后，终于有了大把的安静时间。「白天太吵了，什么都写不出来，」他说，「只有夜里，这个楼安静得像一条船，我就觉得心里的话都浮上来了。」我问他写了多少首了，他翻了翻说，大概三百多首。三百多首，全都写在同一个蓝色笔记本里，从来没有给任何人看过。我问他为什么不发到网上，他摇了摇头说：「写诗不是为了给别人看的，是为了陪自己度过那些安静的夜晚。」
text_pinyin: Wǒmen xiězìlóu de yèbān bǎo'ān xìng Sūn, wǔshí duō suì, měi tiān wǎnshang liù diǎn dào zǎoshang liù diǎn zhíbān. Dàbùfen shíjiān tā jiù zuò zài dàtīng de qiántái hòumiàn, miànqián fàng zhe yī gè lánsè de bǐjìběn. Wǒ yīzhí yǐwéi nà shì zhíbān jìlù, zhídào yǒu yī tiān shēnyè wǒ jiābān chūlái, hàoqí de wèn le yī jù. Lǎo Sūn yǒudiǎn bù hǎoyìsi de bǎ bǐjìběn fān gěi wǒ kàn. Lǐmiàn jì de quán shì shī——bú shì shénme míngrén de shī, shì tā zìjǐ xiě de. Yǒu de xiě yèlǐ dàtīng de dēngguāng, yǒu de xiě língchén sān diǎn de ānjìng, yǒu de xiě chuāng wài de yuèliang. Zìjì bú tài hǎokàn, dàn měi yī shǒu dōu rènrènzhēnzhēn de xiě le rìqī. Tā shuō tā chūzhōng bìyè jiù chūlái dǎgōng le, yīzhí xǐhuan xiě dōngxi dàn cónglái méiyǒu jīhuì. Dāng le yèbān bǎo'ān yǐhòu, zhōngyú yǒu le dà bǎ de ānjìng shíjiān. 「Báitiān tài chǎo le, shénme dōu xiě bù chūlái,」tā shuō, 「zhǐyǒu yèlǐ, zhège lóu ānjìng de xiàng yī tiáo chuán, wǒ jiù juéde xīnlǐ de huà dōu fú shàng lái le.」Wǒ wèn tā xiě le duōshao shǒu le, tā fān le fān shuō, dàgài sānbǎi duō shǒu. Sānbǎi duō shǒu, quándōu xiě zài tóngyī gè lánsè bǐjìběn lǐ, cónglái méiyǒu gěi rènhé rén kàn guò. Wǒ wèn tā wèishéme bù fā dào wǎng shàng, tā yáo le yáo tóu shuō: 「Xiě shī bú shì wèile gěi biéren kàn de, shì wèile péi zìjǐ dùguò nàxiē ānjìng de yèwǎn.」
text_en: The night-shift security guard at our office building is surnamed Sun, in his fifties, on duty from 6 PM to 6 AM every day. Most of the time he just sits behind the front desk in the lobby, a blue notebook in front of him. I always assumed it was the duty log, until one late night when I came out after working overtime and asked out of curiosity. Old Sun, a bit embarrassed, flipped the notebook open for me. It was full of poetry—not famous poets' work, but his own. Some described the lobby lights at night, some the silence at 3 AM, some the moon outside the window. The handwriting wasn't pretty, but every poem had a date carefully noted. He said he'd gone out to work after finishing middle school and had always liked writing but never had the chance. After becoming a night security guard, he finally had vast stretches of quiet time. 'Daytime is too noisy—I can't write anything,' he said. 'Only at night, when this building is as quiet as a ship, do I feel the words inside me float to the surface.' I asked how many poems he'd written. He flipped through and said roughly three hundred. Three hundred poems, all in the same blue notebook, never shown to anyone. I asked why he didn't post them online. He shook his head and said: 'Writing poetry isn't for others to read. It's for keeping myself company through those quiet nights.'

questions.q_zh: 作者一开始以为老孙的蓝色笔记本是什么？

title: The Name I Don't Use Anymore
text_zh: 我有两个名字。一个是身份证上的，是爷爷起的，四个字，带着明显的客家味道。另一个是上小学时老师给改的，两个字，简单好记，同学们都叫这个。后来工作、社交、所有的证件上用的都是身份证上的名字，但心里总觉得那两个字才是「真正的我」。有一次回老家过年，一个多年没见的邻居叫了我一声小时候的名字，我整个人愣了一下，然后不自觉地笑了。那一刻我发现，名字不只是一个符号——它像一把钥匙，能打开特定时期的记忆。身份证上的名字打开的是成年后的世界：工作、合同、银行账户。而那两个字打开的是另一个世界：下课后在田埂上跑、夏天在河里游泳、爷爷坐在门口抽旱烟。两个名字，两段人生，装在同一个人的身体里。现在会叫我那个名字的人越来越少了，老邻居们搬的搬、老的老。有时候我会想，如果有一天再也没有人叫那个名字了，那段记忆是不是也会跟着消失？还是说，它只是安静地睡着了，等待某一天某个声音把它唤醒？
text_pinyin: Wǒ yǒu liǎng gè míngzi. Yī gè shì shēnfènzhèng shàng de, shì yéye qǐ de, sì gè zì, dài zhe míngxiǎn de Kèjiā wèidao. Lìng yī gè shì shàng xiǎoxué shí lǎoshī gěi gǎi de, liǎng gè zì, jiǎndān hǎo jì, tóngxuémen dōu jiào zhège. Hòulái gōngzuò, shèjiāo, suǒyǒu de zhèngjiàn shàng yòng de dōu shì shēnfènzhèng shàng de míngzi, dàn xīnlǐ zǒng juéde nà liǎng gè zì cái shì 「zhēnzhèng de wǒ」. Yǒu yī cì huí lǎojiā guònián, yī gè duō nián méi jiàn de línjū jiào le wǒ yī shēng xiǎo shíhou de míngzi, wǒ zhěnggè rén lèng le yīxià, ránhòu bù zìjué de xiào le. Nà yī kè wǒ fāxiàn, míngzi bù zhǐ shì yī gè fúhào——tā xiàng yī bǎ yàoshi, néng dǎkāi tèdìng shíqī de jìyì. Shēnfènzhèng shàng de míngzi dǎkāi de shì chéngnián hòu de shìjiè: gōngzuò, hétong, yínháng zhànghù. Ér nà liǎng gè zì dǎkāi de shì lìng yī gè shìjiè: xiàkè hòu zài tiángěng shàng pǎo, xiàtiān zài hé lǐ yóuyǒng, yéye zuò zài ménkǒu chōu hànyān. Liǎng gè míngzi, liǎng duàn rénshēng, zhuāng zài tóngyī gè rén de shēntǐ lǐ. Xiànzài huì jiào wǒ nàge míngzi de rén yuè lái yuè shǎo le, lǎo línjūmen bān de bān, lǎo de lǎo. Yǒu shíhou wǒ huì xiǎng, rúguǒ yǒu yī tiān zài yě méiyǒu rén jiào nàge míngzi le, nà duàn jìyì shì bú shì yě huì gēn zhe xiāoshī? Háishi shuō, tā zhǐshì ānjìng de shuì zháo le, děngdài mǒu yī tiān mǒu gè shēngyīn bǎ tā huànxǐng?
text_en: I have two names. One is on my ID card, given by my grandfather—four characters, with a distinctly Hakka flavor. The other was changed by my elementary school teacher—two characters, simple and easy to remember. All my classmates called me by that one. Later, for work, socializing, and all official documents I used the name on my ID, but in my heart I always felt those two characters were the 'real me.' Once when I went back to my hometown for Spring Festival, a neighbor I hadn't seen in years called out my childhood name. My whole body froze for a moment, then I smiled involuntarily. Then I discovered that a name isn't just a symbol—it's like a key that can unlock memories of a specific era. The name on my ID unlocks the adult world: work, contracts, bank accounts. Those two characters unlock another world: running along the rice paddy ridges after school, swimming in the river in summer, Grandpa sitting by the door smoking his pipe. Two names, two lifetimes, housed in the same body. Now fewer and fewer people call me by that name—the old neighbors have moved away or aged. Sometimes I wonder: if one day nobody ever calls that name again, will those memories disappear along with it? Or are they just quietly sleeping, waiting for some voice someday to wake them?

title: The Elevator Etiquette Incident
text_zh: 我们这栋楼只有两部电梯，早上八点是最挤的时候。电梯门一开，所有人都往里冲。有一天早上，住在十八楼的赵叔按住开门键让一位抱着孩子的年轻妈妈先进去，后面的人小声嘀咕了几句。赵叔没说什么，但第二天他在电梯里贴了一张手写的告示：「早高峰电梯使用建议：一、抱小孩的优先。二、拿很多东西的优先。三、赶飞机的优先（请提前说明）。四、以上都不符合的，请微笑排队。」一周以后，告示上多了两条。第五条：「带狗的请走楼梯，因为狗会按所有的楼层按钮。」第六条：「如果你昨晚吃了大蒜，请等下一趟。」
text_pinyin: Wǒmen zhè dòng lóu zhǐ yǒu liǎng bù diàntī, zǎoshang bā diǎn shì zuì jǐ de shíhou. Dàjiā dōu zài gǎn shíjiān shàngbān, diàntī mén yī kāi, suǒyǒu rén dōu wǎng lǐ chōng. Yǒu yī tiān zǎoshang, zhù zài shíbā lóu de Zhào shū àn zhù kāimén jiàn ràng yī wèi bào zhe háizi de niánqīng māma xiān jìnqù, jiéguǒ hòumiàn de rén xián tā dānwù shíjiān, xiǎo shēng dīgu le jǐ jù. Zhào shū méi shuō shénme, dàn dì'èr tiān tā zài diàntī lǐ tiē le yī zhāng shǒuxiě de gàoshi: 「Zǎo gāofēng diàntī shǐyòng jiànyì: yī, bào xiǎohái de yōuxiān. Èr, ná hěn duō dōngxi de yōuxiān. Sān, gǎn fēijī de yōuxiān (qǐng tíqián shuōmíng). Sì, yǐshàng dōu bù fúhé de, qǐng wēixiào páiduì.」Gàoshi tiē chūqù yǐhòu, xiàoguǒ chū rén yìliào. Bú shì shuō dàjiā zhēn de ànzhào guīzé lái le, ér shì měi tiān zǎoshang děng diàntī de shíhou, dàjiā huì zhàn zài nàlǐ dú yī biàn, ránhòu hùxiāng xiào yīxià. Yǒu rén bǔchōng le dì wǔ tiáo: 「Dài gǒu de qǐng zǒu lóutī, yīnwèi gǒu huì àn suǒyǒu de lóucéng ànniǔ.」Yòu yǒu rén jiā le dì liù tiáo: 「Rúguǒ nǐ zuówǎn chī le dàsuàn, qǐng děng xià yī tàng.」Gàoshi yuè lái yuè cháng, yǔqì yuè lái yuè huāngdàn, dàn diàntī lǐ de qìfēn què yuè lái yuè hǎo le. Zhào shū hòulái gēn wǒ shuō: 「Wǒ běnlái xiǎng jiào dàjiā lǐyí, jiéguǒ biànchéng le yī gè xiàohua jí. Dàn nǐ xiǎng xiǎng, néng ràng mòshēngrén yīqǐ xiào de dōngxi, běnshēn bú jiùshì zuì hǎo de lǐyí ma?」
text_en: Our building has only two elevators, and 8 AM is the most crowded time. The moment the doors open, people charge in. One morning, Uncle Zhao from the 18th floor held the door-open button to let a young mother carrying a child go first. People behind him grumbled. Uncle Zhao said nothing, but the next day he posted a handwritten notice in the elevator: 'Morning rush elevator usage suggestions: 1. Those carrying small children go first. 2. Those carrying many things go first. 3. Those catching a flight go first (please state in advance). 4. If none of the above apply, please smile and queue.' A week later, two more rules had appeared on the notice. Number five: 'Those with dogs, please take the stairs, because dogs press every floor button.' Number six: 'If you ate garlic last night, please wait for the next one.'

title: The Post Office Queue
text_zh: 我特意选在工作日下午去邮局寄一个包裹，因为我知道那个时间段人最少。然而我错了。邮局里排着一条安静而坚定的队伍，清一色是老年人。他们不是来寄包裹或取快递的，而是来领退休金的——尽管银行转账早已普及，但这一代人中有很多仍然信任纸质的存折胜过屏幕上的数字。队伍移动得极慢，因为每个人到了窗口都要跟工作人员确认好几遍金额，然后小心翼翼地把钱装进布口袋或者贴身的腰包里。没有人抱怨，也没有人催促。我排在他们后面，一开始心里有些焦躁，但渐渐被这种从容感染了。一位拄着拐杖的大爷主动跟我搭话，问我寄东西去哪里。我说寄回老家给父母。他点点头说：「好，好。我女儿也经常给我寄东西，不过我更喜欢她回来。」这句话朴素得让人无法反驳。邮局在数字时代显得过时而笨拙，但它维护着一种老派的信任关系——人到场，面对面，钱过手。对这些老人来说，效率从来不是最重要的事，确定感才是。排队本身就是一种仪式，证明有些东西是值得等待的。
text_pinyin: Wo teyi xuan zai gongzuori xiawu qu youju ji yi ge baoguo, yinwei wo zhidao nage shijian duan ren zui shao. Ran'er wo cuo le. Youju li pai zhe yi tiao anjing er jianding de duiwu, qingyise shi laonianren. Tamen bu shi lai ji baoguo huo qu kuaidi de, er shi lai ling tuixiujin de—jinguan yinhang zhuanzhang zao yi puji, dan zhe yi dai ren zhong you hen duo rengran xinren zhizhi de cunzhe sheng guo pingmu shang de shuzi. Duiwu yidong de ji man, yinwei mei ge ren dao le chuangkou dou yao gen gongzuo renyuan queren hao ji bian jin'e, ranhou xiaoxinyiyi de ba qian zhuang jin bu koudai huozhe tieshen de yaobao li. Meiyou ren baoyuan, ye meiyou ren cuicu. Wo pai zai tamen houmian, yi kaishi xin li youxie jiaozao, dan jianjian bei zhe zhong congrong ganran le. Yi wei zhu zhe guaizhang de daye zhudong gen wo da hua, wen wo ji dongxi qu nali. Wo shuo ji hui laojia gei fumu. Ta diandian tou shuo: 'Hao, hao. Wo nv'er ye jingchang gei wo ji dongxi, buguo wo geng xihuan ta huilai.' Zhe ju hua pusu de rang ren wufa fanbo. Youju zai shuzi shidai xian de guoshi er benzhuo, dan ta weihu zhe yi zhong laopai de xinren guanxi—ren daochang, mianduimian, qian guoshou. Dui zhexie laoren lai shuo, xiaolv conglai bu shi zui zhongyao de shi, queding gan cai shi. Paidui benshen jiu shi yi zhong yishi, zhengming youxie dongxi shi zhide dengdai de.
text_en: I deliberately chose a weekday afternoon to mail a package at the post office, knowing that time slot would have the fewest people. I was wrong. The post office had a quiet, resolute queue—entirely elderly people. They weren't there to mail packages or pick up deliveries but to collect pensions—even though bank transfers are long ubiquitous, many of this generation still trust paper bankbooks more than numbers on a screen. The queue moved extremely slowly, because each person at the window confirmed the amount several times with the clerk, then carefully tucked the money into a cloth bag or a body-hugging waist pouch. No one complained, no one rushed. I stood behind them, initially a bit restless, but gradually infected by their composure. An old man with a cane struck up conversation, asking where I was mailing something. I said back home to my parents. He nodded and said: 'Good, good. My daughter often sends me things too, but I prefer when she comes back.' That sentence was so plain it couldn't be argued with. The post office seems outdated and clumsy in the digital age, but it maintains an old-fashioned trust relationship—people present, face to face, money changing hands.

title: The Piano on the Seventh Floor
text_zh: 七楼每天晚上十点准时响起钢琴声，弹的总是同一首曲子——德彪西的「月光」。这在我们这栋隔音极差的老楼里不算稀奇，稀奇的是七楼那套房子已经空置了三年。房东在国外，委托中介挂牌出租但一直没租出去。我第一次听到的时候以为是邻居在放音响，但仔细辨认后确定那是现场演奏——有微妙的踏板声和偶尔的停顿，这些是录音里不会有的。我向物业反映过，他们派人检查了七楼，门锁完好，屋内积满灰尘，确实有一架老式立式钢琴，但琴盖上的灰尘没有被动过的痕迹。这件事在楼里传开以后，解释五花八门：有人说是声音通过管道传导产生了定位错觉，有人说是隔壁楼的琴声经过某种建筑学上的折射恰好集中在七楼的位置。我更倾向于一个朴素的解释——也许是楼上或楼下某个不愿被打扰的居民在弹琴，声音通过老旧建筑的结构以一种反直觉的方式传播。但我没有去逐户排查。每天晚上十点听一遍「月光」已经成了我入睡前的仪式，我不太愿意用一个平庸的答案替换掉这个小小的日常奇迹。
text_pinyin: Qi lou mei tian wanshang shi dian zhunshi xiangqi gangqin sheng, tan de zong shi tong yi shou quzi—Debiaoxi de 'Yueguang'. Zhe zai women zhe dong geyin ji cha de lao lou li bu suan xiqi, xiqi de shi qi lou na tao fangzi yijing kongzhi le san nian. Fangdong zai guowai, weituo zhongjie guapai chuzu dan yizhi mei zu chuqu. Wo di yi ci ting dao de shihou yiwei shi linju zai fang yinxiang, dan zixi bianren hou queding na shi xianchang yanzou—you weimiao de taban sheng he ou'er de tingdun, zhexie shi luyin li bu hui you de. Wo xiang wuye fanying guo, tamen pai ren jiancha le qi lou, mensuo wanhao, wu nei ji man huichen, queshi you yi jia laoshi lishi gangqin, dan qin gai shang de huichen meiyou bei dong guo de henji. Zhe jian shi zai lou li chuan kai yihou, jieshi wuhuabamen: you ren shuo shi shengyin tongguo guandao chuandao chansheng le dingwei cuojue, you ren shuo shi gebi lou de qin sheng jingguo mou zhong jianzhuxue shang de zheshe qiahao jizhong zai qi lou de weizhi. Wo geng qingxiang yu yi ge pusu de jieshi—yexu shi lou shang huo lou xia mou ge bu yuan bei darao de jumin zai tan qin, shengyin tongguo laojiu jianzhu de jiegou yi yi zhong fan zhijue de fangshi chuanbo. Dan wo meiyou qu zhuhu paicha. Mei tian wanshang shi dian ting yi bian 'Yueguang' yijing cheng le wo rushui qian de yishi, wo bu tai yuanyi yong yi ge pingyong de da'an tihuan diao zhege xiaoxiao de richang qiji.
text_en: At exactly ten every night, piano music sounds from the seventh floor—always the same piece, Debussy's 'Clair de Lune.' This wouldn't be unusual in our old building with terrible soundproofing, except that the seventh-floor apartment has been vacant for three years. The landlord lives abroad and listed it through an agent, but it's never been rented. The first time I heard it I thought a neighbor was playing speakers, but upon careful listening I confirmed it was live performance—there were subtle pedal sounds and occasional pauses that recordings wouldn't have. I reported it to property management; they sent someone to inspect the seventh floor. The lock was intact, the rooms thick with dust. There was indeed an old upright piano, but the dust on its lid showed no signs of disturbance. After the story spread through the building, explanations were wild and varied: some said sound conducted through pipes created a localization illusion; others said piano from the adjacent building was architecturally refracted to converge at the seventh floor. I lean toward a simpler explanation—perhaps a resident above or below who doesn't want to be disturbed is playing, and the sound propagates through the old building's structure in counterintuitive ways. But I haven't gone door to door investigating. Listening to 'Clair de Lune' at ten every night has become my pre-sleep ritual, and I'm not particularly willing to replace this small daily miracle with a mundane answer.

title: Grandmother's Pickled Vegetables
text_zh: 奶奶的泡菜坛子在厨房角落里蹲了至少四十年，坛身釉面早已斑驳，坛口用一圈水封着，终年不开。每年入秋，奶奶会打开坛盖，取出一些老泡菜，再放入新切好的萝卜、豇豆和辣椒。这个过程她从不让旁人插手，连我母亲都只能在一边看着。她说泡菜坛子是有「性情」的，换一个人动手，菌群就会变，味道就不对了。我小时候不以为然，觉得这是老年人的固执和迷信。直到有一年我在大学修了食品科学的选修课，才知道她说的竟然有道理——每一个老泡菜坛子里都培养着一个独特的微生物生态系统，酸度、盐度、温度的细微差异造就了不可复制的风味。奶奶去世以后，母亲接手了那个坛子，但怎么都泡不出奶奶的味道。她试过调整盐的比例，试过换不同品种的辣椒，甚至请教过同村的老人。最后她承认：也许不是配方的问题，而是奶奶的手——那双揉搓了一辈子蔬菜的手上携带的菌群，才是秘方里真正不可替代的成分。我们保存了一百种古老的食谱，却无法保存一双手上的微生物。传承有时候就是这么脆弱。
text_pinyin: Nainai de paocai tanzi zai chufang jiaoluo li dun le zhishao sishi nian, tan shen youmian zao yi banbo, tan kou yong yi quan shui feng zhe, zhongnian bu kai. Mei nian ru qiu, nainai hui dakai tan gai, quchu yixie lao paocai, zai fang ru xin qie hao de luobo, jiangdou he lajiao. Zhege guocheng ta cong bu rang pangren chashou, lian wo muqin dou zhi neng zai yibian kan zhe. Ta shuo paocai tanzi shi you 'xingqing' de, huan yi ge ren dongshou, junqun jiu hui bian, weidao jiu bu dui le. Wo xiaoshihou bu yiweiran, juede zhe shi laonianren de guzhi he mixin. Zhidao you yi nian wo zai daxue xiu le shipin kexue de xuanxiu ke, cai zhidao ta shuo de jingran you daoli—mei yi ge lao paocai tanzi li dou peiyang zhe yi ge dute de weishengwu shengtai xitong, suandu, yandu, wendu de xiwei chayi zaojiu le buke fuzhi de fengwei. Nainai qushi yihou, muqin jieshou le nage tanzi, dan zenme dou pao bu chu nainai de weidao. Ta shi guo tiaozheng yan de bili, shi guo huan butong pinzhong de lajiao, shenzhi qingjiao guo tong cun de laoren. Zuihou ta chengren: yexu bu shi peifang de wenti, er shi nainai de shou—na shuang roucuo le yibeizi shucai de shou shang xiedai de junqun, cai shi mifang li zhenzheng buke tidai de chengfen. Women baocun le yibai zhong gulao de shipu, que wufa baocun yi shuang shou shang de weishengwu. Chuancheng youshihou jiu shi zheme cuiruo.
text_en: Grandmother's pickle jar has squatted in the kitchen corner for at least forty years, its glazed surface long mottled, the rim sealed with a ring of water, never opened throughout the year. Every autumn, grandmother would open the lid, remove some old pickles, and add freshly cut radish, cowpeas, and chili. She never let anyone else touch this process; even my mother could only watch from the side. She said pickle jars have 'temperament'—if a different person handles it, the bacterial culture changes and the flavor goes wrong. As a child I was dismissive, thinking it was an old person's stubbornness and superstition. It wasn't until a food science elective in college that I learned she was actually right—every old pickle jar cultivates a unique microbial ecosystem, and subtle differences in acidity, salinity, and temperature create irreplicable flavors. After grandmother passed away, my mother took over the jar, but could never achieve grandmother's flavor. She tried adjusting salt ratios, tried different chili varieties, even consulted village elders. Finally she admitted: perhaps it wasn't the recipe, but grandmother's hands—the bacterial culture carried on those hands that had kneaded vegetables for a lifetime was the truly irreplaceable ingredient in the secret formula. We preserve a hundred ancient recipes, but cannot preserve the microorganisms on a pair of hands. Inheritance is sometimes that fragile.

title: The Convenience Store at Three AM
text_zh: 我住的地方附近有一家二十四小时便利店。白天它和别的便利店没什么区别，但凌晨三点的它，有一种完全不同的气质。灯光是一样的白，货架是一样的满，但整个店里只有收银台后面的店员和偶尔进来的一两个顾客。那个时间段来的人都有自己的故事：加班到深夜的程序员，买一盒泡面和一罐啤酒；出租车司机进来买一包烟，站在门口抽完再走；偶尔有喝醉的年轻人进来买水，靠着冰柜坐一会儿清醒一下。我有一段时间失眠很严重，经常凌晨两三点出门散步，最后总会走到这家便利店。进去买一瓶水，不急着走，就站在杂志架前面翻一翻。店员是个二十出头的女孩，从来不催我，也不多说什么，偶尔会说一句：「今天外面冷不冷？」就这一句话，在凌晨三点听起来特别暖。后来失眠好了，我就再也没在那个时间去过。但我一直记得那种感觉：一座巨大的城市在睡觉，只有一盏灯还醒着，等着那些无处可去的人。
text_pinyin: Wǒ zhù de dìfang fùjìn yǒu yī jiā èrshísì xiǎoshí biànlìdiàn. Báitiān tā hé bié de biànlìdiàn méi shénme qūbié, dàn língchén sān diǎn de tā, yǒu yī zhǒng wánquán bùtóng de qìzhì. Dēngguāng shì yīyàng de bái, huòjià shì yīyàng de mǎn, dàn zhěnggè diàn lǐ zhǐ yǒu shōuyíntái hòumiàn de diànyuán hé ǒu'ěr jìnlái de yī liǎng gè gùkè. Nàge shíjiānduàn lái de rén dōu yǒu zìjǐ de gùshi: jiābān dào shēnyè de chéngxùyuán, mǎi yī hé pàomiàn hé yī guàn píjiǔ; chūzūchē sījī jìnlái mǎi yī bāo yān, zhàn zài ménkǒu chōu wán zài zǒu; ǒu'ěr yǒu hē zuì de niánqīngrén jìnlái mǎi shuǐ, kào zhe bīngguì zuò yīhuìr qīngxǐng yīxià. Wǒ yǒu yī duàn shíjiān shīmián hěn yánzhòng, jīngcháng língchén liǎng sān diǎn chūmén sànbù, zuìhòu zǒng huì zǒu dào zhè jiā biànlìdiàn. Jìnqù mǎi yī píng shuǐ, bù jí zhe zǒu, jiù zhàn zài zázhì jià qiánmiàn fān yī fān. Diànyuán shì gè èrshí chūtóu de nǚhái, cónglái bù cuī wǒ, yě bù duō shuō shénme, ǒu'ěr huì shuō yī jù: 「Jīntiān wàimiàn lěng bù lěng?」Jiù zhè yī jù huà, zài língchén sān diǎn tīng qǐlái tèbié nuǎn. Hòulái shīmián hǎo le, wǒ jiù zài yě méi zài nàge shíjiān qù guò. Dàn wǒ yīzhí jìde nà zhǒng gǎnjué: yī zuò jùdà de chéngshì zài shuìjiào, zhǐ yǒu yī zhǎn dēng hái xǐng zhe, děng zhe nàxiē wú chù kě qù de rén.
text_en: Near where I live there's a 24-hour convenience store. During the day it's no different from any other, but at 3 AM it has an entirely different quality. The lights are the same white, the shelves the same full, but the entire store holds only the clerk behind the register and the occasional customer or two. Everyone who comes at that hour has their own story: the programmer working overtime until deep night, buying a cup of instant noodles and a can of beer; the taxi driver popping in for a pack of cigarettes, smoking one by the door before leaving; occasionally a drunk young person coming in for water, leaning against the freezer for a moment to sober up. For a while my insomnia was severe, and I often went out walking at 2 or 3 AM, always ending up at this convenience store. I'd go in, buy a bottle of water, not rush to leave, just stand by the magazine rack and browse. The clerk was a girl barely past twenty. She never hurried me, never said much—just occasionally: 'Is it cold outside today?' That single sentence, heard at 3 AM, felt extraordinarily warm. Later my insomnia got better, and I never went at that hour again. But I've always remembered that feeling: an enormous city sleeping, with just one light still awake, waiting for those who have nowhere else to go.

questions.explanation: The final image—'one light still awake, waiting'—frames the store as the city's last refuge for people with nowhere else to be.

title: The Old Man Who Feeds Stray Cats
text_zh: 每天傍晚六点半，小区花园的角落会出现一位穿灰色夹克的老人，手里提着一个塑料袋，里面装着猫粮。他把猫粮倒在花坛边的几个纸碗里，然后坐在旁边的石头上等。不一会儿，三四只流浪猫就会从各个方向慢慢走过来。它们不急着吃，先远远地看一看，确认是那个老人以后才靠近。老人从来不伸手摸它们，也不叫它们。他就那样安静地坐着，看它们吃完，然后收拾好纸碗，起身离开。我观察了他大半年，几乎每天都是这样。有一次下大雨，我以为他不会来了，结果他打着伞还是来了。那天只来了一只猫，淋得湿透的。老人把伞倾斜过去遮住猫，自己的半边身子露在雨里。后来我终于忍不住跟他搭话。他说他老伴两年前走了，儿女都在外地。「猫不会问你为什么难过，」他说，「它们只是来吃东西。但它们每天都来，这就够了。」第二天傍晚六点半，我往阳台下看了一眼。他已经蹲在那里了，猫也到了。
text_pinyin: Měi tiān bàngwǎn liù diǎn bàn, xiǎoqū huāyuán de jiǎoluò huì chūxiàn yī wèi chuān huīsè jiákè de lǎorén, shǒu lǐ tí zhe yī gè sùliào dài, lǐmiàn zhuāng zhe māoliáng. Tā bǎ māoliáng dào zài huātán biān de jǐ gè zhǐwǎn lǐ, ránhòu zuò zài pángbiān de shítou shàng děng. Bù yīhuìr, sān sì zhī liúlàng māo jiù huì cóng gè gè fāngxiàng mànmàn zǒu guòlái. Tāmen bù jí zhe chī, xiān yuǎnyuǎn de kàn yī kàn, quèrèn shì nàge lǎorén yǐhòu cái kàojìn. Lǎorén cónglái bù shēnshǒu mō tāmen, yě bú jiào tāmen. Tā jiù nàyàng ānjìng de zuò zhe, kàn tāmen chī wán, ránhòu shōushi hǎo zhǐwǎn, qǐshēn líkāi. Wǒ guānchá le tā dà bàn nián, jīhū měi tiān dōu shì zhèyàng. Yǒu yī cì xià dà yǔ, wǒ yǐwéi tā bú huì lái le, jiéguǒ tā dǎ zhe sǎn háishi lái le. Nà tiān zhǐ lái le yī zhī māo, lín de shī tòu de. Lǎorén bǎ sǎn qīngxié guòqù zhē zhù māo, zìjǐ de bàn biān shēnzi lù zài yǔ lǐ. Hòulái wǒ zhōngyú rěn bú zhù gēn tā dā huà. Tā shuō tā lǎobàn liǎng nián qián zǒu le, érnǚ dōu zài wàidì. 「Māo bú huì wèn nǐ wèishéme nánguò,」tā shuō, 「tāmen zhǐshì lái chī dōngxi. Dàn tāmen měi tiān dōu lái, zhè jiù gòu le.」Wǒ hūrán yìshi dào, yěxǔ bú shì tā zài wèi māo, ér shì māo zài péi tā——měi tiān bàngwǎn liù diǎn bàn, zhǔnshí fù yī gè wúshēng de yuēdìng.
text_en: Every evening at 6:30, an old man in a grey jacket appears in the corner of the compound's garden, carrying a plastic bag of cat food. He pours the food into a few paper bowls by the flower bed, then sits on a nearby rock and waits. Before long, three or four stray cats approach slowly from different directions. They're in no rush to eat—first they look from afar, and only after confirming it's that old man do they draw close. The old man never reaches out to pet them, never calls them. He just sits there quietly, watches them finish, then tidies up the bowls and leaves. I observed him for over half a year; it was like this almost every day. Once during a heavy downpour, I assumed he wouldn't come, but he showed up with an umbrella. That day only one cat came, soaked through. The old man tilted the umbrella over the cat, leaving half his own body exposed to the rain. Eventually I couldn't resist striking up a conversation. He said his wife had passed two years ago; his children were all in other cities. 'Cats won't ask you why you're sad,' he said. 'They just come to eat. But they come every day, and that's enough.' maybe he wasn't feeding the cats—the cats were keeping him company. Every evening at 6:30, faithfully keeping a silent appointment.

options.pinyin: jiào jǐ shēng biǎoshì gǎnxiè

title: The Breakfast Cart Geography
text_zh: 我住的那条路上有三个早餐摊，彼此之间隔了不到两百米，但各自的气场完全不同。最东边的是一对河南夫妻，卖胡辣汤和油条，客人主要是建筑工地的工人和附近仓库的搬运工。中间那个是一位本地阿姨，卖粥和包子，来吃的大多是带孩子上学的家长和去菜场买菜的老人。最西边的是一个年轻小伙，卖咖啡和三明治，顾客基本上是附近写字楼的白领。三个早餐摊，三种客群，三种食物，三种说话的方式。河南夫妻嗓门大，跟客人聊天从来不用敬语；本地阿姨温温柔柔的，会多给熟客的孩子加一个小笼包；年轻小伙话不多，但咖啡做得很认真，拉花都不含糊。我有时候觉得，这三个早餐摊就是一部微型的社会学教科书。谁在哪里吃早餐，不仅说明了他吃什么，还说明了他是谁、他从哪里来、他要去哪里。一条两百米的路，浓缩了一座城市里至少三个阶层的清晨。而连接它们的，不过是同一条人行道和同一片早晨的阳光。
text_pinyin: Wǒ zhù de nà tiáo lù shàng yǒu sān gè zǎocān tān, bǐcǐ zhī jiān gé le búdào liǎng bǎi mǐ, dàn gèzì de qìchǎng wánquán bùtóng. Zuì dōngbiān de shì yī duì Hénán fūqī, mài húlàtāng hé yóutiáo, kèrén zhǔyào shì jiànzhù gōngdì de gōngrén hé fùjìn cāngkù de bānyùngōng. Zhōngjiān nàge shì yī wèi běndì āyí, mài zhōu hé bāozi, lái chī de dàduō shì dài háizi shàngxué de jiāzhǎng hé qù càichǎng mǎi cài de lǎorén. Zuì xībiān de shì yī gè niánqīng xiǎohuǒ, mài kāfēi hé sānmíngzhì, gùkè jīběn shàng shì fùjìn xiězìlóu de báilǐng. Sān gè zǎocān tān, sān zhǒng kèqún, sān zhǒng shíwù, sān zhǒng shuōhuà de fāngshì. Hénán fūqī sǎngmén dà, gēn kèrén liáotiān cónglái búyòng jìngyǔ; běndì āyí wēnwēn róuróu de, huì duō gěi shúkè de háizi jiā yī gè xiǎo lóngbāo; niánqīng xiǎohuǒ huà bù duō, dàn kāfēi zuò de hěn rènzhēn, lāhuā dōu bù hánhu. Wǒ yǒu shíhou juéde, zhè sān gè zǎocān tān jiùshì yī bù wēixíng de shèhuìxué jiàokēshū. Shuí zài nǎlǐ chī zǎocān, bù jǐn shuōmíng le tā chī shénme, hái shuōmíng le tā shì shuí, tā cóng nǎlǐ lái, tā yào qù nǎlǐ. Yī tiáo liǎng bǎi mǐ de lù, nóngsuō le yī zuò chéngshì lǐ zhìshǎo sān gè jiēcéng de qīngchén. Ér liánjiē tāmen de, búguò shì tóngyī tiáo rénxíngdào hé tóngyī piàn zǎochen de yángguāng.
text_en: On the street where I live there are three breakfast stalls, less than two hundred meters apart, yet each with an entirely different atmosphere. At the eastern end is a Henan couple selling hot-and-sour soup and fried dough sticks; their customers are mainly construction workers and warehouse laborers. In the middle is a local auntie selling porridge and steamed buns; most of her diners are parents taking children to school and elderly people heading to the market. At the western end is a young man selling coffee and sandwiches; his customers are almost all white-collar workers from nearby office buildings. Three breakfast stalls, three customer groups, three cuisines, three styles of conversation. The Henan couple are loud and never use polite language with customers. The local auntie is gentle and soft-spoken, slipping an extra soup dumpling to a regular's child. The young man says little, but makes his coffee with great care—even the latte art is meticulous. Sometimes I feel these three stalls are a miniature sociology textbook. Where someone eats breakfast doesn't just tell you what they eat—it tells you who they are, where they come from, and where they're going. A two-hundred-meter stretch of road condenses the mornings of at least three social strata of a city. And what connects them is nothing more than the same sidewalk and the same morning sunlight.

title: The Rain on the Window
text_zh: 下雨天的时候，我喜欢坐在窗边看雨水沿着玻璃往下流。每一颗雨滴落在玻璃上，都会停顿一下，像是在犹豫往哪个方向走。然后，它会选择一条路线往下滑，中途可能会遇到另一颗雨滴，两颗合成一颗更大的，速度突然加快。有时候两颗雨滴的路线明明很近，却始终没有碰到一起，各自沿着自己的轨迹滑到窗框边消失了。我小时候经常玩一个游戏：选两颗雨滴，打赌哪一颗先滑到底。后来长大了，看雨滴的时候想的东西不一样了。我开始觉得雨滴在玻璃上的路径和人的一生很像——你以为你在选择方向，其实是玻璃上看不见的纹路和灰尘在替你做决定。两个人的轨迹靠得再近，也不一定会相遇；而那些看起来毫不相干的雨滴，反而可能在某个拐弯处汇合。这样的想法当然有点矫情。但下雨天本来就是适合矫情的天气，不是吗？所以我允许自己在窗边坐一个下午，看那些小小的水滴，完成它们短暂而认真的旅程。
text_pinyin: Xiàyǔ tiān de shíhou, wǒ xǐhuan zuò zài chuāng biān kàn yǔshuǐ yánzhe bōli wǎng xià liú. Měi yī kē yǔdī luò zài bōli shàng, dōu huì tíngdùn yīxià, xiàng shì zài yóuyù wǎng nǎge fāngxiàng zǒu. Ránhòu, tā huì xuǎnzé yī tiáo lùxiàn wǎng xià huá, zhōngtú kěnéng huì yùdào lìng yī kē yǔdī, liǎng kē héchéng yī kē gèng dà de, sùdù tūrán jiākuài. Yǒu shíhou liǎng kē yǔdī de lùxiàn míngmíng hěn jìn, què shǐzhōng méiyǒu pèng dào yīqǐ, gèzì yánzhe zìjǐ de guǐjì huá dào chuāngkuàng biān xiāoshī le. Wǒ xiǎo shíhou jīngcháng wán yī gè yóuxì: xuǎn liǎng kē yǔdī, dǎdǔ nǎ yī kē xiān huá dào dǐ. Hòulái zhǎng dà le, kàn yǔdī de shíhou xiǎng de dōngxi bù yīyàng le. Wǒ kāishǐ juéde yǔdī zài bōli shàng de lùjìng hé rén de yīshēng hěn xiàng——nǐ yǐwéi nǐ zài xuǎnzé fāngxiàng, qíshí shì bōli shàng kàn bú jiàn de wénlù hé huīchén zài tì nǐ zuò juédìng. Liǎng gè rén de guǐjì kào de zài jìn, yě bù yīdìng huì xiāngyù; ér nàxiē kàn qǐlái háo bù xiāngān de yǔdī, fǎn'ér kěnéng zài mǒu gè guǎiwān chù huìhé. Zhèyàng de xiǎngfǎ dāngrán yǒudiǎn jiǎoqíng. Dàn xiàyǔ tiān běnlái jiùshì shìhé jiǎoqíng de tiānqì, bú shì ma? Suǒyǐ wǒ yǔnxǔ zìjǐ zài chuāng biān zuò yī gè xiàwǔ, kàn nàxiē xiǎo xiǎo de shuǐdī, wánchéng tāmen duǎnzàn ér rènzhēn de lǚchéng.
text_en: On rainy days I like to sit by the window and watch raindrops flow down the glass. Each drop that lands on the glass pauses for a moment, as if hesitating about which direction to go. Then it chooses a path and slides down, perhaps meeting another drop midway—two merging into one larger drop, suddenly accelerating. Sometimes two drops take paths that are very close yet never touch, each sliding along its own trajectory to the window frame, where it disappears. As a child I often played a game: pick two raindrops and bet which one reaches the bottom first. Growing up, I began thinking differently when watching raindrops. I started feeling that a raindrop's path on glass is much like a person's life—you think you're choosing your direction, but actually it's the invisible texture and dust on the glass making the decisions for you. Two people's trajectories can be as close as possible yet never meet; while drops that seem completely unrelated might converge at some unexpected turn. Thoughts like this are admittedly a bit sentimental. But rainy days were made for sentimentality, weren't they? So I allow myself to sit by the window for an afternoon, watching those tiny droplets complete their brief but earnest journeys.

questions.q_zh: 作者把雨滴的路径和人生做了什么类比？
questions.q_en: What analogy does the author draw between raindrop paths and life?

options.pinyin: rénshēng xiàng yǔdī yīyàng duǎnzàn
options.text_en: Life is as brief as a raindrop

options.text: 你以为在选择方向，但看不见的因素在替你做决定；相遇与否也不完全取决于距离
options.pinyin: nǐ yǐwéi zài xuǎnzé fāngxiàng, dàn kàn bú jiàn de yīnsù zài tì nǐ zuò juédìng; xiāngyù yǔ fǒu yě bù wánquán qǔjué yú jùlí

options.text: 人生应该像雨滴一样自由
