title: The Dialect I Can Understand but Not Speak
title_zh: 听得懂但说不出的方言
text_zh: 我爸是四川人，我妈是北京人，我在北京长大。从小到大，爸爸跟妈妈说普通话，但跟爷爷奶奶打电话的时候说四川话。我听了二十多年，四川话基本上能听懂，但说不出来。有一次回老家过年，亲戚们都说四川话，我全程都能听懂，但回答的时候只能用普通话。表哥笑我说：「你这个假四川人，耳朵是四川的，嘴巴是北京的。」我当时笑了，但心里有一种奇怪的感觉。后来我想明白了：听得懂一种语言但说不出来，其实是一种特殊的位置——你站在那个文化的边界上，看得见里面的人在做什么，听得见他们在说什么，但你进不去。你永远是一个有通行证的旁观者。这种感觉不完全是失落。它也给了我一种独特的视角：因为进不去，反而能看到里面的人看不到的东西。就像翻译永远比原文多出一层理解——那层理解来自距离。
text_pinyin: Wǒ bà shì Sìchuānrén, wǒ mā shì Běijīngrén, wǒ zài Běijīng zhǎng dà. Cóng xiǎo dào dà, bàba gēn māma shuō pǔtōnghuà, dàn gēn yéye nǎinai dǎ diànhuà de shíhou shuō Sìchuānhuà. Wǒ tīng le èrshí duō nián, Sìchuānhuà jīběn shàng néng tīng dǒng, dàn shuō bù chūlái. Yǒu yī cì huí lǎojiā guònián, qīnqimen dōu shuō Sìchuānhuà, wǒ quánchéng dōu néng tīng dǒng, dàn huídá de shíhou zhǐ néng yòng pǔtōnghuà. Biǎogē xiào wǒ shuō: 「Nǐ zhège jiǎ Sìchuānrén, ěrduo shì Sìchuān de, zuǐba shì Běijīng de.」Wǒ dāngshí xiào le, dàn xīnlǐ yǒu yī zhǒng qíguài de gǎnjué. Hòulái wǒ xiǎng míngbai le: tīng de dǒng yī zhǒng yǔyán dàn shuō bù chūlái, qíshí shì yī zhǒng tèshū de wèizhì——nǐ zhàn zài nàge wénhuà de biānjiè shàng, kàn de jiàn lǐmiàn de rén zài zuò shénme, tīng de jiàn tāmen zài shuō shénme, dàn nǐ jìn bú qù. Nǐ yǒngyuǎn shì yī gè yǒu tōngxíngzhèng de pángguānzhě. Zhè zhǒng gǎnjué bù wánquán shì shīluò. Tā yě gěi le wǒ yī zhǒng dútè de shìjiǎo: yīnwèi jìn bú qù, fǎn'ér néng kàn dào lǐmiàn de rén kàn bú dào de dōngxi. Jiù xiàng fānyì yǒngyuǎn bǐ yuánwén duō chū yī céng lǐjiě——nà céng lǐjiě lái zì jùlí.
text_en: My father is from Sichuan, my mother from Beijing. I grew up in Beijing. All my life, Dad spoke Mandarin with Mom, but switched to Sichuanese when calling Grandpa and Grandma. After listening for over twenty years, I can understand Sichuanese but can't speak it. Once when I went back to the hometown for Spring Festival, all the relatives spoke Sichuanese. I understood everything throughout, but could only answer in Mandarin. My cousin laughed: 'You fake Sichuanese person—your ears are from Sichuan, but your mouth is from Beijing.' I laughed at the time, but felt something strange inside. Later I figured it out: being able to understand a language but not speak it is actually a special position—you stand at the border of that culture, able to see what the people inside are doing, hear what they're saying, but you can't enter. You're forever an onlooker with a pass. This feeling isn't entirely one of loss. It also gave me a unique perspective: because I can't enter, I can see things that the people inside cannot. Just as translation always carries one more layer of understanding than the original—that layer comes from distance.

title: The Weight of Slippers
text_zh: 父亲住院以后，我回家整理他的东西，在门口鞋架上看到他那双棕色皮拖鞋，鞋底已经被磨出了两个浅浅的凹痕——恰好是脚掌和脚跟的位置。我拿起来掂了掂，很轻，但那两个凹痕让它有了某种超出物理重量的分量。母亲说这双拖鞋父亲穿了至少八年，穿坏过一次鞋带，他自己用鱼线缝上了，缝得歪歪扭扭但异常牢固。我低头细看，果然在左脚的鞋带处发现了那段透明的缝线，针脚粗犷得像在缝帆布袋。父亲不是一个惜物的人，衣服随便穿，杯子随便用，唯独这双拖鞋例外。我猜测原因可能很简单：它已经被穿成了脚的形状，换一双新的需要重新磨合，而父亲是那种讨厌一切磨合期的人。但母亲给了另一个解释：「你爸每天下班回来做的第一件事就是换上这双拖鞋。对他来说，穿上拖鞋意味着一天的工作结束了，他终于到家了。」一双拖鞋承载着八年的「到家」，两个凹痕是八年重力的累积。我把它放回了鞋架上，没有收起来。
text_pinyin: Fuqin zhuyuan yihou, wo hui jia zhengli ta de dongxi, zai menkou xiejia shang kan dao ta na shuang zongse pi tuoxie, xiedi yijing bei mo chu le liang ge qianqian de aohen—qiahao shi jiaozhang he jiaogen de weizhi. Wo na qilai dian le dian, hen qing, dan na liang ge aohen rang ta you le mou zhong chaochuwuli zhongliang de fenliang. Muqin shuo zhe shuang tuoxie fuqin chuan le zhishao ba nian, chuan huai guo yi ci xiedai, ta ziji yong yuxian feng shang le, feng de waiwainiuniu dan yichang laogu. Wo ditou xi kan, guoran zai zuojiao de xiedai chu faxian le na duan touming de fengxian, zhenjiao cuguang de xiang zai feng fanbu dai. Fuqin bu shi yi ge xiwu de ren, yifu suibian chuan, beizi suibian yong, weidu zhe shuang tuoxie liwai. Wo caice yuanyin keneng hen jiandan: ta yijing bei chuan cheng le jiao de xingzhuang, huan yi shuang xin de xuyao chongxin mohe, er fuqin shi na zhong taoyan yiqie moheqi de ren. Dan muqin gei le ling yi ge jieshi: 'Ni ba mei tian xiaban huilai zuo de di yi jian shi jiu shi huan shang zhe shuang tuoxie. Dui ta lai shuo, chuan shang tuoxie yiwei zhe yi tian de gongzuo jieshu le, ta zhongyu dao jia le.' Yi shuang tuoxie chengzai zhe ba nian de 'dao jia', liang ge aohen shi ba nian zhongli de leiji. Wo ba ta fang hui le xiejia shang, meiyou shou qilai. Yexu deng fuqin chuyuan huilai, ta xuyao zai di yi shijian zhao dao ta.
text_en: After my father was hospitalized, I went home to sort his things and saw his brown leather slippers on the shoe rack by the door, the soles worn into two shallow indentations—right where the ball and heel of the foot rest. I picked them up and weighed them in my hand—very light, but those two indentations gave them a weight beyond physics. My mother said he'd worn these slippers for at least eight years, broke one strap once and sewed it back himself with fishing line—crooked but remarkably sturdy. I looked closely and indeed found the transparent stitching at the left strap, the stitches rough as if sewing a canvas bag. My father isn't a sentimental man about possessions—wears any clothes, uses any cup—except for these slippers. I suspect the reason is simple: they've been worn into the shape of his feet, and switching to a new pair would require breaking in, and my father is the type who detests all break-in periods. But my mother offered another explanation: 'The first thing your dad does coming home from work every day is put on these slippers. For him, putting on slippers means the day's work is over—he's finally home.' A pair of slippers carrying eight years of 'being home,' two indentations the accumulation of eight years of gravity. I put them back on the shoe rack without packing them away.

title: The Art of Queueing
text_zh: 在上海，排队不只是排队。它是一场没有裁判的心理博弈。网红餐厅门口的队伍里至少有三种人：真的想吃的、拍完照就走的、以及根本不知道这家店卖什么但就是不想当那个先离开的人。第三种人是最危险的——他们会一直排到最后，点一份最贵的菜，拍一张照片发朋友圈，然后给差评。更精彩的是药店门口领赠品鸡蛋的队伍。退休阿姨们自发组建了一套情报系统：前面的人负责回报「还剩几盒」，中间的人负责传递信息，后面的人负责决策。如果鸡蛋快没了，队伍末端会立刻出现骚动——有人假装腿脚不好要求插队，有人突然「认出」前面的熟人，有人干脆从旁边的小巷绕到队伍前方假装刚到。我亲眼看到过两个阿姨为了一盒鸡蛋差点动手，最后被第三个阿姨劝住了。劝住的方式是：「别吵了，我刚看到街对面那家也在发，而且是双黄蛋。」两个人瞬间和解，一起小跑过马路。我认识的一位社会学研究者说过：「告诉我一个城市怎么排队，我就能告诉你这个城市的社会信任程度。」按这个标准，上海的社会信任程度大约等于：「我信任你不会插队，除非鸡蛋快没了。」
text_pinyin: Zài Shànghǎi, páiduì bù zhǐ shì páiduì. Tā shì yì chǎng méiyǒu cáipàn de xīnlǐ bóyì. Wǎnghóng cāntīng ménkǒu de duìwu lǐ zhìshǎo yǒu sān zhǒng rén: zhēn de xiǎng chī de, pāi wán zhào jiù zǒu de, yǐjí gēnběn bù zhīdào zhè jiā diàn mài shénme dàn jiùshì bù xiǎng dāng nàge xiān líkāi de rén. Dì sān zhǒng rén shì zuì wēixiǎn de — tāmen huì yìzhí pái dào zuìhòu, diǎn yí fèn zuì guì de cài, pāi yì zhāng zhàopiàn fā péngyouquān, ránhòu gěi chàpíng. Gèng jīngcǎi de shì yàodiàn ménkǒu lǐng zèngpǐn jīdàn de duìwu. Tuìxiū āyí men zìfā zǔjiàn le yí tào qíngbào xìtǒng: qiánmiàn de rén fùzé huíbào "hái shèng jǐ hé", zhōngjiān de rén fùzé chuándì xìnxī, hòumiàn de rén fùzé juécè. Rúguǒ jīdàn kuài méi le, duìwu mòduān huì lìkè chūxiàn sāodòng — yǒurén jiǎzhuāng tuǐjiǎo bù hǎo yāoqiú chāduì, yǒurén tūrán "rèn chū" qiánmiàn de shúrén, yǒurén gāncuì cóng pángbiān de xiǎo xiàng rào dào duìwu qiánfāng jiǎzhuāng gāng dào. Wǒ qīnyǎn kàn dào guò liǎng ge āyí wèile yì hé jīdàn chàdiǎn dòngshǒu, zuìhòu bèi dì sān ge āyí quàn zhù le. Quàn zhù de fāngshì shì: "Bié chǎo le, wǒ gāng kàn dào jiē duìmiàn nà jiā yě zài fā, érqiě shì shuānghuáng dàn." Liǎng ge rén shùnjiān héjiě, yìqǐ xiǎo pǎo guò mǎlù. Wǒ rènshi de yí wèi shèhuìxué yánjiūzhě shuō guò: "Gàosu wǒ yí ge chéngshì zěnme páiduì, wǒ jiù néng gàosu nǐ zhège chéngshì de shèhuì xìnrèn chéngdù." Àn zhège biāozhǔn, Shànghǎi de shèhuì xìnrèn chéngdù dàyuē děngyú: "Wǒ xìnrèn nǐ bú huì chāduì, chúfēi jīdàn kuài méi le."
text_en: In Shanghai, queueing isn't just queueing. It's an unrefereeed psychological contest. The line outside a trendy restaurant contains at least three species: those who actually want to eat, those who'll leave after taking a photo, and those who have no idea what the restaurant sells but refuse to be the first to leave. The third type is the most dangerous—they'll queue to the end, order the most expensive dish, photograph it for social media, then leave a one-star review. Even more spectacular are the lines outside pharmacies giving away promotional eggs. Retired aunties have spontaneously assembled an intelligence network: the front line reports 'how many boxes left,' the middle relays information, the rear makes decisions. When eggs run low, the tail end erupts—someone fakes a limp to request cutting in line, someone suddenly 'recognizes' an acquaintance near the front, someone simply loops through a side alley to the front pretending they just arrived. I personally witnessed two aunties nearly come to blows over a box of eggs, stopped only by a third auntie whose peacemaking method was: 'Stop fighting—the shop across the street is also giving them out, and those are double-yolk eggs.' The two instantly reconciled and jogged across the road together. An urban sociologist I know once said: 'Tell me how a city queues, and I can tell you its level of social trust.' By that standard, Shanghai's social trust level roughly equals: 'I trust you not to cut in line, unless the eggs are running out.'

questions.explanation: Shanghai's queueing isn't moral virtue but collective rationality: everyone independently calculates that orderly waiting optimizes their own cost-benefit.

title: The Night Gardener
text_zh: 住在我楼下的退休物理老师有一个秘密：他只在夜里照料花园。每天凌晨两点左右，他会打着一只微弱的手电筒走进小区公共花坛，蹲下来拔草、松土、修剪枝叶。我是偶然从失眠的窗口发现这件事的。第一次看到的时候还以为是小偷，仔细辨认后才认出他的身影。我后来鼓起勇气问他为什么选择深夜劳作，他的回答出乎意料：「植物在夜里其实更活跃——它们白天光合作用，夜里才真正生长。我老了，觉少，刚好陪它们。而且夜里没人看见，我可以跟它们说话，白天这么做的话邻居们会以为我疯了。」他说他确实会跟每一株植物说话，告诉它们水够不够、根扎得稳不稳。我问他植物能听懂吗，他笑了笑说：「听不懂。但说话的过程让我观察得更仔细。嘴巴在描述的时候，眼睛会注意到平时忽略的细节。」这大概是我听过的关于语言与观察之间关系的最朴素也最精确的解释。他的花园是小区里公认养得最好的，但没有人知道它是在凌晨两点的手电筒光里一棵一棵被照看过来的。
text_pinyin: Zhu zai wo louxia de tuixiu wuli laoshi you yi ge mimi: ta zhi zai yeli zhaoliao huayuan. Mei tian lingchen liang dian zuoyou, ta hui da zhe yi zhi weiruo de shoudiantong zou jin xiaoqu gonggong huatan, dun xialai ba cao, song tu, xiujian zhiye. Wo shi ou'ran cong shimian de chuangkou faxian zhe jian shi de. Di yi ci kan dao de shihou hai yiwei shi xiaotou, zixi bianren hou cai renchu ta de shenying. Wo houlai gu qi yongqi wen ta weishenme xuanze shenye laozuo, ta de huida chuhu yiliao: 'Zhiwu zai yeli qishi geng huoyue—tamen baitian guanghe zuoyong, yeli cai zhenzheng shengzhang. Wo lao le, jue shao, ganghao pei tamen. Erqie yeli mei ren kanjian, wo keyi gen tamen shuo hua, baitian zheme zuo de hua linju men hui yiwei wo feng le.' Ta shuo ta queshi hui gen mei yi zhu zhiwu shuo hua, gaosu tamen shui gou bu gou, gen zha de wen bu wen. Wo wen ta zhiwu neng ting dong ma, ta xiao le xiao shuo: 'Ting bu dong. Dan shuo hua de guocheng rang wo guancha de geng zixi. Zuiba zai miaoshu de shihou, yanjing hui zhuyidao pingshi hulue de xijie.' Zhe dagai shi wo ting guo de guanyu yuyan yu guancha zhijian guanxi de zui pusu ye zui jingque de jieshi. Ta de huayuan shi xiaoqu li gongren yang de zui hao de, dan meiyou ren zhidao ta shi zai lingchen liang dian de shoudiantong guang li yi ke yi ke bei zhaokan guolai de.
text_en: The retired physics teacher living below me has a secret: he only tends the garden at night. Around 2 AM each night, he takes a dim flashlight into the community's public flower beds, squats down to pull weeds, loosen soil, and trim branches. I discovered this by accident from my insomniac window. The first time I thought it was a thief; only after careful looking did I recognize his silhouette. I later mustered the courage to ask why he chose to work at night. His answer was unexpected: 'Plants are actually more active at night—they photosynthesize during the day but truly grow at night. I'm old and sleep little, so I happen to keep them company. Besides, at night no one sees me, so I can talk to them. Doing that in daytime would make the neighbors think I'm crazy.' He said he does speak to every plant, telling them whether they have enough water, whether their roots are firm. I asked if plants can understand. He smiled and said: 'They can't. But the process of speaking makes me observe more carefully. When the mouth is describing, the eyes notice details usually overlooked.' This is perhaps the most plain yet most precise explanation I've heard about the relationship between language and observation. His garden is universally acknowledged as the best-maintained in the community, but nobody knows it was tended plant by plant under a flashlight at 2 AM.

title: The Map Collector
text_zh: 我认识一个从不用导航软件的人。他叫老方，五十多岁，是一个城市规划局的退休干部。他的爱好是收集纸质地图——不是那种旅游纪念品，而是各个年代的城市交通图和区域规划图。他的书房里堆着上百份发黄的旧地图，按年份整齐排列。每次出门前他都会翻出对应区域的纸质地图研究一番，然后靠记忆导航。我问他为什么不用手机，他说：「导航软件给你的是一条最优路线，但它阉割了所有旁枝末节。你只知道从A到B最快怎么走，但不知道C那条巷子曾经是条河，D那个路口的名字来源于一棵已经被砍掉的古樟树。」老方带我在老城区走过一次，那次散步彻底改变了我看城市的方式。他指着每一个不起眼的路名牌告诉我它背后的故事，就像一个考古学家读地层一样读城市的皮肤。他说纸质地图最珍贵的地方在于它记录了「曾经的可能性」——那些被否决的规划方案、被填埋的河流、被改名的街道，在旧地图上都还活着。导航软件只关心「现在怎么走」，而纸质地图保存着「曾经可以怎么走」的记忆。
text_pinyin: Wo renshi yi ge cong bu yong daohang ruanjian de ren. Ta jiao Lao Fang, wushi duo sui, shi yi ge chengshi guihua ju de tuixiu ganbu. Ta de aihao shi shouji zhizhi ditu—bu shi na zhong luyou jinianpin, er shi ge ge niandai de chengshi jiaotong tu he quyu guihua tu. Ta de shufang li dui zhe shang bai fen fahuang de jiu ditu, an nianfen zhengqi pailie. Mei ci chumen qian ta dou hui fan chu duiying quyu de zhizhi ditu yanjiu yi fan, ranhou kao jiyi daohang. Wo wen ta weishenme bu yong shouji, ta shuo: 'Daohang ruanjian gei ni de shi yi tiao zuiyou luxian, dan ta yange le suoyou pangzhi mojie. Ni zhi zhidao cong A dao B zui kuai zenme zou, dan bu zhidao C na tiao xiangzi cengjing shi tiao he, D nage lukou de mingzi laiyuanyu yi ke yijing bei kan diao de gu zhangshu.' Lao Fang dai wo zai lao chengqu zou guo yi ci, na ci sanbu chedi gaibian le wo kan chengshi de fangshi. Ta zhi zhe mei yi ge bu qiyan de luming pai gaosu wo ta beihou de gushi, jiu xiang yi ge kaoguxuejia du diceng yiyang du chengshi de pifu. Ta shuo zhizhi ditu zui zhengui de difang zaiyu ta jilu le 'cengjing de kenengxing'—naxie bei foujue de guihua fang'an, bei tianmai de heliu, bei gaiming de jiedao, zai jiu ditu shang dou hai huo zhe. Daohang ruanjian zhi guanxin 'xianzai zenme zou', er zhizhi ditu baocun zhe 'cengjing keyi zenme zou' de jiyi.
text_en: I know someone who never uses navigation apps. His name is Old Fang, in his fifties, a retired official from the urban planning bureau. His hobby is collecting paper maps—not tourist souvenirs, but city traffic maps and regional planning maps from various eras. His study is stacked with over a hundred yellowed old maps, neatly arranged by year. Before every outing he unfolds the paper map of the relevant area to study, then navigates by memory. I asked why he doesn't use his phone. He said: 'Navigation apps give you the optimal route, but they castrate all the side details. You only know the fastest way from A to B, but don't know that the alley at C used to be a river, or that the intersection at D is named after an ancient camphor tree that's already been cut down.' Old Fang took me on a walk through the old city once, and that stroll completely changed how I see cities. He pointed at every inconspicuous street sign and told me its backstory, reading the city's skin like an archaeologist reads geological strata. He said the most precious thing about paper maps is that they record 'what once could have been'—rejected planning proposals, buried rivers, renamed streets all still live on old maps. Navigation apps only care about 'how to get there now,' while paper maps preserve the memory of 'how one could have once gone.'

title: The Balcony Astronomer
text_zh: 楼上那个独居的年轻人在阳台上架了一台天文望远镜。在城市里看星星这件事本身就有些荒谬——光污染严重到肉眼只能看到最亮的几颗行星和偶尔路过的飞机。但他每个晴朗的夜晚都会准时出现在阳台上，把望远镜对准天空，一待就是几个小时。楼里的人好奇过，有人问他能看到什么，他说：「其实看不到太多。但关键不在于看到什么，而在于那个仰头的动作本身。」他告诉我他是做程序开发的，每天工作十二个小时，对着屏幕上无穷的代码行，视线永远向下或平视。望远镜给了他一个物理上的理由把脖子仰起来，把视线投向无限远。他说这个姿势本身就有治疗效果——当你意识到自己在看的是几万光年外的一颗恒星的残影，白天那些让你焦虑的截止日期和代码冲突就会变得非常小。我后来也试过在阳台上仰望天空，没有望远镜，什么也看不到。但他说的对，重要的是那个仰头的动作。在一个所有人都低头看手机的时代，偶尔仰头的人大概看起来都像异类。可也许正因为异类，那几颗模糊的星才会被看见。
text_pinyin: Lou shang nage duju de nianqing ren zai yangtai shang jia le yi tai tianwen wangyuanjing. Zai chengshi li kan xingxing zhe jian shi benshen jiu youxie huangmiu—guang wuran yanzhong dao rouyan zhi neng kan dao zui liang de ji ke xingxing he ou'er luguo de feiji. Dan ta mei ge qinglang de yewan dou hui zhunshi chuxian zai yangtai shang, ba wangyuanjing dui zhun tiankong, yi dai jiu shi ji ge xiaoshi. Lou li de ren haoqi guo, you ren wen ta neng kan dao shenme, ta shuo: 'Qishi kan bu dao tai duo. Dan guanjian bu zaiyu kan dao shenme, er zaiyu nage yang tou de dongzuo benshen.' Ta gaosu wo ta shi zuo chengxu kaifa de, mei tian gongzuo shi'er ge xiaoshi, dui zhe pingmu shang wuqiong de daima hang, shixian yongyuan xiang xia huo pingshi. Wangyuanjing gei le ta yi ge wuli shang de liyou ba bozi yang qilai, ba shixian tou xiang wuxian yuan. Ta shuo zhege zishi benshen jiu you zhiliao xiaoguo—dang ni yishi dao ziji zai kan de shi ji wan guangnian wai de yi ke hengxing de canying, baitian naxie rang ni jiaolv de jiezhiriqi he daima chongtu jiu hui bian de feichang xiao. Wo houlai ye shi guo zai yangtai shang yangwang tiankong, meiyou wangyuanjing, shenme ye kan bu dao. Dan ta shuo de dui, zhongyao de shi nage yang tou de dongzuo. Zai yi ge suoyou ren dou ditou kan shouji de shidai, ou'er yang tou de ren dagai kan qilai dou xiang yilei. Ke yexu zheng yinwei yilei, na ji ke mohu de xing cai hui bei kanjian.
text_en: The young man living alone upstairs set up a telescope on his balcony. Stargazing in the city is inherently absurd—light pollution is so severe the naked eye can only see the brightest few planets and the occasional passing airplane. But every clear night he appears punctually on the balcony, points the telescope at the sky, and stays for hours. Building residents have been curious; someone asked what he can see. He said: 'Actually, not much. But the point isn't what you see—it's the act of looking up itself.' He told me he's a software developer, working twelve hours a day staring at infinite lines of code on screen, his gaze always downward or straight ahead. The telescope gives him a physical reason to tilt his neck upward and cast his gaze infinitely far. He said this posture itself has therapeutic effect—when you realize you're looking at the afterimage of a star tens of thousands of light-years away, the deadlines and code conflicts that caused your daytime anxiety become small. I later tried looking up at the sky from my balcony too, without a telescope, seeing nothing. But he was right—what matters is the act of looking up.

title: The Underground Bookstore
text_zh: 城市里还存活着的独立书店大多学会了伪装。有些变成了咖啡馆，卖咖啡顺带卖书；有些变成了文创空间，书只是背景墙的装饰。但我知道一家完全不伪装的书店，它在一栋老居民楼的半地下室里，没有招牌，没有公众号，你必须知道门牌号才能找到。老板姓严，四十多岁，以前在出版社做编辑，辞职后开了这家店。店里只卖他自己读过的书，每一本都附有他手写的简短书评，夹在扉页里。我问他为什么不做线上销售，他摇摇头说：「卖书不是卖商品，是把一个人读过的东西递给另一个人。这个'递'的动作必须是面对面的，我要看到对方的脸，判断这本书是否适合他。」他确实会拒绝卖书给某些人——不是势利，而是他真的觉得那本书跟那个人不匹配。有一次我想买一本存在主义哲学的书，他看了我一眼说：「你现在的状态不适合读这个，先拿这本散文集去吧。」我当时有点不高兴，但一个月后回想才觉得他说得对。半地下室的书店潮湿昏暗，空间狭小，但每一次走进去都像进入一个有人把关的私人图书馆。在算法推荐统治一切的时代，一个凭直觉帮你选书的真人，也许是最后的奢侈品。
text_pinyin: Chengshi li hai cunhuo zhe de duli shudian daduo xue hui le weizhuang. Youxie bian cheng le kafei guan, mai kafei shundai mai shu; youxie bian cheng le wenchuang kongjian, shu zhi shi beijing qiang de zhuangshi. Dan wo zhidao yi jia wanquan bu weizhuang de shudian, ta zai yi dong lao jumin lou de ban dixiashi li, meiyou zhaopai, meiyou gongzhonghao, ni bixu zhidao menpai hao cai neng zhao dao. Laoban xing Yan, sishi duo sui, yiqian zai chubanshe zuo bianji, cizhi hou kai le zhe jia dian. Dian li zhi mai ta ziji du guo de shu, mei yi ben dou fu you ta shouxie de jianduan shuping, jia zai feiyelili. Wo wen ta weishenme bu zuo xianshang xiaoshou, ta yaoyao tou shuo: 'Mai shu bu shi mai shangpin, shi ba yi ge ren du guo de dongxi di gei ling yi ge ren. Zhege di de dongzuo bixu shi mianduimian de, wo yao kan dao duifang de lian, panduan zhe ben shu shifou shihe ta.' Ta queshi hui jujue mai shu gei mou xie ren—bu shi shili, er shi ta zhende juede na ben shu gen na ge ren bu pipei. You yi ci wo xiang mai yi ben cunzai zhuyi zhexue de shu, ta kan le wo yi yan shuo: 'Ni xianzai de zhuangtai bu shihe du zhege, xian na zhe ben sanwen ji qu ba.' Wo dangshi you dian bu gaoxing, dan yi ge yue hou huixiang cai juede ta shuo de dui. Ban dixiashi de shudian chaoshi hun'an, kongjian xiaxiao, dan mei yi ci zou jinqu dou xiang jinru yi ge you ren baguan de siren tushuguan. Zai suanfa tuijian tongzhi yiqie de shidai, yi ge ping zhijue bang ni xuan shu de zhenren, yexu shi zuihou de shechipin.
text_en: Most surviving independent bookstores have learned to disguise themselves — becoming cafes or cultural spaces where books are decoration. But I know one that doesn't disguise itself. It's in the semi-basement of an old residential building: no sign, no social media. You must know the door number. The owner, surnamed Yan, formerly an editor, only sells books he's read, each with his handwritten review tucked into the title page. I asked why he doesn't sell online. He shook his head: 'Selling books isn't selling commodities — it's handing something one person has read to another. This act must be face to face; I need to see whether the book suits them.' He refuses certain sales — not snobbery, but genuine mismatch. Once I wanted an existentialist philosophy book. He glanced at me: 'Your current state isn't right for this. Take this essay collection first.' I was annoyed, but a month later realized he was right. The semi-basement is damp, dim, and cramped, but walking in feels like entering a private library with someone standing guard.

title: The Library's Secret Hours
text_zh: 在区图书馆工作的朋友告诉我一个有趣的规律：图书馆最安静的时间不是开馆时，而是下午两点到三点之间——这是午饭后的倦怠期，大多数人在这个时段要么打盹要么走神。但这恰恰是图书馆最「忠实」的读者群体出没的时间。他们是一群退休老人，每天准时出现，各自占据固定的座位，翻阅固定的报纸和杂志。座位的归属已经形成了一种默契的领地制度——没有明文规定，但如果有人坐了「别人的」位置，空气里会弥漫一种微妙的不快。最让我感兴趣的是图书馆的另一个秘密时段：周二和周四的早上九点，总有几位母亲带着学龄前的孩子来听故事会。图书馆管理员小陈说，这些母亲其实不全是为了孩子——她们需要一个正当的理由出门，需要跟其他成年人说说话，需要一个不用解释自己为什么不上班的公共空间。「图书馆的功能远比'借书'复杂得多，」小陈说，「它其实是一个伪装成文化机构的社区庇护所。」
text_pinyin: Zai qu tushuguan gongzuo de pengyou gaosu wo yi ge youqu de guilu: tushuguan zui anjing de shijian bu shi kai guan shi, er shi xiawu liang dian dao san dian zhijian—zhe shi wufan hou de juandai qi, daduoshu ren zai zhege shiduan yaome dadun yaome zoushen. Dan zhe qiaqia shi tushuguan zui 'zhongshi' de duzhe qunti chumo de shijian. Tamen shi yi qun tuixiu laoren, mei tian zhunshi chuxian, gezi zhanju guding de zuowei, fanyue guding de baozhi he zazhi. Zuowei de guishu yijing xingcheng le yi zhong moqi de lingdi zhidu—meiyou mingwen guiding, dan ruguo you ren zuo le 'bieren de' weizhi, kongqi li hui miman yi zhong weimiao de bukuai. Zui rang wo gan xingqu de shi tushuguan de ling yi ge mimi shiduan: zhou'er he zhousi de zaoshang jiu dian, zong you ji wei muqin dai zhe xuelingqian de haizi lai ting gushihui. Tushuguan guanliyuan Xiao Chen shuo, zhexie muqin qishi bu quan shi weile haizi—tamen xuyao yi ge zhengdang de liyou chumen, xuyao gen qita chengnianren shuo shuo hua, xuyao yi ge bu yong jieshi ziji weishenme bu shangban de gonggong kongjian. 'Tushuguan de gongneng yuan bi jie shu fuza de duo,' Xiao Chen shuo, 'ta qishi shi yi ge weizhuang cheng wenhua jigou de shequ bihuosuo.'
text_en: A friend who works at the district library told me an interesting pattern: the library's quietest time isn't opening hour but between 2 and 3 PM—the post-lunch drowsy period when most people are either dozing or zoning out. But this is precisely when the library's most 'loyal' reader group appears. They're a group of retirees who arrive punctually each day, each claiming a fixed seat, reading fixed newspapers and magazines. Seat ownership has formed a tacit territorial system—no written rules, but if someone sits in 'another's' spot, a subtle displeasure permeates the air. What interests me most is the library's other secret time slot: Tuesday and Thursday mornings at nine, a few mothers always bring preschool children for story time. Librarian Xiao Chen says these mothers aren't entirely there for the children—they need a legitimate reason to leave the house, need to talk to other adults, need a public space where they don't have to explain why they're not at work. 'The library's function is far more complex than borrowing books,' Xiao Chen says. 'It's actually a community shelter disguised as a cultural institution.'

title: The Night Shift Nurse
text_zh: 在医院陪夜的时候，凌晨三点的走廊像另一个世界。日光灯只开了一半，墙上的影子拉得很长，偶尔有输液架的轮子在地板上滑过，发出细小而尖锐的声音。夜班护士小赵每隔两小时巡房一次，她穿着白大褂走过长长的走廊时脚步极轻，连鞋底摩擦地板的声音都被刻意压到最低。她告诉我她学了三年才掌握这种走路方式——「在病房里，声音是有重量的。白天人多，噪音被稀释了，但凌晨三点任何一个细微的声响都会被放大十倍。一个关门声可能让刚睡着的病人惊醒，多醒一次就少恢复一点。」她随身带着一个小笔记本，记录着每个病人的睡眠规律——谁容易惊醒，谁会梦游，谁需要在固定时间翻身防止褥疮。这些信息不在病历里，却决定着病人夜间的生活质量。我问她做夜班最大的挑战是什么，她想了想说：「不是困，是寂寞。凌晨的医院只有你是清醒的，你要对着一整层楼的沉睡负责，这种责任感有时候重得让人喘不过气。」天快亮的时候，她站在走廊尽头的窗前看了一会儿渐渐发白的天空，然后回过头对我说：「每次看到天亮我都会松一口气——又安全地陪他们度过了一个夜晚。」
text_pinyin: Zai yiyuan pei ye de shihou, lingchen san dian de zoulang xiang ling yi ge shijie. Riguangdeng zhi kai le yiban, qiang shang de yingzi la de hen chang, ou'er you shuyejia de lunzi zai diban shang hua guo, fachu xixiao er jianirrui de shengyin. Yeban hushi Xiao Zhao mei ge liang xiaoshi xunfang yi ci, ta chuan zhe bai dagua zou guo changchang de zoulang shi jiaobu ji qing, lian xiedi moca diban de shengyin dou bei keyi ya dao zuidi. Ta gaosu wo ta xue le san nian cai zhangwo zhe zhong zoulu fangshi—'Zai bingfang li, shengyin shi you zhongliang de. Baitian ren duo, zaoyin bei xishi le, dan lingchen san dian renhe yi ge xiwei de shengxiang dou hui bei fangda shi bei. Yi ge guan men sheng keneng rang gang shui zhao de bingren jingxing, duo xing yi ci jiu shao huifu yi dian.' Ta suishen dai zhe yi ge xiao bijiben, jilu zhe mei ge bingren de shuimian guilu—shei rongyi jingxing, shei hui mengyou, shei xuyao zai guding shijian fanshen fangzhi ruchuang. Zhexie xinxi bu zai bingli li, que jueding zhe bingren yeqian de shenghuo zhiliang. Wo wen ta zuo yeban zuida de tiaozhan shi shenme, ta xiang le xiang shuo: 'Bu shi kun, shi jimo. Lingchen de yiyuan zhi you ni shi qingxing de, ni yao dui zhe yi zheng ceng lou de chenshui fuze, zhe zhong zerenggan youshihou zhong de rang ren chuan bu guo qi.' Tian kuai liang de shihou, ta zhan zai zoulang jintou de chuang qian kan le yihuir jianjian fabai de tiankong, ranhou hui guo tou dui wo shuo: 'Mei ci kan dao tian liang wo dou hui song yi kou qi—you anquan de pei tamen duguo le yi ge yewan.'
text_en: During my night vigil at the hospital, the 3 AM corridor was like another world. Half the fluorescent lights on, shadows stretched long, occasionally an IV stand's wheels sliding with a sharp sound. Night nurse Xiao Zhao makes rounds every two hours; her footsteps are deliberately light, even shoe-sole sounds suppressed. She said it took three years to master this walk — 'In hospital wards, sound has weight. At 3 AM any tiny sound is amplified tenfold. One door closing might wake a patient who just fell asleep.' She carries a notebook recording each patient's sleep patterns — who startles easily, who sleepwalks, who needs turning to prevent bedsores. This information isn't in medical charts but determines nighttime quality of life. I asked about the biggest challenge of night shift. She thought: 'Not sleepiness — loneliness. In the predawn hospital you're the only one awake, responsible for an entire floor of sleepers. That weight sometimes makes it hard to breathe.' As dawn approached, she stood at the corridor window watching the sky lighten, then said: 'Every time I see daylight I breathe relief — I've safely accompanied them through another night.'

title: The Midnight Caller
text_zh: 连续三个月，每周三午夜十二点零五分，我的座机电话都会响。拿起听筒，那头沉默几秒，然后挂断。来电显示是一个本地号码，但回拨过去永远是忙音。我一开始以为是骚扰电话或者技术故障，但它的精确度让人起疑——每周三，不多不少，十二点零五分，持续响铃五声后如果我没接就自动挂断；如果我接了，对方沉默约三秒再挂。朋友建议我报警，但这算什么案件呢？对方既没说话也没威胁。我试过在周三之前拔掉电话线，那天夜里果然没有来电，但下一个周三恢复后照常响。我甚至换了一个新电话号码，但第三个周三，新号码照样在十二点零五分响了。这让我后背发凉，因为我确信没有人知道我的新号码。最终我选择了一种可能是最不理性的应对方式：每到周三午夜，我提前把茶泡好，坐在电话旁边等着它响，然后接起来，在对方沉默的三秒钟里说一句「晚安」再挂掉。第四个月的时候，电话突然不再响了。我至今不知道那三个月的来电意味着什么，但我偶尔会想起那些深夜的「晚安」——也许这个世界上，有些事不需要解释，只需要有人回应。
text_pinyin: Lianxu san ge yue, mei zhousan wuye shi'er dian ling wu fen, wo de zuoji dianhua dou hui xiang. Na qi tingtong, natou chenmo ji miao, ranhou guaduan. Laidian xianshi shi yi ge bendi haoma, dan hui bo guoqu yongyuan shi mangyin. Wo yi kaishi yiwei shi saorao dianhua huozhe jishu guzhang, dan ta de jingqudu rang ren qi yi—mei zhousan, bu duo bu shao, shi'er dian ling wu fen, chixu xiangle wu sheng hou ruguo wo mei jie jiu zidong guaduan; ruguo wo jie le, duifang chenmo yue san miao zai gua. Pengyou jianyi wo baojing, dan zhe suan shenme anjian ne? Duifang ji mei shuo hua ye mei weixie. Wo shi guo zai zhousan zhiqian ba diao dianhua xian, na tian yeli guoran meiyou laidian, dan xia yi ge zhousan huifu hou zhao chang xiang. Wo shenzhi huan le yi ge xin dianhua haoma, dan di san ge zhousan, xin haoma zhaoyang zai shi'er dian ling wu fen xiang le. Zhe rang wo houbei fa liang, yinwei wo quexin meiyou ren zhidao wo de xin haoma. Zuizhong wo xuanze le yi zhong keneng shi zui bu lixing de yingdui fangshi: mei dao zhousan wuye, wo tiqian ba cha pao hao, zuo zai dianhua pangbian deng zhe ta xiang, ranhou jie qilai, zai duifang chenmo de san miao zhong li shuo yi ju 'wan'an' zai gua diao. Di si ge yue de shihou, dianhua turan bu zai xiang le. Wo zhijin bu zhidao na san ge yue de laidian yiwei zhe shenme, dan wo ou'er hui xiangqi naxie shenye de 'wan'an'—yexu zhege shijie shang, youxie shi bu xuyao jieshi, zhi xuyao you ren huiying.
text_en: For three months, every Wednesday at exactly 12:05 AM, my landline would ring. I'd pick up; the other end would go silent a few seconds, then hang up. Caller ID showed a local number, but calling back got a busy signal. Its precision was suspicious — every Wednesday, 12:05, ringing five times before auto-disconnecting; if I answered, three seconds of silence before hanging up. I tried unplugging the line before Wednesday; no call came, but the next Wednesday after reconnecting, it rang as usual. I changed my number. The third Wednesday, the new number rang at 12:05. This sent chills — I was certain no one knew my new number. I chose the least rational response: every Wednesday midnight I brewed tea, sat by the phone, picked up, and said 'good night' during the caller's silence before hanging up. In the fourth month, the calls stopped. I still don't know what those three months meant, but I think of those late-night 'good nights' — some things don't need explanation, only someone to respond.

title: The Garden That Blooms at Night
text_zh: 隔壁别墅的花园有一个违反常识的现象：某些花只在夜间开放。我说的不是昙花或夜来香这些本来就是夜开的品种，而是明明应该白天开的月季、茉莉和栀子花，到了那个花园里却反过来了——白天闭合，入夜后才渐渐绽放。园艺师朋友来看过一次，百思不得其解。他检查了土壤酸碱度、光照时间、浇水频率，所有参数都在正常范围内。唯一异常的是花园的光照环境：隔壁那栋别墅的主人是个夜猫子，每天从傍晚开始亮灯，客厅的落地窗正对花园，灯光一直亮到凌晨三四点。园艺师推测，长年累月的人工光源可能扰乱了植物的昼夜节律，让它们「误以为」夜间才是白天。如果这个解释成立，那么这个花园里上演的是一种微型的生态错位——人类的生活节奏强行改写了植物的生物钟。我站在围墙外面看过几次那个花园的夜间花期，月光下绽放的茉莉确实有一种诡异的美，像一群在错误时区醒来的旅人，茫然却灿烂。
text_pinyin: Gebi bieshu de huayuan you yi ge weifan changshi de xianxiang: mou xie hua zhi zai yeqian kaifang. Wo shuo de bu shi tanhua huo yelaixin zhexie benlai jiu shi ye kai de pinzhong, er shi mingming yinggai baitian kai de yueji, moli he zhizihua, dao le nage huayuan li que fan guolai le—baitian bihe, ru ye hou cai jianjian zhanfang. Yuanyi shi pengyou lai kan guo yi ci, baisibujieq. Ta jiancha le turang suanjian du, guangzhao shijian, jiao shui pinlv, suoyou canshu dou zai zhengchang fanwei nei. Weiyi yichang de shi huayuan de guangzhao huanjing: gebi na dong bieshu de zhuren shi ge yemao zi, mei tian cong bangwan kaishi liang deng, keting de luodi chuang zheng dui huayuan, dengguang yizhi liang dao lingchen san si dian. Yuanyi shi tuice, changnianleiyue de rengong guangyuan keneng raoluan le zhiwu de zhouye jielv, rang tamen 'wu yiwei' yeqian cai shi baitian. Ruguo zhege jieshi chengli, name zhege huayuan li shangyan de shi yi zhong weixing de shengtai cuowei—renlei de shenghuo jiezou qiangxing gaixie le zhiwu de shengwuzhong. Wo zhan zai weiqiang waimian kan guo ji ci nage huayuan de yeqian huaqi, yueguang xia zhanfang de moli queshi you yi zhong guiyi de mei, xiang yi qun zai cuowu shiqu xing lai de lvren, mangran que canlan. Yexu mei youshihou qiaqia chansheng yu cuowei—zai bu gai kai de shihou kai le, zai bu gai liang de shihou liang le, fan'er bi yiqie anbu jiuban geng ling ren zhumu.
text_en: The garden of the villa next door has a phenomenon that defies common sense: certain flowers only bloom at night. I don't mean night-blooming species like moonflowers or tuberoses, but roses, jasmine, and gardenias that should bloom by day—in that garden they're reversed, closing during daytime and gradually opening after dark. A horticulturist friend came to look once and was completely baffled. He checked soil pH, light exposure, watering frequency—all parameters within normal range. The only anomaly was the garden's light environment: the villa's owner is a night owl who turns on lights starting at dusk, the living room's floor-to-ceiling windows facing the garden, lights staying on until 3 or 4 AM. The horticulturist speculated that years of artificial light may have disrupted the plants' circadian rhythms, making them 'believe' nighttime is daytime. If this explanation holds, the garden stages a miniature ecological displacement—human living rhythms forcibly rewriting plants' biological clocks. I've stood outside the wall watching the garden's nighttime bloom several times. Jasmine opening under moonlight does have an eerie beauty, like a group of travelers who woke up in the wrong time zone—bewildered yet radiant.

title: The Photograph I Don't Recognize
text_zh: 整理旧手机的时候，翻到一张六年前在西藏拍的照片——照片里是我自己，站在纳木错湖边，笑得非常灿烂。问题是我完全不记得拍这张照片的场景。不是健忘，而是照片里那个人的笑容、站姿、甚至穿的衣服都让我感到陌生，仿佛在看一个长得跟我很像的陌生人。我试图回忆那次旅行的细节，只能想起一些模糊的片段——高原反应的头痛、酥油茶的气味、夜里帐篷外的风声。但那个灿烂的笑容背后是什么情绪，我完全想不起来。这让我意识到一个不舒服的事实：六年前的那个我和现在的我，也许在生物学上是同一个人，但在心理学上已经是两个不同的人了。细胞更换了好几轮，经历重塑了性格，连对快乐的定义都变了——六年前的我大概觉得站在壮丽的湖边就是快乐，而现在的我更可能在一杯安静的咖啡里找到类似的感觉。照片是时间的陷阱：它保存了一个瞬间的你，但那个瞬间的你已经不存在了，存在的只是关于他的一堆像素。我把那张照片存进了一个单独的文件夹，取名叫「前任」——不是别人，是以前的自己。
text_pinyin: Zhengli jiu shouji de shihou, fan dao yi zhang liu nian qian zai Xizang pai de zhaopian—zhaopian li shi wo ziji, zhan zai Namucuo hu bian, xiao de feichang canlan. Wenti shi wo wanquan bu jide pai zhe zhang zhaopian de changjing. Bu shi jianwang, er shi zhaopian li nage ren de xiaorong, zhanzhi, shenzhi chuan de yifu dou rang wo gandao mosheng, fangfu zai kan yi ge zhang de gen wo hen xiang de moshengren. Wo shitu huiyi na ci lxing de xijie, zhi neng xiangqi yixie mohu de pianduan—gaoyuan fanying de toutong, suyoucha de qiwei, yeli zhangpeng wai de fengsheng. Dan nage canlan de xiaorong beihou shi shenme qingxu, wo wanquan xiang bu qilai. Zhe rang wo yishi dao yi ge bu shufu de shishi: liu nian qian de nage wo he xianzai de wo, yexu zai shengwuxue shang shi tong yi ge ren, dan zai xinlixue shang yijing shi liang ge butong de ren le. Xibao genghuan le hao ji lun, jingli chongsu le xingge, lian dui kuaile de dingyi dou bian le—liu nian qian de wo dagai juede zhan zai zhuangli de hu bian jiu shi kuaile, er xianzai de wo geng keneng zai yi bei anjing de kafei li zhao dao leisi de ganjue. Zhaopian shi shijian de xianjing: ta baocun le yi ge shunjian de ni, dan nage shunjian de ni yijing bu cunzai le, cunzai de zhi shi guanyu ta de yi dui xiangsu. Wo ba na zhang zhaopian cun jin le yi ge dandude wenjian jia, qu ming jiao 'qianren'—bu shi bieren, shi yiqian de ziji.
text_en: Sorting through an old phone, I found a photo from six years ago taken in Tibet—it's me, standing by Namtso Lake, smiling radiantly. The problem is I have absolutely no memory of the scene when it was taken. Not forgetfulness—the smile, posture, even the clothes of the person in the photo feel foreign to me, as if looking at a stranger who closely resembles me. I tried recalling details of that trip but could only summon vague fragments—altitude sickness headache, the smell of butter tea, wind outside the tent at night. But what emotion was behind that radiant smile, I cannot recall at all. This made me realize an uncomfortable truth: the me from six years ago and the current me may be biologically the same person, but psychologically we're already two different people. Cells have been replaced several times over, experiences have reshaped personality, even the definition of happiness has changed—six years ago I probably felt that standing beside a magnificent lake was happiness, while the current me is more likely to find a similar feeling in a quiet cup of coffee.

title: The Group Chat Etiquette
text_zh: 我目前同时存在于六十三个微信群里，其中大约五十个处于永久静音状态。每个群都有自己的潜规则，违反任何一条都可能引发社交灾难。比如家族群：只能发鼓励性质的内容和养生文章，绝对不能发政治观点或任何可能引发争论的话题，否则你大姨会在下次家庭聚餐时意味深长地看你一眼。同学群的规则更微妙：可以回忆往事但不能提当年谁追过谁，可以炫耀孩子但程度不能超过组委会默认的阈值——一般来说，考上重点中学可以发，考上清华就只能低调私聊了。最复杂的是工作群。老板发了一条消息以后，第一个回复「收到」的人显得过于谄媚，最后回复的人显得不够积极，最安全的策略是在第三到第五个回复。如果老板在群里发了一条工作指令附带一个微笑表情，那个微笑的含义至少有七种可能的解读，跟古代的皇帝圣旨一样需要反复推敲。
text_pinyin: Wo muqian tongshi cunzai yu liushisan ge weixin qun li, qizhong dayue wushi ge chu yu yongjiu jingyin zhuangtai. Mei ge qun dou you ziji de qian guize, weifan renhe yi tiao dou keneng yinfa shejiao zainan. Biru jiazu qun: zhi neng fa guli xingzhi de neirong he yangsheng wenzhang, juedui bu neng fa zhengzhi guandian huo renhe keneng yinfa zhenglun de huati, fouze ni dayi hui zai xia ci jiating jucan shi yiweishenchang de kan ni yi yan. Tongxue qun de guize geng weimiao: keyi huiyi wangshi dan bu neng ti dangnian shei zhui guo shei, keyi xuanyao haizi dan chengdu bu neng chaoguo zuweihu moren de yuzhì—yiban lai shuo, kao shang zhongdian zhongxue keyi fa, kao shang Qinghua jiu zhi neng diao silian le. Zui fuza de shi gongzuo qun. Laoban fa le yi tiao xiaoxi yihou, di yi ge huifu 'shoudao' de ren xian de guoyu chanmei, zuihou huifu de ren xian de bu gou jiji, zui anquan de celue shi zai di san dao di wu ge huifu. Ruguo laoban zai qun li fa le yi tiao gongzuo zhiling fudai yi ge weixiao biaoqing, nage weixiao de hanyi zhishao you qi zhong keneng de jiedu, gen gudai de huangdi shengzhi yiyang xuyao fanfu tuiqiao. Wo youshihou juede, renlei hua zai qunliao guize shang de renzhi ziyuan, ruguo yong lai xue yi men waiyu dagai zao yi jingtong san zhong le.
text_en: I'm currently a member of sixty-three WeChat groups, about fifty of which are permanently muted. Each group has its own unwritten rules; violating any could trigger a social disaster. For example, the family group: only encouragement and health articles allowed, absolutely no political opinions or controversy-inducing topics, or your aunt will give you a meaningful look at the next family dinner. Classmate group rules are more subtle: reminiscing is fine but don't mention who had a crush on whom back then; you can show off your kids but not beyond the committee's default threshold — getting into a key middle school can be shared, but getting into Tsinghua should be kept to modest private messages. Most complex is the work group. After the boss sends a message, the first to reply 'received' seems too sycophantic, the last to reply seems not proactive enough; the safest strategy is replying third to fifth. If the boss sends a work directive accompanied by a smiley emoji, that smile has at least seven possible interpretations, requiring as much scrutiny as an ancient emperor's imperial decree.

title: The Parking Space Diplomacy
text_zh: 我们小区地下车库有九十八个车位和一百零三辆车，这意味着永远有五辆车找不到地方停。这个简单的算术题催生了一套堪比国际外交的复杂博弈。首先是「占位神器」的军备竞赛：最早有人用椅子占车位，很快升级为路锥，再后来出现了写着恶毒警告语的纸板牌、铁链加锁、甚至有人搬来了一盆巨大的仙人掌。物业出了一条规定禁止私人占位，但执行力度约等于联合国决议。最精彩的一幕发生在上个月：三楼的刘叔回来发现自己的车位被一辆陌生的外地牌照车占了，他没有打电话投诉，而是默默地把自己的车横着停在那辆车前面，然后回家睡觉了。第二天早上对方车主下来发现出不去，打了十七个电话刘叔都没接。最终对方不得不通过物业、保安、邻居层层传话，亲自上门道歉。刘叔这才不紧不慢地下楼挪车，挪之前还说了句：「记住了，这是我的位置。」
text_pinyin: Women xiaoqu dixia cheku you jiushiba ge chewei he yibailingsan liang che, zhe yiweizhe yongyuan you wu liang che zhao bu dao difang ting. Zhege jiandan de suanshu ti cuisheng le yi tao kanbi guoji waijiao de fuza boyi. Shouxian shi 'zhan wei shenqi' de junbei jingsai: zuizao you ren yong yizi zhan chewei, hen kuai shengji wei luzhui, zai houlai chuxian le xie zhe edu jinggaoyu de zhibanpai, tielian jia suo, shenzhi you ren ban lai le yi pen juda de xianrenzhang. Wuye chu le yi tiao guiding jinzhi siren zhan wei, dan zhixing lidu yue dengyu lianheguo jueyi. Yushi dixia cheku xingcheng le yi tao wanquan jiyu renji guanxi de fei zhengshi fenpei xitong: er lou de Zhang zong gen bao'an Lao Li shi paiyou, ta de chewei yongyuan you ren bang kan zhe; qi lou de Zhao jie mei nian chunjie gei wuye song chaye, ta de chewei pangbian de guodao conglai bu hui bei du zhu. Zui jingcai de yi mu fasheng zai shang ge yue: san lou de Liu shu huilai faxian ziji de chewei bei yi liang mosheng de waidi paizhao che zhan le, ta meiyou da dianhua tousu, er shi momo de ba ziji de che heng zhe ting zai na liang che qianmian, ranhou hui jia shuijiao le. Di er tian zaoshang duifang chezhu xialai faxian chu bu qu, da le shiqi ge dianhua Liu shu dou mei jie. Zuizhong duifang budebu tongguo wuye, bao'an, linju cengceng chuanhua, qinzi shangmen daoqian. Liu shu zhe cai bu jin bu man de xialou nuo che, nuo zhiqian hai shuo le ju: 'Jizhu le, zhe shi wo de weizhi.'—chewei waijiao de zuigao duanwei, dagai jiu shi bu dong shengse de weikun.
text_en: Our community's underground garage has ninety-eight spots and one hundred and three cars, meaning five cars can never find parking. This simple arithmetic problem has spawned a complex game rivaling international diplomacy. First came the 'spot-saving device' arms race: it started with chairs, quickly escalated to traffic cones, then appeared cardboard signs with vicious warnings, chains with padlocks, and someone even hauled in a giant cactus. Property management issued a rule banning private spot-claiming, but enforcement was roughly equivalent to UN resolutions. The most brilliant scene happened last month: Uncle Liu from the third floor returned to find his spot taken by an unfamiliar car with out-of-town plates. He didn't call to complain but silently parked his car sideways in front of the offender, then went home to sleep. The next morning the other driver came down to find they couldn't get out and made seventeen unanswered calls to Uncle Liu. Eventually the driver had to go through property management, security, and neighbors layer by layer to deliver a personal door-to-door apology. Only then did Uncle Liu unhurriedly go down to move his car, saying before he did: 'Remember, this is my spot.'

title: The Wonton Shop's Last Winter
title_zh: 馄饨店的最后一个冬天
text_zh: 街口那家开了三十年的馄饨店要关了，老板娘周姐说房租涨了三倍，撑不下去了。消息传出来以后的那个星期，店里每天都排长队，平时从不来的人也突然涌了过来——这大概就是人类的本性，只有在即将失去的时候才意识到珍贵。周姐的馄饨没什么惊天动地的秘方，就是猪肉荠菜馅，皮薄到能隐约看见里面的绿色。她包馄饨的速度极快，两只手像在表演魔术，一捏一折一扔，每个馄饨落在笸箩里的声音轻得像一声叹息。我问过她为什么不做外卖扩大销量，她说：「馄饨离开了汤就不是馄饨了。外卖盒里泡二十分钟的馄饨跟我锅里刚捞出来的是两种东西。我卖了三十年，不想在最后几个月毁了自己的名声。」关店那天我特意去吃了最后一碗。周姐照例在碗里放了一撮虾皮、几滴香油、一小把葱花——三十年不变的配置，精确得像一个不需要更新的算法。吃完以后我把碗放在桌上，看着碗底残留的汤汁，忽然觉得这碗馄饨的味道并不特别好吃，但它是这条街的味觉坐标——坐标消失以后，整条街在味觉上就迷路了。
text_pinyin: Jiekou na jia kai le sanshi nian de huntun dian yao guan le, laobanniang Zhou jie shuo fangzu zhang le san bei, cheng bu xia qu le. Xiaoxi chuan chulai yihou de nage xingqi, dian li mei tian dou pai changdui, pingshi cong bu lai de ren ye turan yong le guolai—zhe dagai jiu shi renlei de benxing, zhi you zai jijiang shiqu de shihou cai yishi dao zhengui. Zhou jie de huntun mei shenme jingtian dongdi de mifang, jiu shi zhurou jicai xian, pi bao dao neng yinyue kanjian limian de lvse. Ta bao huntun de sudu ji kuai, liang zhi shou xiang zai biaoyan moshu, yi nie yi zhe yi reng, mei ge huntun luo zai poluo li de shengyin qing de xiang yi sheng tanxi. Wo wen guo ta weishenme bu zuo waimai kuoda xiaoliang, ta shuo: 'Huntun likai le tang jiu bu shi huntun le. Waimai he li pao ershi fenzhong de huntun gen wo guo li gang lao chulai de shi liang zhong dongxi. Wo mai le sanshi nian, bu xiang zai zuihou ji ge yue hui le ziji de mingsheng.' Guan dian na tian wo teyi qu chi le zuihou yi wan. Zhou jie zhaoli zai wan li fang le yi cuo xiapi, ji di xiangyou, yi xiao ba conghua—sanshi nian bu bian de peizhi, jingque de xiang yi ge bu xuyao gengxin de suanfa. Chi wan yihou wo ba wan fang zai zhuo shang, kan zhe wan di canliu de tangzhi, huran juede zhe wan huntun de weidao bing bu tebie hao chi, dan ta shi zhe tiao jie de weijue zuobiao—zuobiao xiaoshi yihou, zheng tiao jie zai weijue shang jiu milu le.
text_en: The wonton shop at the corner that's been open thirty years is closing. The owner, Sister Zhou, says rent tripled and she can't hold on. In the week after the news, the shop had long queues daily; people who never came suddenly appeared—this is probably human nature, only recognizing preciousness when about to lose it. Sister Zhou's wontons have no earth-shattering recipe—just pork and shepherd's purse filling, wrappers thin enough to faintly see the green inside. She wraps wontons incredibly fast, both hands performing magic—one pinch, one fold, one toss, each wonton landing in the basket with a sound light as a sigh. I once asked why she doesn't do delivery to increase sales. She said: 'Wontons without broth aren't wontons. A wonton soaked twenty minutes in a delivery box and one just ladled from my pot are two different things. I've been selling for thirty years; I don't want to ruin my reputation in the last few months.' On closing day I made a point to eat the last bowl. Sister Zhou placed her usual pinch of dried shrimp, few drops of sesame oil, and small handful of scallions in the bowl—thirty years unchanged, precise as an algorithm that never needs updating. After finishing, I set the bowl on the table, looking at the residual broth, and suddenly felt the wonton's flavor wasn't particularly delicious, but it was this street's taste coordinate—once the coordinate disappears, the entire street becomes lost in taste.

title: The Wrong Train
text_zh: 在日本旅行的第三天，我坐错了列车。应该去京都的车把我带到了一个从未听说过的小站，站名用片假名写的，我一个字也看不懂。手机恰好没电了，下一班返程车要等两个小时。我站在空荡荡的站台上，前所未有地感到了一种纯粹的「迷失」——不是焦虑意义上的迷失，而是所有导航和日程都失效以后产生的一种奇异的轻盈感。我走出车站，沿着唯一一条路往前走，路两边是整齐的稻田，远处有几栋灰色的民居，一只猫蹲在邮筒旁边晒太阳。我在一家只有三张桌子的小面馆吃了一碗乌冬面，老板是个不会说英语的老太太，我们之间的交流完全靠手势和微笑。那碗面出奇地好吃，也许是因为饥饿，也许是因为没有任何期待。吃完面我在镇子里走了一圈，发现了一个只有十几块墓碑的小墓地、一家门口贴着手写「定休日」的理发店、和一台还在运转的古老自动贩卖机。这些都不是任何旅行攻略会提到的「景点」，但它们拼凑出了一幅比任何网红打卡地都更真实的日本生活图景。回到站台等车的时候，我想：也许旅行最好的时刻就是计划崩溃的时刻——当你不知道自己在哪里的时候，你才会真正开始看见你所在的地方。
text_pinyin: Zai Riben lxing de di san tian, wo zuo cuo le lieche. Yinggai qu Jingdu de che ba wo dai dao le yi ge cong wei tingshuoguo de xiao zhan, zhanming yong pianjiaming xie de, wo yi ge zi ye kan bu dong. Shouji qiahao mei dian le, xia yi ban fancheng che yao deng liang ge xiaoshi. Wo zhan zai kongdangdang de zhantai shang, qiansuoweiyou de gandao le yi zhong chuncui de 'mishi'—bu shi jiaolv yiyi shang de mishi, er shi suoyou daohang he richeng dou shixi yihou chansheng de yi zhong qiyi de qingying gan. Wo zou chu chezhan, yan zhe weiyi yi tiao lu wang qian zou, lu liang bian shi zhengqi de daotian, yuanchu you ji dong huise de minju, yi zhi mao dun zai youtong pangbian shai taiyang. Wo zai yi jia zhi you san zhang zhuozi de xiao mianguan chi le yi wan wudong mian, laoban shi ge bu hui shuo Yingyu de laotaitai, women zhijian de jiaoliu wanquan kao shoushi he weixiao. Na wan mian chuqi de hao chi, yexu shi yinwei ji'e, yexu shi yinwei meiyou renhe qidai. Chi wan mian wo zai zhenzi li zou le yi quan, faxian le yi ge zhi you shi ji kuai mubei de xiao mudi, yi jia menkou tie zhe shouxie 'dingxiuri' de lifa dian, he yi tai hai zai yunzhuan de gulao zidong fanmai ji. Zhexie dou bu shi renhe lxing gonglue hui ti dao de 'jingdian', dan tamen pincoule yi fu bi renhe wanghong daka di dou geng zhenshi de Riben shenghuo tujing. Hui dao zhantai deng che de shihou, wo xiang: yexu lxing zuihao de shike jiu shi jihua bengkui de shike—dang ni bu zhidao ziji zai nali de shihou, ni cai hui zhenzheng kaishi kanjian ni suozai de difang.
text_en: On the third day of traveling in Japan, I took the wrong train. The one bound for Kyoto took me to a tiny station I'd never heard of, its name written in katakana I couldn't read. My phone happened to be dead, and the next return train was two hours away. Standing on the empty platform, I felt an unprecedented pure 'lostness'—not anxious lostness, but a strange lightness produced when all navigation and schedules become invalid. I walked out of the station along the only road, neat rice paddies on both sides, a few grey houses in the distance, a cat squatting by a mailbox sunbathing. I ate a bowl of udon at a noodle shop with only three tables; the owner was an old lady who spoke no English, and we communicated entirely through gestures and smiles. The noodles were surprisingly delicious—perhaps from hunger, perhaps from having zero expectations. After eating, I walked around the town, discovering a small cemetery with only a dozen headstones, a barbershop with a handwritten 'closed today' sign on its door, and an ancient vending machine still running. None of these are 'sights' any travel guide would mention, but they assembled a picture of Japanese life more authentic than any Instagram hotspot.

title: The Fog on the Glass
text_zh: 冬天的咖啡馆里，靠窗的座位总是最先被占满。不是因为风景好——外面灰蒙蒙的，树也秃了——而是因为玻璃上会起一层雾气。有人用手指在上面画画，有人写字，更多的人只是看着那层雾慢慢凝成水珠，顺着玻璃滑下来。我观察过很多次，发现一个规律：独自来的人更容易注意到雾气，而结伴来的人几乎不会看窗户。这大概说明了什么。孤独的人对环境的感知更敏锐，因为他们没有对话来填满注意力。于是周围的一切——光线、温度、声音、雾气的形成与消散——都变成了他们的伴侣。今天我坐在角落，看着一个年轻女孩在雾气上画了一只猫，然后用手掌一擦，猫就消失了。她又画了一只，又擦掉。反复了五六次，每次画的猫都不一样。我忽然想，也许她不是在画猫，而是在练习告别。每一次擦掉都是一次小小的放手，每一次重画都是一次重新开始。
text_pinyin: Dōngtiān de kāfēiguǎn lǐ, kào chuāng de zuòwèi zǒng shì zuì xiān bèi zhàn mǎn. Bú shì yīnwèi fēngjǐng hǎo——wàimiàn huīméngméng de, shù yě tū le——ér shì yīnwèi bōli shàng huì qǐ yī céng wùqì. Yǒu rén yòng shǒuzhǐ zài shàngmian huà huà, yǒu rén xiě zì, gèng duō de rén zhǐshì kàn zhe nà céng wù mànmàn níng chéng shuǐzhū, shùn zhe bōli huá xiàlái. Wǒ guānchá guò hěn duō cì, fāxiàn yī gè guīlǜ: dúzì lái de rén gèng róngyì zhùyì dào wùqì, ér jiébàn lái de rén jīhū bú huì kàn chuānghu. Zhè dàgài shuōmíng le shénme. Gūdú de rén duì huánjìng de gǎnzhī gèng mǐnruì, yīnwèi tāmen méiyǒu duìhuà lái tiánmǎn zhùyìlì. Yúshì zhōuwéi de yīqiè——guāngxiàn, wēndù, shēngyīn, wùqì de xíngchéng yǔ xiāosàn——dōu biànchéng le tāmen de bànlǚ. Jīntiān wǒ zuò zài jiǎoluò, kàn zhe yī gè niánqīng nǚhái zài wùqì shàng huà le yī zhī māo, ránhòu yòng shǒuzhǎng yī cā, māo jiù xiāoshī le. Tā yòu huà le yī zhī, yòu cā diào. Fǎnfù le wǔ liù cì, měi cì huà de māo dōu bù yīyàng. Wǒ hūrán xiǎng, yěxǔ tā bú shì zài huà māo, ér shì zài liànxí gàobié. Měi yī cì cā diào dōu shì yī cì xiǎo xiǎo de fàngshǒu, měi yī cì chóng huà dōu shì yī cì chóngxīn kāishǐ. Wùqì zhōngjiù huì sàn qù, dàn tā liànxí guò de nà zhǒng cóngróng, yěxǔ huì liú zài shǒuzhǐ de jīròu jìyì lǐ.
text_en: In winter cafés, the window seats are always claimed first. Not for the view—it's gray outside and the trees are bare—but because a layer of fog forms on the glass. Some trace drawings with their fingers, some write words, but most just watch the mist slowly bead into droplets that slide down the pane. I've observed this many times and noticed a pattern: people who come alone are more likely to notice the fog, while those who come with companions almost never look at the window. This probably says something. Solitary people perceive their surroundings more keenly, because they have no conversation to fill their attention. So everything around them—light, temperature, sound, the forming and fading of mist—becomes their companion. Today I sat in the corner watching a young woman draw a cat on the fogged glass, then wipe it away with her palm. She drew another, wiped it again. Five or six times, each cat different from the last. It suddenly occurred to me that perhaps she wasn't drawing cats—she was practicing goodbye. Each erasure was a small letting go, each redrawing a fresh start. The fog will eventually clear, but the composure she practiced may linger in the muscle memory of her fingers.

title: The Sound of Rain on Different Roofs
title_zh: 雨落在不同屋顶的声音
text_zh: 我在不同的城市住过很多种房子，发现雨打在不同材质的屋顶上，声音完全不一样。瓦片屋顶是清脆的，像有人在轻轻敲一排排小碗。铁皮屋顶是急促而密集的，像一万颗小石子同时落下来，带着金属的回响。混凝土屋顶几乎没有声音，雨水被吸收了，只有很大的雨才能听到沉闷的嗡嗡声。木头屋顶介于瓦片和混凝土之间，温和、低沉，像远处有人在打鼓。我最喜欢的是老家那种瓦片屋顶。小时候下雨的午后，我躺在床上听雨声，能分辨出雨的大小、方向甚至温度。冷雨打在瓦上更脆，暖雨更沉。风从东边来的时候，东边的瓦先响；风转了方向，声音的重心也跟着移动。这就像一种天然的环绕声系统，不需要任何设备，只需要一排老瓦片和一场恰到好处的雨。后来住进城市的高层公寓，我再也听不到雨声了。窗户是双层隔音的，空调的嗡嗡声代替了一切。有时候我会打开一个播放雨声的应用程序，但怎么听都觉得差了什么。差的大概不是声音本身，而是那种「此刻正在下雨，而我恰好在听」的即时感。
text_pinyin: Wǒ zài bùtóng de chéngshì zhù guò hěn duō zhǒng fángzi, fāxiàn yǔ dǎ zài bùtóng cáizhì de wūdǐng shàng, shēngyīn wánquán bù yīyàng. Wǎpiàn wūdǐng shì qīngcuì de, xiàng yǒu rén zài qīngqīng qiāo yī pái pái xiǎo wǎn. Tiěpí wūdǐng shì jícù ér mìjí de, xiàng yī wàn kē xiǎo shízǐ tóngshí luò xiàlái, dài zhe jīnshǔ de huíxiǎng. Hùnníngtǔ wūdǐng jīhū méiyǒu shēngyīn, yǔshuǐ bèi xīshōu le, zhǐyǒu hěn dà de yǔ cái néng tīng dào chénmèn de wēngwēng shēng. Mùtou wūdǐng jiè yú wǎpiàn hé hùnníngtǔ zhī jiān, wēnhé, dīchén, xiàng yuǎnchù yǒu rén zài dǎ gǔ. Wǒ zuì xǐhuan de shì lǎojiā nà zhǒng wǎpiàn wūdǐng. Xiǎoshíhou xià yǔ de wǔhòu, wǒ tǎng zài chuáng shàng tīng yǔshēng, néng fēnbiàn chū yǔ de dàxiǎo, fāngxiàng shènzhì wēndù. Lěng yǔ dǎ zài wǎ shàng gèng cuì, nuǎn yǔ gèng chén. Fēng cóng dōngbiān lái de shíhou, dōngbiān de wǎ xiān xiǎng; fēng zhuǎn le fāngxiàng, shēngyīn de zhòngxīn yě gēn zhe yídòng. Zhè jiù xiàng yī zhǒng tiānrán de huánrào shēng xìtǒng, bù xūyào rènhé shèbèi, zhǐ xūyào yī pái lǎo wǎpiàn hé yī chǎng qiàdàohǎochù de yǔ. Hòulái zhù jìn chéngshì de gāocéng gōngyù, wǒ zài yě tīng bú dào yǔshēng le. Chuānghu shì shuāngcéng géyīn de, kōngtiáo de wēngwēng shēng dàitì le yīqiè. Yǒu shíhou wǒ huì dǎkāi yī gè bōfàng yǔshēng de yìngyòng chéngxù, dàn zěnme tīng dōu juéde chà le shénme. Chà de dàgài bú shì shēngyīn běnshēn, ér shì nà zhǒng「cǐkè zhèngzài xià yǔ, ér wǒ qiàhǎo zài tīng」de jíshí gǎn.
text_en: I've lived in many kinds of houses across different cities and discovered that rain sounds completely different on different roofing materials. Tile roofs are crisp, like someone gently tapping rows of small bowls. Tin roofs are rapid and dense, like ten thousand pebbles falling at once with a metallic echo. Concrete roofs are nearly silent—the water is absorbed, and only heavy rain produces a muffled hum. Wooden roofs fall between tile and concrete: mild, low, like distant drumming. My favorite was the tile roof of my hometown home. On rainy afternoons as a child, I'd lie in bed listening to the rain and could distinguish its intensity, direction, even temperature. Cold rain on tiles sounds crisper; warm rain, deeper. When wind came from the east, the eastern tiles sounded first; when the wind shifted, the center of sound shifted with it. It was like a natural surround-sound system, requiring no equipment—just a row of old tiles and a well-timed rain. Later I moved into a high-rise apartment in the city and could no longer hear the rain. The windows are double-paned for insulation; the hum of air conditioning replaced everything. Sometimes I open a rain-sound app, but something always feels missing. What's missing probably isn't the sound itself, but the immediacy of 'it is raining right now, and I happen to be listening.'

questions.q_zh: 作者认为雨声应用程序缺少的是什么？
questions.q_en: What does the author think the rain-sound app is missing?

options.pinyin: yīnzhì bú gòu hǎo
options.text_en: The sound quality isn't good enough

options.text: 「此刻正在下雨而我恰好在听」的即时感
options.pinyin: 「cǐkè zhèngzài xià yǔ ér wǒ qiàhǎo zài tīng」de jíshí gǎn
options.text_en: The immediacy of 'it's raining right now and I happen to be listening'

options.pinyin: yǔshēng de zhǒnglèi bú gòu duō
options.text_en: Not enough varieties of rain sounds

questions.explanation: Rain-sound apps reproduce the audio but miss the key ingredient: 'it's raining right now and I happen to be listening'—presence and coincidence can't be recorded.

questions.q_zh: 根据文章，冷雨和暖雨打在瓦片上的声音有什么不同？
questions.q_en: According to the passage, how do cold rain and warm rain sound different on tiles?

options.pinyin: lěng yǔ gèng cuì, nuǎn yǔ gèng chén
options.text_en: Cold rain is crisper, warm rain is deeper

options.text: 冷雨更大声，暖雨更安静
options.pinyin: lěng yǔ gèng dà shēng, nuǎn yǔ gèng ānjìng
options.text_en: Cold rain is louder, warm rain is quieter

options.text: 冷雨节奏更快，暖雨节奏更慢
options.pinyin: lěng yǔ jiézòu gèng kuài, nuǎn yǔ jiézòu gèng màn
options.text_en: Cold rain has faster rhythm, warm rain slower

questions.explanation: The passage distinguishes rain by temperature: cold rain strikes tiles with a crisper, sharper sound; warm rain produces a deeper, softer resonance.

title: The Last Customer
text_zh: 我常去的那家面馆每天晚上十点关门。但我发现，真正决定关门时间的不是时钟，而是最后一位客人。如果九点半还没人来，老板就开始收拾，擦桌子，把调料瓶一个个盖好，椅子倒扣在桌面上。但如果九点五十来了一个人，他会重新把灶台打开，像没有任何不耐烦地煮一碗面。我问过他为什么不严格按时间关门。他说：「人家大晚上的还要出来吃面，要么是加班太晚，要么是心里不太舒服，怎么都是需要一碗热面的人。我早关十分钟，对我来说只是早睡十分钟；但对那个人来说，可能就是在一个疲惫的夜晚被拒绝了一次。」这番话我记了很久。后来我注意到，很多那种在打烊前最后十分钟进来的客人，都不太说话。他们安静地坐下，安静地吃完，有时候抬头看看老板在忙什么，眼神里带着一种说不清的感激。不是那种需要表达的感激，是那种「你还在，真好」的感激。我想，这大概就是一间好店铺和一间普通店铺的区别。好的店铺卖的不只是食物，还有一种「来了就不会被拒绝」的承诺。
text_pinyin: Wǒ cháng qù de nà jiā miànguǎn měi tiān wǎnshang shí diǎn guānmén. Dàn wǒ fāxiàn, zhēnzhèng juédìng guānmén shíjiān de bú shì shízhōng, ér shì zuìhòu yī wèi kèrén. Rúguǒ jiǔ diǎn bàn hái méi rén lái, lǎobǎn jiù kāishǐ shōushi, cā zhuōzi, bǎ tiáoliào píng yī gè gè gài hǎo, yǐzi dào kòu zài zhuōmiàn shàng. Dàn rúguǒ jiǔ diǎn wǔshí lái le yī gè rén, tā huì chóngxīn bǎ zàotái dǎkāi, xiàng méiyǒu rènhé bù nàifán de zhǔ yī wǎn miàn. Wǒ wèn guò tā wèishéme bù yángé àn shíjiān guānmén. Tā shuō: 「Rénjia dà wǎnshang de hái yào chūlái chī miàn, yàome shì jiābān tài wǎn, yàome shì xīnlǐ bú tài shūfu, zěnme dōu shì xūyào yī wǎn rè miàn de rén. Wǒ zǎo guān shí fēnzhōng, duì wǒ lái shuō zhǐshì zǎo shuì shí fēnzhōng; dàn duì nàge rén lái shuō, kěnéng jiùshì zài yī gè píbèi de yèwǎn bèi jùjué le yī cì.」Zhè fān huà wǒ jì le hěn jiǔ. Hòulái wǒ zhùyì dào, hěn duō nà zhǒng zài dǎyáng qián zuìhòu shí fēnzhōng jìnlái de kèrén, dōu bú tài shuōhuà. Tāmen ānjìng de zuò xià, ānjìng de chī wán, yǒu shíhou tái tóu kàn kàn lǎobǎn zài máng shénme, yǎnshén lǐ dài zhe yī zhǒng shuō bù qīng de gǎnjī. Bú shì nà zhǒng xūyào biǎodá de gǎnjī, shì nà zhǒng「nǐ hái zài, zhēn hǎo」de gǎnjī. Wǒ xiǎng, zhè dàgài jiùshì yī jiān hǎo diànpù hé yī jiān pǔtōng diànpù de qūbié. Hǎo de diànpù mài de bù zhǐshì shíwù, hái yǒu yī zhǒng「lái le jiù bú huì bèi jùjué」de chéngnuò.
text_en: The noodle shop I frequent closes at ten every evening. But I've noticed that what truly determines closing time isn't the clock—it's the last customer. If no one comes by nine-thirty, the owner starts cleaning up: wiping tables, capping condiment bottles one by one, flipping chairs upside down on the tabletops. But if someone walks in at nine-fifty, he'll relight the stove and cook a bowl of noodles without a trace of impatience. I once asked him why he doesn't close strictly on time. He said: 'Someone coming out this late for noodles is either working overtime too late or feeling uneasy inside—either way, they're someone who needs a hot bowl of noodles. Closing ten minutes early only means I sleep ten minutes earlier; but for that person, it might mean being turned away on an exhausting night.' I remembered those words for a long time. Later I noticed that many customers who come in during the last ten minutes before closing barely speak. They sit down quietly, eat quietly, sometimes glancing up to see what the owner is doing, with a kind of ineffable gratitude in their eyes. Not the kind of gratitude that needs expressing, but the kind that says 'you're still here—that's good.' I think this is probably the difference between a good shop and an ordinary one. A good shop sells not just food, but a promise: 'come in, and you won't be turned away.'

questions.q_zh: 老板为什么不严格按时间关门？
questions.q_en: Why doesn't the owner close strictly on time?

options.pinyin: tā xiǎng duō zhuàn diǎn qián
options.text_en: He wants to earn more money

options.text: 他觉得晚来的人更需要一碗热面，不想拒绝他们
options.pinyin: tā juéde wǎn lái de rén gèng xūyào yī wǎn rè miàn, bù xiǎng jùjué tāmen
options.text_en: He feels late customers need a hot bowl of noodles more and doesn't want to refuse them

options.pinyin: tā zìjǐ yě shuì de hěn wǎn
options.text_en: He stays up late himself anyway

questions.explanation: The owner feels late arrivals need a hot meal more urgently than others and can't bring himself to turn them away at closing time.

questions.q_zh: 文章最后说「好的店铺卖的不只是食物」，还卖什么？
questions.q_en: A good shop sells more than food—what else does it sell?

options.pinyin: yōuzhì de fúwù tàidu
options.text_en: Quality service attitude

options.text: 一种「来了就不会被拒绝」的承诺
options.pinyin: yī zhǒng「lái le jiù bú huì bèi jùjué」de chéngnuò
options.text_en: A promise that 'you won't be turned away once you come'

options.pinyin: wēnnuǎn de yòngcān huánjìng
options.text_en: A warm dining environment

questions.explanation: Beyond food, the shop sells a promise: 'you won't be turned away once you come.' Reliability and welcome, not just noodles.

title: Shadows of Bicycles
text_zh: 下午三点左右的阳光最适合看影子。太阳不高不低，影子被拉长但不至于变形，每个细节都清晰可辨。我住的那条巷子里停着很多自行车，下午的时候，地上会出现一排影子，车轮、车把、车筐都投射在灰白色的水泥地上。我注意到一件有意思的事情：自行车的影子比自行车本身更好看。因为影子把三维的东西压缩成了二维，去掉了锈迹、划痕、掉漆这些岁月留下的痕迹，只保留了最纯粹的轮廓。一辆老旧的自行车，影子看起来跟新的一样。这让我想到一个关于记忆的问题。我们对一个人的记忆，是不是也像影子一样？时间会慢慢抹去细节——他说过的蠢话、他偶尔的自私、他让你失望的那些时刻——最终只留下一个干净的轮廓：他对你笑的样子，他递给你一杯水的动作，他离开时回头看了你一眼。也许遗忘不是一种损失，而是一种精炼。就像阳光只投射出物体的形状，遗忘只保留了关系的本质。那些自行车每天承受着风吹日晒，但它们的影子永远是新的。
text_pinyin: Xiàwǔ sān diǎn zuǒyòu de yángguāng zuì shìhé kàn yǐngzi. Tàiyáng bù gāo bù dī, yǐngzi bèi lā cháng dàn bú zhìyú biànxíng, měi gè xìjié dōu qīngxī kě biàn. Wǒ zhù de nà tiáo xiàngzi lǐ tíng zhe hěn duō zìxíngchē, xiàwǔ de shíhou, dì shàng huì chūxiàn yī pái yǐngzi, chēlún, chēbǎ, chēkuāng dōu tóushè zài huībái sè de shuǐní dì shàng. Wǒ zhùyì dào yī jiàn yǒu yìsi de shìqing: zìxíngchē de yǐngzi bǐ zìxíngchē běnshēn gèng hǎokàn. Yīnwèi yǐngzi bǎ sān wéi de dōngxi yāsuō chéng le èr wéi, qùdiào le xiùjì, huáhén, diào qī zhèxiē suìyuè liú xià de hénjì, zhǐ bǎoliú le zuì chúncuì de lúnkuò. Yī liàng lǎojiù de zìxíngchē, yǐngzi kàn qǐlái gēn xīn de yīyàng. Zhè ràng wǒ xiǎng dào yī gè guānyú jìyì de wèntí. Wǒmen duì yī gè rén de jìyì, shì bu shì yě xiàng yǐngzi yīyàng? Shíjiān huì mànmàn mǒ qù xìjié——tā shuō guò de chǔn huà, tā ǒu'ěr de zìsī, tā ràng nǐ shīwàng de nàxiē shíkè——zuìzhōng zhǐ liú xià yī gè gānjìng de lúnkuò: tā duì nǐ xiào de yàngzi, tā dì gěi nǐ yī bēi shuǐ de dòngzuò, tā líkāi shí huí tóu kàn le nǐ yī yǎn. Yěxǔ yíwàng bú shì yī zhǒng sǔnshī, ér shì yī zhǒng jīngliàn. Jiù xiàng yángguāng zhǐ tóushè chū wùtǐ de xíngzhuàng, yíwàng zhǐ bǎoliú le guānxi de běnzhì. Nàxiē zìxíngchē měi tiān chéngshòu zhe fēngchuī rìshài, dàn tāmen de yǐngzi yǒngyuǎn shì xīn de.
text_en: The sunlight around three in the afternoon is best for watching shadows. The sun sits neither too high nor too low; shadows are stretched but not distorted, every detail sharply defined. Many bicycles are parked along my alley, and in the afternoon a row of shadows appears on the ground—wheels, handlebars, baskets all projected onto the pale gray cement. I noticed something interesting: the shadows of bicycles look better than the bicycles themselves. Shadows compress three dimensions into two, stripping away rust, scratches, chipped paint—all the marks of passing years—leaving only the purest outline. An old, worn bicycle casts a shadow that looks brand new. This made me think about memory. Are our memories of a person like shadows too? Time gradually erases the details—the foolish things they said, their occasional selfishness, the moments they disappointed you—until only a clean silhouette remains: the way they smiled at you, the gesture of handing you a glass of water, the backward glance as they left. Perhaps forgetting isn't a loss but a refinement. Just as sunlight projects only an object's shape, forgetting preserves only the essence of a relationship. Those bicycles endure wind and sun every day, but their shadows are forever new.

questions.q_zh: 作者为什么觉得自行车的影子比自行车本身更好看？
questions.q_en: Why does the author think bicycle shadows look better than the bicycles themselves?

options.text: 影子去掉了锈迹和划痕，只保留了最纯粹的轮廓
options.pinyin: yǐngzi qùdiào le xiùjì hé huáhén, zhǐ bǎoliú le zuì chúncuì de lúnkuò
options.text_en: Shadows remove rust and scratches, keeping only the purest outline

options.pinyin: yǐngzi de yánsè gèng hǎokàn
options.text_en: The color of shadows is more attractive

options.pinyin: yǐngzi bǐ zìxíngchē gèng dà
options.text_en: Shadows are larger than bicycles

questions.explanation: Shadows strip away rust, scratches, and wear, keeping only the geometric outline—an idealized version that the physical object can't match.

questions.q_zh: 作者认为遗忘和影子有什么共同点？
questions.q_en: What do the author and shadows have in common according to the passage?

options.pinyin: liǎng zhě dōu shì yī zhǒng sǔnshī
options.text_en: Both are a form of loss

options.text: 两者都是一种精炼——去掉细节，保留本质
options.pinyin: liǎng zhě dōu shì yī zhǒng jīngliàn——qùdiào xìjié, bǎoliú běnzhì
options.text_en: Both are a refinement—removing detail, preserving essence

options.pinyin: liǎng zhě dōu huì suí shíjiān biànhuà
options.text_en: Both change with time

questions.explanation: Both the author's writing and shadows perform the same operation: removing clutter and detail to preserve essential form. Refinement through subtraction.

title: The Thermometer Outside the Window
text_zh: 楼下王阿姨的窗台上挂着一个老式温度计，红色酒精柱的那种。每天早上她都要看一眼，然后决定今天穿什么。手机上也能看气温，但她不信。她说：「手机上的温度是气象局测的，离我家好几公里远。我窗台上的温度才是我真正要面对的温度。」我一开始觉得这话没什么道理，后来想想，其实很有道理。气象数据是一种平均值，一种抽象。它告诉你这个城市的温度，但不是你阳台上的温度，不是你出门拐弯处那阵穿堂风的温度，不是你走到地铁站那段上坡路上因为运动而感受到的温度。真正的体感温度是非常个人的，跟你的体质、心情、睡眠质量、早餐吃了什么都有关系。王阿姨的温度计虽然可能不如气象站的精确，但它测的是「她的温度」——她所站的那个位置、她的朝向、她的楼层高度。这是一种拒绝被数据代表的坚持。我们的生活越来越被各种平均值包围：平均房价、平均薪资、平均寿命。但没有人活在平均值里。每个人都活在自己窗台上那个温度计的读数里。
text_pinyin: Lóu xià Wáng āyí de chuāngtái shàng guà zhe yī gè lǎoshì wēndùjì, hóngsè jiǔjīng zhù de nà zhǒng. Měi tiān zǎoshang tā dōu yào kàn yī yǎn, ránhòu juédìng jīntiān chuān shénme. Shǒujī shàng yě néng kàn qìwēn, dàn tā bú xìn. Tā shuō: 「Shǒujī shàng de wēndù shì qìxiàngjú cè de, lí wǒ jiā hǎo jǐ gōnglǐ yuǎn. Wǒ chuāngtái shàng de wēndù cái shì wǒ zhēnzhèng yào miànduì de wēndù.」Wǒ yī kāishǐ juéde zhè huà méi shénme dàolǐ, hòulái xiǎng xiǎng, qíshí hěn yǒu dàolǐ. Qìxiàng shùjù shì yī zhǒng píngjūn zhí, yī zhǒng chōuxiàng. Tā gàosu nǐ zhège chéngshì de wēndù, dàn bú shì nǐ yángtái shàng de wēndù, bú shì nǐ chūmén guǎi wān chù nà zhèn chuāntáng fēng de wēndù, bú shì nǐ zǒu dào dìtiě zhàn nà duàn shàng pō lù shàng yīnwèi yùndòng ér gǎnshòu dào de wēndù. Zhēnzhèng de tǐgǎn wēndù shì fēicháng gèrén de, gēn nǐ de tǐzhì, xīnqíng, shuìmián zhìliàng, zǎocān chī le shénme dōu yǒu guānxi. Wáng āyí de wēndùjì suīrán kěnéng bùrú qìxiàng zhàn de jīngquè, dàn tā cè de shì「tā de wēndù」——tā suǒ zhàn de nàge wèizhì, tā de cháoxiàng, tā de lóucéng gāodù. Zhè shì yī zhǒng jùjué bèi shùjù dàibiǎo de jiānchí. Wǒmen de shēnghuó yuè lái yuè bèi gè zhǒng píngjūn zhí bāowéi: píngjūn fángjià, píngjūn xīnzī, píngjūn shòumìng. Dàn méiyǒu rén huó zài píngjūn zhí lǐ. Měi gè rén dōu huó zài zìjǐ chuāngtái shàng nàge wēndùjì de dúshù lǐ.
text_en: Auntie Wang downstairs has an old-fashioned thermometer on her windowsill—the kind with a red alcohol column. Every morning she checks it, then decides what to wear. She could check the temperature on her phone, but she doesn't trust it. She says: 'The phone temperature is measured by the weather bureau, several kilometers from my home. The temperature on my windowsill is the temperature I actually have to face.' At first I thought this made no sense, but thinking more, it makes a lot of sense. Meteorological data is an average, an abstraction. It tells you the city's temperature, but not your balcony's temperature, not the temperature of that cross-draft at the corner when you step out, not the temperature you feel walking uphill to the subway station because of exertion. True perceived temperature is deeply personal—related to your constitution, mood, sleep quality, what you ate for breakfast. Auntie Wang's thermometer may not be as precise as the weather station's, but it measures 'her temperature'—the position where she stands, her building's orientation, her floor height. This is a kind of insistence on not being represented by data. Our lives are increasingly surrounded by averages: average housing prices, average salaries, average lifespans. But nobody lives inside an average. Everyone lives in the reading on the thermometer on their own windowsill.

options.text_en: She thinks phone temperatures are often wrong

questions.q_en: What is the core idea at the end of the passage?

options.text: 气象数据不准确，不值得信任
options.pinyin: qìxiàng shùjù bù zhǔnquè, bù zhíde xìnrèn
options.text_en: Weather data is inaccurate and untrustworthy

title: The Way Old People Sit
text_zh: 公园里有一排长椅，每天下午都坐满了老人。我路过的时候仔细看过，发现每个人坐着的姿势都不一样，而且这些姿势似乎透露着各自不同的人生态度。有的老人坐得很直，双手放在膝盖上，像是随时准备站起来去做什么事。这种人大概一辈子都很忙碌，即使退休了也闲不下来。有的老人身体往后靠，双腿伸得很长，脸朝着太阳，眯着眼睛，像一只晒太阳的猫。这种人应该是终于学会了什么都不做的艺术。还有一种我特别喜欢观察的：微微前倾，两只手交叉放在拐杖顶端，下巴搁在手背上，眼睛半闭半睁地看着前方。这种姿势兼具了放松和警觉，好像在说：「我不需要再去做什么了，但我还在看着这个世界。」他们看的不是具体的什么——不是走过的行人，不是飞过的鸟，不是远处的楼——他们看的是时间本身在流动。那种目光是超越了具体对象的，它穿过公园，穿过树木，落在某个不属于任何空间的地方。年轻人是做不到这种目光的，因为年轻人的眼睛总是在寻找什么。只有活够了、看够了的人，才能学会不寻找地看。
text_pinyin: Gōngyuán lǐ yǒu yī pái chángyǐ, měi tiān xiàwǔ dōu zuò mǎn le lǎorén. Wǒ lùguò de shíhou zǐxì kàn guò, fāxiàn měi gè rén zuò zhe de zīshì dōu bù yīyàng, érqiě zhèxiē zīshì sìhū tòulù zhe gèzì bùtóng de rénshēng tàidu. Yǒu de lǎorén zuò de hěn zhí, shuāng shǒu fàng zài xīgài shàng, xiàng shì suíshí zhǔnbèi zhàn qǐlái qù zuò shénme shì. Zhè zhǒng rén dàgài yī bèizi dōu hěn mánglù, jíshǐ tuìxiū le yě xián bú xiàlái. Yǒu de lǎorén shēntǐ wǎng hòu kào, shuāng tuǐ shēn de hěn cháng, liǎn cháo zhe tàiyáng, mī zhe yǎnjīng, xiàng yī zhī shài tàiyáng de māo. Zhè zhǒng rén yīnggāi shì zhōngyú xuéhuì le shénme dōu bú zuò de yìshù. Hái yǒu yī zhǒng wǒ tèbié xǐhuan guānchá de: wēiwēi qián qīng, liǎng zhī shǒu jiāochā fàng zài guǎizhàng dǐngduān, xiàba gē zài shǒubèi shàng, yǎnjīng bàn bì bàn zhēng de kàn zhe qiánfāng. Zhè zhǒng zīshì jiānjù le fàngsōng hé jǐngjué, hǎoxiàng zài shuō: 「Wǒ bù xūyào zài qù zuò shénme le, dàn wǒ hái zài kàn zhe zhège shìjiè.」Tāmen kàn de bú shì jùtǐ de shénme——bú shì zǒu guò de xíngrén, bú shì fēi guò de niǎo, bú shì yuǎnchù de lóu——tāmen kàn de shì shíjiān běnshēn zài liúdòng. Nà zhǒng mùguāng shì chāoyuè le jùtǐ duìxiàng de, tā chuān guò gōngyuán, chuān guò shùmù, luò zài mǒu gè bù shǔyú rènhé kōngjiān de dìfāng. Niánqīng rén shì zuò bú dào zhè zhǒng mùguāng de, yīnwèi niánqīng rén de yǎnjīng zǒng shì zài xúnzhǎo shénme. Zhǐyǒu huó gòu le, kàn gòu le de rén, cái néng xuéhuì bù xúnzhǎo de kàn.
text_en: There's a row of benches in the park, filled with elderly people every afternoon. Passing by and looking carefully, I noticed everyone sits differently, and these postures seem to reveal distinct life philosophies. Some sit bolt upright, hands on knees, as if ready to stand and do something at any moment. These people were probably busy their whole lives—even retired, they can't sit still. Others lean back, legs stretched long, faces toward the sun, eyes squinting like a cat basking in warmth. They've probably finally mastered the art of doing nothing. Then there's one posture I especially like to observe: leaning slightly forward, hands crossed atop a cane, chin resting on the back of the hands, eyes half-closed gazing ahead. This posture blends relaxation with alertness, as if saying: 'I don't need to do anything anymore, but I'm still watching the world.' They're not watching anything specific—not the passersby, not the birds overhead, not the buildings in the distance—they're watching time itself flow. That gaze transcends particular objects; it passes through the park, through the trees, and lands somewhere that belongs to no space at all.

questions.explanation: Young eyes constantly scan and search for the next thing. Old eyes have learned to look without seeking—a gaze that receives rather than hunts.

title: The Smell of an Old Bookstore
text_zh: 离我家不远有一间旧书店，走进去的第一秒，你会被一种气味包裹住。那是纸张、灰尘、木头书架和微弱的霉气混合在一起的味道。很多人会觉得不好闻，但我觉得那是一种非常诚实的气味。它在告诉你：这里的东西已经被时间处理过了。新书店是没有气味的，或者说，它的气味是被设计过的——咖啡、香薰、新印的油墨。这些气味都在说「请留下来」，是一种商业策略。旧书店的气味不取悦你，它只是存在。你爱来就来，不来也无所谓。这种不刻意讨好的态度反而让人放松。我在那里翻过一本1987年出版的散文集，书页已经泛黄，边缘有些脆了。翻开某一页，发现有人用铅笔在一句话下面画了一条线：「所有的相遇都是重逢。」我不知道画线的人是谁，也不知道他在什么样的心境下觉得这句话值得标注。但我知道，我此刻翻到这一页，看到这条线，构成了一种跨越时间的对话。他在三十多年前读到这句话的时候停了下来，我在今天读到它的时候也停了下来。我们被同一句话打动了，虽然隔着几十年。旧书店里最珍贵的不是那些书，而是书上留下的这些痕迹——它们是匿名的人际关系。
text_pinyin: Lí wǒ jiā bù yuǎn yǒu yī jiān jiù shūdiàn, zǒu jìnqù de dì yī miǎo, nǐ huì bèi yī zhǒng qìwèi bāoguǒ zhù. Nà shì zhǐzhāng, huīchén, mùtou shūjià hé wēiruò de méi qì hùnhé zài yīqǐ de wèidào. Hěn duō rén huì juéde bù hǎo wén, dàn wǒ juéde nà shì yī zhǒng fēicháng chéngshí de qìwèi. Tā zài gàosu nǐ: zhèlǐ de dōngxi yǐjīng bèi shíjiān chǔlǐ guò le. Xīn shūdiàn shì méiyǒu qìwèi de, huòzhě shuō, tā de qìwèi shì bèi shèjì guò de——kāfēi, xiāngxūn, xīn yìn de yóumò. Zhèxiē qìwèi dōu zài shuō「qǐng liú xiàlái」, shì yī zhǒng shāngyè cèlüè. Jiù shūdiàn de qìwèi bù qǔyuè nǐ, tā zhǐshì cúnzài. Nǐ ài lái jiù lái, bù lái yě wúsuǒwèi. Zhè zhǒng bú kèyì tǎohǎo de tàidu fǎn'ér ràng rén fàngsōng. Wǒ zài nàlǐ fān guò yī běn yī jiǔ bā qī nián chūbǎn de sǎnwénjí, shūyè yǐjīng fàn huáng, biānyuán yǒuxiē cuì le. Fānkāi mǒu yī yè, fāxiàn yǒu rén yòng qiānbǐ zài yī jù huà xiàmian huà le yī tiáo xiàn: 「Suǒyǒu de xiāngyù dōu shì chóngféng.」Wǒ bù zhīdào huà xiàn de rén shì shéi, yě bù zhīdào tā zài shénme yàng de xīnjìng xià juéde zhè jù huà zhíde biāozhù. Dàn wǒ zhīdào, wǒ cǐkè fān dào zhè yī yè, kàn dào zhè tiáo xiàn, gòuchéng le yī zhǒng kuàyuè shíjiān de duìhuà. Tā zài sānshí duō nián qián dú dào zhè jù huà de shíhou tíng le xiàlái, wǒ zài jīntiān dú dào tā de shíhou yě tíng le xiàlái. Wǒmen bèi tóng yī jù huà dǎdòng le, suīrán gé zhe jǐ shí nián. Jiù shūdiàn lǐ zuì zhēnguì de bú shì nàxiē shū, ér shì shū shàng liú xià de zhèxiē hénjì——tāmen shì nìmíng de rénjì guānxi.
text_en: Not far from my home is an old bookstore. The first second you step in, a smell envelops you. It's a blend of paper, dust, wooden shelves, and faint mildew. Many find it unpleasant, but I think it's a very honest scent. It's telling you: the things here have been processed by time. New bookstores are odorless—or rather, their scent is designed: coffee, aromatherapy, fresh ink. These smells all say 'please stay,' a commercial strategy. The old bookstore's smell doesn't try to please you; it simply exists. Come if you like, or don't—it makes no difference. This attitude of not deliberately trying to charm you is actually relaxing. I once leafed through an essay collection published in 1987, its pages yellowed and edges grown brittle. On one page, someone had drawn a pencil line under a sentence: 'All encounters are reunions.' I don't know who drew that line or what state of mind made them feel the sentence was worth marking. But I know that my turning to this page now and seeing that line creates a conversation across time. They stopped at this sentence over thirty years ago; I stopped at it today. We were both moved by the same words, though separated by decades.

questions.q_zh: 作者在旧书上看到画线的句子后，有什么感悟？

options.text_en: He wanted to find the person who drew the line
