title: Learning to Wait
text_zh: 我以前不会等待。排队焦虑，等人烦躁，等外卖的时候每三十秒刷新一次进度条。后来我注意到我的邻居老赵。老赵是退休木匠，唯一的爱好是钓鱼。有一次我看他钓了一下午，一条鱼都没钓到。我问他急不急。他头也不回地说：「急什么？鱼又没有上班要迟到。」他跟我解释，做木匠的时候刷了漆要等它干，胶了榫头要等它固，急是没有用的。我说我理解道理，但做不到。他沉默了一会儿说：「你等外卖的时候做什么？」我说刷手机。他说：「那你不是在等待，你是在逃避等待。真正的等待是什么都不干，就坐着。」我试了一次。等红灯的时候什么都不干，就站着。我坚持了大约十一秒就拿出了手机。第二次坚持了十八秒。第三次我忍到了绿灯亮，感觉像完成了一次马拉松。后来我告诉老赵我的进步，他正在收拾鱼竿——还是一条没钓到。他说：「不错，我练了三十年才做到的事你一周就会了。」我说：「那您钓鱼三十年钓到过几条鱼？」他认真想了想：「加起来的话……大概够吃一顿火锅。」他把空桶往肩上一扛，走了。我站在河边看着他的背影，突然觉得等待本身已经是他的全部收获了。鱼只是借口。
text_pinyin: Wǒ yǐqián bú huì děngdài. Páiduì jiāolǜ, děng rén fánzào, děng wàimài de shíhou měi sānshí miǎo shuāxīn yí cì jìndùtiáo. Hòulái wǒ zhùyì dào wǒ de línjū Lǎo Zhào. Lǎo Zhào shì tuìxiū mùjiang, wéiyī de àihào shì diàoyú. Yǒu yí cì wǒ kàn tā diào le yí ge xiàwǔ, yì tiáo yú dōu méi diào dào. Wǒ wèn tā jí bù jí. Tā tóu yě bù huí de shuō: "Jí shénme? Yú yòu méiyǒu shàngbān yào chídào." Tā gēn wǒ jiěshì, zuò mùjiang de shíhou shuā le qī yào děng tā gān, jiāo le sǔntou yào děng tā gù, jí shì méiyǒu yòng de. Wǒ shuō wǒ lǐjiě dàoli, dàn zuò bú dào. Tā chénmò le yíhuìr shuō: "Nǐ děng wàimài de shíhou zuò shénme?" Wǒ shuō shuā shǒujī. Tā shuō: "Nà nǐ bú shì zài děngdài, nǐ shì zài táobì děngdài. Zhēnzhèng de děngdài shì shénme dōu bù gàn, jiù zuò zhe." Wǒ shì le yí cì. Děng hóngdēng de shíhou shénme dōu bù gàn, jiù zhàn zhe. Wǒ jiānchí le dàyuē shíyī miǎo jiù ná chū le shǒujī. Dì èr cì jiānchí le shíbā miǎo. Dì sān cì wǒ rěn dào le lǜdēng liàng, gǎnjué xiàng wánchéng le yí cì mǎlāsōng. Hòulái wǒ gàosu Lǎo Zhào wǒ de jìnbù, tā zhèngzài shōushi yúgān — háishi yì tiáo méi diào dào. Tā shuō: "Búcuò, wǒ liàn le sānshí nián cái zuò dào de shì nǐ yì zhōu jiù huì le." Wǒ shuō: "Nà nín diàoyú sānshí nián diào dào guò jǐ tiáo yú?" Tā rènzhēn xiǎng le xiǎng: "Jiā qǐlái dehuà...... dàgài gòu chī yí dùn huǒguō." Tā bǎ kōng tǒng wǎng jiān shàng yì káng, zǒu le. Wǒ zhàn zài hé biān kàn zhe tā de bèiyǐng, tūrán juéde děngdài běnshēn yǐjīng shì tā de quánbù shōuhuò le. Yú zhǐ shì jièkǒu.
text_en: I used to be terrible at waiting. Anxious in queues, restless waiting for people, refreshing the delivery tracker every thirty seconds. Then I noticed my neighbor Old Zhao. Retired carpenter, only hobby: fishing. Once I watched him fish all afternoon without a single catch. I asked if he was anxious. Without turning: 'Anxious about what? The fish don't have a clock to punch.' He explained that as a carpenter, after varnishing you wait for it to dry, after gluing a mortise you wait for it to set — rushing is useless. I said I understood but couldn't do it. He asked: 'What do you do while waiting for delivery?' Scroll my phone. He said: 'Then you're not waiting — you're avoiding waiting. Real waiting is doing nothing.' I tried. At a red light, doing nothing. I lasted eleven seconds before pulling out my phone. Second attempt: eighteen seconds. Third time I held out until the green light. Later I told Old Zhao. He was packing up — still zero fish. He said: 'Not bad. Took me thirty years to learn what you picked up in a week.' I said: 'In thirty years, how many fish have you caught?' He thought: 'Enough for one hotpot.' He slung the empty bucket over his shoulder and walked away. Waiting itself was his catch. The fish were a pretext.

questions.q_zh: 老赵为什么钓不到鱼也不着急？
questions.q_en: Why wasn't Old Zhao anxious despite catching no fish?

options.text: 他已经习惯钓不到鱼了
options.pinyin: tā yǐjīng xíguàn diào bú dào yú le
options.text_en: He was used to not catching fish

options.text: 做木匠的经验教会了他等待不是空白，而是装满生活的时间
options.pinyin: zuò mùjiàng de jīngyàn jiāohuì le tā děngdài bú shì kòngbái, ér shì zhuāng mǎn shēnghuó de shíjiān
options.text_en: His carpentry experience taught him that waiting isn't emptiness but time filled with life

options.text: 他只是为了享受户外的空气
options.pinyin: tā zhǐshì wèile xiǎngshòu hùwài de kōngqì
options.text_en: He just wanted to enjoy the outdoor air

questions.explanation: Old Zhao's carpentry taught him that waiting is active—filled with subtle life—not empty. He applies the same patience to fishing without anxiety.

questions.q_zh: 作者认为等待的质量取决于什么？
questions.q_en: What does the author think determines the quality of waiting?

options.pinyin: děngdài de shíjiān chángduǎn
options.text_en: The length of the wait

options.pinyin: yī gè rén de gǎnzhī lì
options.text_en: A person's perceptiveness

options.pinyin: děngdài de yuányīn shìfǒu zhíde
options.text_en: Whether the reason for waiting is worthwhile

questions.explanation: A perceptive person fills waiting with observation and thought. A dull person experiences only emptiness. Perceptiveness determines whether waiting is rich or barren.

title: The Grocery List
text_zh: 我妈每次去菜市场之前都写买菜单，不用手机备忘录，用圆珠笔写在旧日历纸背面。字很小，排列紧凑，偶尔加括号：「如果新鲜的话」「看价钱」「跟上次那个摊比一下」。有一次我仔细研究了一张她的买菜单，发现它的信息密度超过大多数公司的项目计划书。「排骨（周末炖汤，买李屠户家的，不要张家的，上次太肥）」——这一行包含了时间规划、供应商评估和质量控制。「鸡蛋x2打（给楼下张奶奶带一打，她上周帮我收了快递）」——这是人情往来的债务清算。「豆腐（嫩的，老王家搬走以后买菜场东头那家的，不如老王但凑合）」——这是供应链中断后的替代方案分析。最让我困惑的是清单末尾永远会出现的一行：「小明爱吃的那种糕点」。我三十五岁了。我已经十二年没有住在家里了。我上次吃那种糕点可能是高中的事。但她的清单上，这一行从未消失过。有一次我忍不住跟她说：「妈，我现在不怎么吃糕点了。」她看了我一眼，继续写单子：「那我买回来放着，万一你回来呢。」我后来把几张她用过的买菜单夹进了一本书里。清单上有些菜她已经买不到了，有些摊位已经不在了，有些邻居已经搬走了。但「小明爱吃的那种糕点」还在。它是整张清单上唯一从不打折、从不比价、从不标注替代方案的一项。
text_pinyin: Wǒ mā měi cì qù càishìchǎng zhīqián dōu xiě mǎi cài dān, bú yòng shǒujī bèiwànglù, yòng yuánzhūbǐ xiě zài jiù rìlì zhǐ bèimiàn. Zì hěn xiǎo, páiliè jǐncòu, ǒu'ěr jiā kuòhào: "rúguǒ xīnxiān dehuà" "kàn jiàqian" "gēn shàng cì nàge tān bǐ yíxià". Yǒu yí cì wǒ zǐxì yánjiū le yì zhāng tā de mǎi cài dān, fāxiàn tā de xìnxī mìdù chāoguò dàduōshù gōngsī de xiàngmù jìhuàshū. "Páigǔ (zhōumò dùn tāng, mǎi Lǐ túhù jiā de, búyào Zhāng jiā de, shàng cì tài féi)" — zhè yì háng bāohán le shíjiān guīhuà, gōngyìngshāng pínggū hé zhìliàng kòngzhì. "Jīdàn x2 dǎ (gěi lóuxià Zhāng nǎinai dài yì dǎ, tā shàng zhōu bāng wǒ shōu le kuàidì)" — zhè shì rénqíng wǎnglái de zhàiwù qīngsuàn. "Dòufu (nèn de, Lǎo Wáng jiā bān zǒu yǐhòu mǎi càichǎng dōngtou nà jiā de, bùrú Lǎo Wáng dàn còuhe)" — zhè shì gōngyìngliàn zhōngduàn hòu de tìdài fāng'àn fēnxī. Zuì ràng wǒ kùnhuò de shì qīngdān mòwěi yǒngyuǎn huì chūxiàn de yì háng: "Xiǎo Míng ài chī de nà zhǒng gāodiǎn". Wǒ sānshíwǔ suì le. Wǒ yǐjīng shí'èr nián méiyǒu zhù zài jiālǐ le. Wǒ shàng cì chī nà zhǒng gāodiǎn kěnéng shì gāozhōng de shì. Dàn tā de qīngdān shàng, zhè yì háng cóng wèi xiāoshī guò. Yǒu yí cì wǒ rěn bú zhù gēn tā shuō: "Mā, wǒ xiànzài bù zěnme chī gāodiǎn le." Tā kàn le wǒ yì yǎn, jìxù xiě dānzi: "Nà wǒ mǎi huílái fàng zhe, wànyī nǐ huílái ne." Wǒ hòulái bǎ jǐ zhāng tā yòng guò de mǎi cài dān jiā jìn le yì běn shū lǐ. Qīngdān shàng yǒuxiē cài tā yǐjīng mǎi bú dào le, yǒuxiē tānwèi yǐjīng bú zài le, yǒuxiē línjū yǐjīng bān zǒu le. Dàn "Xiǎo Míng ài chī de nà zhǒng gāodiǎn" hái zài. Tā shì zhěng zhāng qīngdān shàng wéiyī cóng bù dǎzhé, cóng bù bǐjià, cóng bù biāozhù tìdài fāng'àn de yí xiàng.
text_en: Before every market trip, my mother writes a grocery list — not on her phone, but in ballpoint pen on old calendar paper. Tight handwriting with parentheses: 'if it's fresh,' 'check the price.' Once I studied a list and found its information density exceeds most corporate project plans. 'Ribs (weekend stew — buy from Butcher Li, not Zhang, last time too fatty)' — time planning, vendor evaluation, quality control. 'Eggs x2 dozen (bring one dozen for Grandma Zhang downstairs, she picked up my delivery last week)' — social-debt settlement. 'Tofu (soft — since Old Wang moved away, buy from east-end stall, not as good but acceptable)' — supply-chain contingency. What puzzles me most is the line at the bottom of every single list: 'The pastry Xiao Ming likes.' I'm thirty-five. I haven't lived at home in twelve years. The last time I ate that pastry was probably high school. But that line has never disappeared. Once I said: 'Mom, I don't eat pastries anymore.' She glanced at me and kept writing: 'I'll buy some, in case you come home.' I pressed a few used lists inside a book. Some items she can no longer find, some stalls no longer exist. But 'the pastry Xiao Ming likes' is still there — the only item that never gets a discount comparison, a substitute, or a price check.

questions.q_zh: 作者为什么觉得妈妈的买菜单比文学作品更真实？
questions.q_en: Why does the author consider his mother's grocery list more authentic than literary works?

options.text: 因为它没有修辞和隐喻，只记录最基础的家庭需求
options.pinyin: yīnwèi tā méiyǒu xiūcí hé yǐnyù, zhǐ jìlù zuì jīchǔ de jiātíng xūqiú
options.text_en: Because it has no rhetoric or metaphor, only recording basic family needs

options.text: 因为它是手写的，不是打印的
options.pinyin: yīnwèi tā shì shǒuxiě de, bú shì dǎyìn de
options.text_en: Because it's handwritten, not printed

options.text: 因为菜市场比书店更有生活气息
options.pinyin: yīnwèi càishìchǎng bǐ shūdiàn gèng yǒu shēnghuó qìxī
options.text_en: Because markets have more of a life atmosphere than bookstores

questions.explanation: The grocery list has no rhetorical devices, no metaphors—just raw needs. The author considers this radical honesty more authentic than crafted prose.

questions.q_zh: 最后一行「小明爱吃的那种糕点」说明了什么？
questions.q_en: What does the last line 'the pastry Xiao Ming likes' reveal?

options.text: 作者经常回家看望母亲
options.pinyin: zuòzhě jīngcháng huí jiā kànwàng mǔqīn
options.text_en: The author often visits his mother

options.text: 母亲仍然记得并牵挂着已经长大的孩子的喜好
options.pinyin: mǔqīn réngrán jìde bìng qiānguà zhe yǐjīng zhǎng dà de háizi de xǐhào
options.text_en: The mother still remembers and cares about her grown child's preferences

options.text: 小明经常让母亲买糕点
options.pinyin: Xiǎo Míng jīngcháng ràng mǔqīn mǎi gāodiǎn
options.text_en: Xiao Ming often asks his mother to buy pastries

questions.explanation: Among practical items, 'the pastry Xiao Ming likes' appears—proof that even in routine shopping, the mother tracks her grown child's preferences.

title: The Sound of Slippers
text_zh: 住在老式居民楼里，你会学到一种特殊的本领：通过脚步声辨认邻居。每个人走路的节奏不同，穿的拖鞋也不同，在走廊里产生的声音就构成了各自的签名。三楼的李叔穿棉拖鞋，走路很轻，「噗噗噗」的，像有人在捏一团棉花。四楼的周阿姨穿塑料拖鞋，步子很快，「啪嗒啪嗒」的，总像在赶什么事。五楼新搬来的年轻人穿木底拖鞋，「嗒嗒嗒」的，声音清脆但不恼人，像一首节拍简单的打击乐。住久了，你甚至不需要看就知道是谁经过了你的门口。深夜两三点，如果听到「噗噗噗」，我就知道是李叔又起来上厕所了。早上六点，「啪嗒啪嗒」开始响起，说明周阿姨已经出门买菜了。这些声音构成了一种社区的生物钟。它让你知道：你不是一个人住在这栋楼里，你被其他人的生活节奏包围着。这种包围不是打扰，而是一种安慰。就像森林里的虫鸣鸟叫——当它们存在的时候你不觉得什么，但如果哪天突然全部安静了，你会本能地感到不安。社区也是这样。如果哪天李叔的「噗噗噗」没有出现，我大概会忍不住去敲他的门看看他是不是没事。
text_pinyin: Zhù zài lǎoshì jūmínlóu lǐ, nǐ huì xué dào yī zhǒng tèshū de běnlǐng: tōngguò jiǎobù shēng biànrèn línjū. Měi gè rén zǒulù de jiézòu bùtóng, chuān de tuōxié yě bùtóng, zài zǒuláng lǐ chǎnshēng de shēngyīn jiù gòuchéng le gèzì de qiānmíng. Sān lóu de Lǐ shū chuān mián tuōxié, zǒulù hěn qīng, 「pūpūpū」de, xiàng yǒu rén zài niē yī tuán miánhua. Sì lóu de Zhōu āyí chuān sùliào tuōxié, bùzi hěn kuài, 「pādā pādā」de, zǒng xiàng zài gǎn shénme shì. Wǔ lóu xīn bān lái de niánqīng rén chuān mù dǐ tuōxié, 「dādādā」de, shēngyīn qīngcuì dàn bú nǎo rén, xiàng yī shǒu jiépāi jiǎndān de dǎjī yuè. Zhù jiǔ le, nǐ shènzhì bù xūyào kàn jiù zhīdào shì shéi jīngguò le nǐ de ménkǒu. Shēnyè liǎng sān diǎn, rúguǒ tīng dào「pūpūpū」, wǒ jiù zhīdào shì Lǐ shū yòu qǐlái shàng cèsuǒ le. Zǎoshang liù diǎn, 「pādā pādā」kāishǐ xiǎng qǐ, shuōmíng Zhōu āyí yǐjīng chūmén mǎi cài le. Zhèxiē shēngyīn gòuchéng le yī zhǒng shèqū de shēngwù zhōng. Tā ràng nǐ zhīdào: nǐ bú shì yī gè rén zhù zài zhè dòng lóu lǐ, nǐ bèi qítā rén de shēnghuó jiézòu bāowéi zhe. Zhè zhǒng bāowéi bú shì dǎrǎo, ér shì yī zhǒng ānwèi. Jiù xiàng sēnlín lǐ de chóng míng niǎo jiào——dāng tāmen cúnzài de shíhou nǐ bù juéde shénme, dàn rúguǒ nǎ tiān tūrán quánbù ānjìng le, nǐ huì běnnéng de gǎndào bù'ān. Shèqū yě shì zhèyàng. Rúguǒ nǎ tiān Lǐ shū de「pūpūpū」méiyǒu chūxiàn, wǒ dàgài huì rěn bú zhù qù qiāo tā de mén kàn kàn tā shì bu shì méi shì.
text_en: Living in an old-style residential building, you acquire a special skill: identifying neighbors by their footsteps. Everyone walks at a different rhythm and wears different slippers, so the sounds in the hallway form each person's signature. Uncle Li on the third floor wears cotton slippers and walks softly—'pff, pff, pff'—like someone squeezing a wad of cotton. Auntie Zhou on the fourth floor wears plastic slippers with brisk steps—'clack-clack-clack'—always as if rushing somewhere. The young person who recently moved to the fifth floor wears wooden-soled slippers—'tap, tap, tap'—crisp but not annoying, like simple percussion. After living here long enough, you don't even need to look to know who's passing your door. At two or three in the morning, if I hear 'pff, pff, pff,' I know Uncle Li is up for the bathroom again. At six a.m., when 'clack-clack-clack' begins, Auntie Zhou has already headed out to buy groceries. These sounds form a kind of community body clock. They tell you: you're not living in this building alone; you're surrounded by other people's rhythms. This surrounding isn't disturbance—it's comfort. Like birdsong and insects in a forest—you don't notice them when they're there, but if one day they all go silent, you instinctively feel uneasy. A community works the same way. If one day Uncle Li's 'pff, pff, pff' doesn't appear, I'd probably find myself knocking on his door to check if he's alright.

questions.q_zh: 作者把邻居的拖鞋声比作什么？
questions.q_en: What does the author compare the neighbors' slipper sounds to?

options.pinyin: zàoyīn wūrǎn
options.text_en: Noise pollution

options.text: 一种社区的生物钟和每个人的签名
options.pinyin: yī zhǒng shèqū de shēngwù zhōng hé měi gè rén de qiānmíng
options.text_en: A community body clock and each person's signature

options.pinyin: yī shǒu jiāoxiǎngyuè
options.text_en: A symphony

questions.explanation: Each neighbor's slipper sound is distinctive—a personal acoustic signature that also marks time, functioning as the community's body clock.

questions.q_zh: 如果李叔的脚步声没有出现，作者会怎么做？
questions.q_en: What would the author do if Uncle Li's footsteps didn't appear?

options.text: 感到轻松，因为更安静了
options.pinyin: gǎndào qīngsōng, yīnwèi gèng ānjìng le
options.text_en: Feel relieved because it's quieter

options.pinyin: bú huì zhùyì dào
options.text_en: Wouldn't notice

options.text: 会去敲他的门看看他是不是没事
options.pinyin: huì qù qiāo tā de mén kàn kàn tā shì bu shì méi shì
options.text_en: Would knock on his door to check on him

questions.explanation: If Uncle Li's footsteps don't appear at the expected time, the author would check on him—the absent sound triggers concern for the person.

title: The Color of Five O'Clock
text_zh: 我一直觉得一天当中最好看的时刻是下午五点左右。那时候太阳已经开始西沉，但还没有进入正式的日落阶段。光线从金黄色变成一种带着琥珀色调的暖橙色，所有的东西都被这种光镀了一层颜色。白色的墙变成了淡金色，灰色的马路变成了温暖的棕色，连行人的脸都好看了起来，因为那种光会抹平皱纹和瑕疵，给每一张脸加上一层柔焦滤镜。摄影师管这个叫「黄金时刻」，但普通人很少注意到它。因为五点钟通常是下班的时候，人们要么困在地铁里看手机，要么堵在路上听广播。他们错过的不是一次日落，而是一天之中光线最温柔的二十分钟。我养成了一个习惯，每天五点左右，无论在做什么，都抬头看一眼窗外。有时候是一棵被光染成金色的银杏树，有时候是远处楼房的玻璃反射出的一片碎光，有时候只是天空从蓝色过渡到橙色的那条分界线。这些东西每天都在发生，但每天都不一样。「美」不是稀缺的，美只是需要你在正确的时刻抬一下头。
text_pinyin: Wǒ yīzhí juéde yī tiān dāngzhōng zuì hǎokàn de shíkè shì xiàwǔ wǔ diǎn zuǒyòu. Nà shíhou tàiyáng yǐjīng kāishǐ xī chén, dàn hái méiyǒu jìnrù zhèngshì de rìluò jiēduàn. Guāngxiàn cóng jīnhuáng sè biànchéng yī zhǒng dài zhe hǔpò sèdiào de nuǎn chéngsè, suǒyǒu de dōngxi dōu bèi zhè zhǒng guāng dù le yī céng yánsè. Báisè de qiáng biànchéng le dàn jīnsè, huīsè de mǎlù biànchéng le wēnnuǎn de zōngsè, lián xíngrén de liǎn dōu hǎokàn le qǐlái, yīnwèi nà zhǒng guāng huì mǒ píng zhòuwén hé xiácī, gěi měi yī zhāng liǎn jiā shàng yī céng róujiāo lǜjìng. Shèyǐngshī guǎn zhège jiào「huángjīn shíkè」, dàn pǔtōng rén hěn shǎo zhùyì dào tā. Yīnwèi wǔ diǎn zhōng tōngcháng shì xiàbān de shíhou, rénmen yàome kùn zài dìtiě lǐ kàn shǒujī, yàome dǔ zài lù shàng tīng guǎngbō. Tāmen cuòguò de bú shì yī cì rìluò, ér shì yī tiān zhī zhōng guāngxiàn zuì wēnróu de èrshí fēnzhōng. Wǒ yǎngchéng le yī gè xíguàn, měi tiān wǔ diǎn zuǒyòu, wúlùn zài zuò shénme, dōu tái tóu kàn yī yǎn chuāng wài. Yǒu shíhou shì yī kē bèi guāng rǎn chéng jīnsè de yínxìng shù, yǒu shíhou shì yuǎnchù lóufáng de bōli fǎnshè chū de yī piàn suì guāng, yǒu shíhou zhǐshì tiānkōng cóng lán sè guòdù dào chéngsè de nà tiáo fēnjièxiàn. Zhèxiē dōngxi měi tiān dōu zài fāshēng, dàn měi tiān dōu bù yīyàng. 「Měi」bú shì xīquē de, měi zhǐshì xūyào nǐ zài zhèngquè de shíkè tái yīxià tóu.
text_en: I've always felt the most beautiful moment of the day is around five in the afternoon. By then the sun has begun its descent but hasn't entered the formal sunset stage. Light shifts from golden to a warm amber-tinged orange, coating everything in an extra layer of color. White walls turn pale gold, gray roads become warm brown, and even people's faces look better because this light smooths out wrinkles and blemishes, adding a soft-focus filter to every face. Photographers call it the 'golden hour,' but most people rarely notice it. Five o'clock is usually quitting time—people are either trapped in the subway staring at phones or stuck in traffic listening to the radio. What they miss isn't just a sunset, but the gentlest twenty minutes of light in the entire day. I've formed a habit: around five every day, no matter what I'm doing, I look up and glance out the window. Sometimes it's a ginkgo tree dyed golden by the light, sometimes scattered reflections from building glass in the distance, sometimes just the boundary line where sky transitions from blue to orange. These things happen every day, yet they're different every day.

questions.q_zh: 为什么作者说普通人很少注意到「黄金时刻」？
questions.q_en: Why does the author say most people rarely notice the 'golden hour'?

options.text: 因为五点钟通常是下班时间，人们在地铁或路上
options.pinyin: yīnwèi wǔ diǎn zhōng tōngcháng shì xiàbān shíjiān, rénmen zài dìtiě huò lù shàng
options.text_en: Because five o'clock is usually quitting time and people are in the subway or on the road

options.text: 因为那种光线太微弱了
options.pinyin: yīnwèi nà zhǒng guāngxiàn tài wēiruò le
options.text_en: Because the light is too faint

options.text: 因为日落每天都一样，没什么特别的
options.pinyin: yīnwèi rìluò měi tiān dōu yīyàng, méi shénme tèbié de
options.text_en: Because sunsets are the same every day, nothing special

questions.explanation: Five o'clock is rush hour—people are underground in subways or stuck on roads. The golden hour plays to an empty audience.

questions.q_zh: 文章最后一句「美只是需要你在正确的时刻抬一下头」表达了什么观点？
questions.q_en: What idea does the final sentence express—'beauty just needs you to look up at the right moment'?

options.text: 美并不稀缺，关键在于人的感知和注意力
options.pinyin: měi bìng bù xīquē, guānjiàn zàiyú rén de gǎnzhī hé zhùyìlì
options.text_en: Beauty isn't scarce—what matters is human perception and attention

options.text: 要学会在日落的时候停下工作
options.pinyin: yào xuéhuì zài rìluò de shíhou tíng xià gōngzuò
options.text_en: You should learn to stop work at sunset

options.text: 摄影师比普通人更懂得欣赏美
options.pinyin: shèyǐngshī bǐ pǔtōng rén gèng dǒngde xīnshǎng měi
options.text_en: Photographers understand beauty better than ordinary people

questions.explanation: Beauty isn't scarce; human attention is. The sentence argues that perception and timing, not rarity, determine whether beauty is experienced.

title: The Shape of Steam
text_zh: 每天早上我都会泡一杯茶，然后花几分钟看茶杯上方的蒸汽。蒸汽是透明的，但在逆光的条件下，你能看到它的形状——不规则的丝缕，从杯口升起，扭曲、旋转、扩散，最终消失在空气里。没有任何两秒钟的蒸汽形状是一样的。这可能是世界上最短暂的艺术品：存在即消亡，诞生即结束。我试过给蒸汽拍照，但相机捕捉到的只是一团模糊的白色。蒸汽的美在于它的运动，而照片是静止的。就好像你不能用一张照片来表现一段音乐的美——你需要时间维度来承载它。有些东西注定只能被当下的眼睛看到，不能被记录、不能被保存、不能被分享。这不是一种遗憾，而是一种特权。因为它只属于那个此刻正在看的人。我们的时代把「可分享」当作价值的标准——如果不能拍照发朋友圈，这次体验就好像不存在。但蒸汽提醒我，有些最好的东西，恰恰是不可分享的。它们的珍贵之处，就在于它们只发生一次，只被一个人看到，然后永远消失。像一个只对你一个人讲的秘密。
text_pinyin: Měi tiān zǎoshang wǒ dōu huì pào yī bēi chá, ránhòu huā jǐ fēnzhōng kàn chábēi shàngfāng de zhēngqì. Zhēngqì shì tòumíng de, dàn zài nìguāng de tiáojiàn xià, nǐ néng kàn dào tā de xíngzhuàng——bù guīzé de sīlǚ, cóng bēi kǒu shēng qǐ, niǔqū, xuánzhuǎn, kuòsàn, zuìzhōng xiāoshī zài kōngqì lǐ. Méiyǒu rènhé liǎng miǎozhōng de zhēngqì xíngzhuàng shì yīyàng de. Zhè kěnéng shì shìjiè shàng zuì duǎnzàn de yìshùpǐn: cúnzài jí xiāowáng, dànshēng jí jiéshù. Wǒ shì guò gěi zhēngqì pāizhào, dàn xiàngjī bǔzhuō dào de zhǐshì yī tuán móhu de báisè. Zhēngqì de měi zàiyú tā de yùndòng, ér zhàopiàn shì jìngzhǐ de. Jiù hǎoxiàng nǐ bù néng yòng yī zhāng zhàopiàn lái biǎoxiàn yī duàn yīnyuè de měi——nǐ xūyào shíjiān wéidù lái chéngzài tā. Yǒuxiē dōngxi zhùdìng zhǐ néng bèi dāngxià de yǎnjīng kàn dào, bù néng bèi jìlù, bù néng bèi bǎocún, bù néng bèi fēnxiǎng. Zhè bú shì yī zhǒng yíhàn, ér shì yī zhǒng tèquán. Yīnwèi tā zhǐ shǔyú nàge cǐkè zhèngzài kàn de rén. Wǒmen de shídài bǎ「kě fēnxiǎng」dāng zuò jiàzhí de biāozhǔn——rúguǒ bù néng pāizhào fā péngyouquān, zhè cì tǐyàn jiù hǎoxiàng bù cúnzài. Dàn zhēngqì tíxǐng wǒ, yǒuxiē zuì hǎo de dōngxi, qiàqià shì bùkě fēnxiǎng de. Tāmen de zhēnguì zhī chù, jiù zàiyú tāmen zhǐ fāshēng yī cì, zhǐ bèi yī gè rén kàn dào, ránhòu yǒngyuǎn xiāoshī. Xiàng yī gè zhǐ duì nǐ yī gè rén jiǎng de mìmì.
text_en: Every morning I brew a cup of tea, then spend a few minutes watching the steam above the cup. Steam is transparent, but backlit you can see its shape—irregular wisps rising from the rim, twisting, rotating, spreading, eventually vanishing into the air. No two seconds of steam look the same. This may be the world's most fleeting artwork: to exist is to perish, to be born is to end. I've tried photographing steam, but the camera captures only a blurry white blur. Steam's beauty lies in its movement, and photographs are still. It's like trying to capture the beauty of music in a single photograph—you need the dimension of time. Some things are destined to be seen only by the present eye: unrecordable, unsaveable, unshareable. This isn't a regret—it's a privilege. Because it belongs only to the person looking at that exact moment. Our era treats 'shareability' as the standard of value—if you can't photograph it for social media, the experience almost doesn't exist. But steam reminds me that some of the best things are precisely unshareable.

questions.q_zh: 作者为什么说给蒸汽拍照是不成功的？
questions.q_en: Why does the author say photographing steam is unsuccessful?

options.text: 蒸汽的美在于运动，而照片是静止的
options.pinyin: zhēngqì de měi zàiyú yùndòng, ér zhàopiàn shì jìngzhǐ de
options.text_en: Steam's beauty lies in its movement, but photos are still

options.text: 蒸汽太小了，相机拍不清楚
options.pinyin: zhēngqì tài xiǎo le, xiàngjī pāi bù qīngchu
options.text_en: Steam is too small for the camera to capture clearly

options.pinyin: guāngxiàn tiáojiàn bù hǎo
options.text_en: The lighting conditions were bad

questions.explanation: Steam's beauty depends on continuous movement. A photograph freezes it into a static shape, killing exactly what makes it beautiful.

questions.q_zh: 作者对「可分享」作为价值标准持什么态度？
questions.q_en: What is the author's attitude toward 'shareability' as a standard of value?

options.pinyin: tā rènwéi fēnxiǎng hěn zhòngyào
options.text_en: He thinks sharing is important

options.text: 他认为有些最好的东西恰恰是不可分享的
options.pinyin: tā rènwéi yǒuxiē zuì hǎo de dōngxi qiàqià shì bùkě fēnxiǎng de
options.text_en: He thinks some of the best things are precisely unshareable

options.pinyin: tā fǎnduì shǐyòng shèjiāo méitǐ
options.text_en: He opposes using social media

questions.explanation: The author pushes back against 'shareability' as a value metric. Some experiences are valuable precisely because they resist capture and transmission.

title: The Bench That Nobody Sits On
text_zh: 小区里有一张长椅，位置很好，在一棵大榕树下面，有树荫有微风，但几乎没有人坐。原因很简单：它旁边有一个垃圾桶。尽管环卫工人每天清理，垃圾桶并不臭，但人们还是本能地避开它。大家宁愿站着等人，也不愿坐在垃圾桶旁边。这种回避不是理性的判断，而是一种心理上的排斥。我偶尔会故意去坐那张长椅。坐下来之后发现，那其实是整个小区最安静的一个角落。因为没人来，也没人经过。垃圾桶反而变成了一道天然的屏障，把你和人群隔开。鸟在树上叫，风吹过榕树的气根，午后的阳光透过树叶落下来，形成一地斑驳的光影。这种体验是小区里其他长椅给不了的，因为那些长椅总是坐满了聊天的老人或者玩手机的年轻人。我开始想：生活中有多少好东西，是因为旁边放了一个「垃圾桶」而被我们错过的？一份工资不高但很有意义的工作、一个外表普通但内心丰富的人、一部评分不高但非常独特的电影。我们太习惯根据标签做判断了，以至于忘了亲自坐下来感受一下。
text_pinyin: Xiǎoqū lǐ yǒu yī zhāng chángyǐ, wèizhì hěn hǎo, zài yī kē dà róngshù xiàmian, yǒu shùyìn yǒu wēifēng, dàn jīhū méiyǒu rén zuò. Yuányīn hěn jiǎndān: tā pángbiān yǒu yī gè lājī tǒng. Jǐnguǎn huánwèi gōngrén měi tiān qīnglǐ, lājī tǒng bìng bú chòu, dàn rénmen háishi běnnéng de bìkāi tā. Dàjiā nìngyuàn zhàn zhe děng rén, yě bú yuàn zuò zài lājī tǒng pángbiān. Zhè zhǒng huíbì bú shì lǐxìng de pànduàn, ér shì yī zhǒng xīnlǐ shàng de páichì. Wǒ ǒu'ěr huì gùyì qù zuò nà zhāng chángyǐ. Zuò xiàlái zhīhòu fāxiàn, nà qíshí shì zhěnggè xiǎoqū zuì ānjìng de yī gè jiǎoluò. Yīnwèi méi rén lái, yě méi rén jīngguò. Lājī tǒng fǎn'ér biànchéng le yī dào tiānrán de píngzhàng, bǎ nǐ hé rénqún gé kāi. Niǎo zài shù shàng jiào, fēng chuī guò róngshù de qìgēn, wǔhòu de yángguāng tòu guò shùyè luò xiàlái, xíngchéng yī dì bānbó de guāngyǐng. Zhè zhǒng tǐyàn shì xiǎoqū lǐ qítā chángyǐ gěi bù liǎo de, yīnwèi nàxiē chángyǐ zǒng shì zuò mǎn le liáotiān de lǎorén huòzhě wán shǒujī de niánqīng rén. Wǒ kāishǐ xiǎng: shēnghuó zhōng yǒu duōshǎo hǎo dōngxi, shì yīnwèi pángbiān fàng le yī gè「lājī tǒng」ér bèi wǒmen cuòguò de? Yī fèn gōngzī bù gāo dàn hěn yǒu yìyì de gōngzuò, yī gè wàibiǎo pǔtōng dàn nèixīn fēngfù de rén, yī bù píngfēn bù gāo dàn fēicháng dútè de diànyǐng. Wǒmen tài xíguàn gēnjù biāoqiān zuò pànduàn le, yǐzhìyú wàng le qīnzì zuò xiàlái gǎnshòu yīxià.
text_en: In the residential compound there's a bench in an excellent spot—under a large banyan tree with shade and breeze—yet almost nobody sits on it. The reason is simple: there's a trash bin next to it. Though sanitation workers clean it daily and it doesn't smell, people instinctively avoid it. They'd rather stand and wait than sit beside a trash bin. This avoidance isn't rational judgment but psychological rejection. I occasionally sit there on purpose. Once seated, I discovered it's actually the quietest corner in the entire compound. Because nobody comes, nobody passes by. The trash bin has become a natural barrier separating you from the crowd. Birds sing in the tree, wind blows through the banyan's aerial roots, afternoon sunlight filters through leaves creating dappled patterns of light and shadow. This experience is something other benches in the compound can't offer, because those are always full of chatting elders or phone-scrolling young people. I began to wonder: how many good things in life do we miss because there's a 'trash bin' placed beside them? A low-paying but meaningful job, an ordinary-looking but richly inner person, a low-rated but genuinely unique film. We've grown too accustomed to judging by labels, forgetting to sit down and experience things for ourselves.

questions.q_zh: 为什么那张位置很好的长椅几乎没人坐？
questions.q_en: Why does almost nobody sit on the well-positioned bench?

options.pinyin: yīnwèi yǐzi huài le
options.text_en: Because the bench is broken

options.text: 因为旁边有垃圾桶，人们心理上排斥
options.pinyin: yīnwèi pángbiān yǒu lājī tǒng, rénmen xīnlǐ shàng páichì
options.text_en: Because there's a trash bin next to it and people are psychologically repelled

options.pinyin: yīnwèi méiyǒu shùyìn
options.text_en: Because there's no shade

questions.explanation: The bench has good positioning but a trash bin beside it creates psychological repulsion. Location is fine; the association drives people away.

questions.q_zh: 作者用「垃圾桶」来比喻什么？
questions.q_en: What does the author use the 'trash bin' as a metaphor for?

options.text: 生活中那些让我们因为表面标签而错过好东西的负面因素
options.pinyin: shēnghuó zhōng nàxiē ràng wǒmen yīnwèi biǎomiàn biāoqiān ér cuòguò hǎo dōngxi de fùmiàn yīnsù
options.text_en: Negative surface-level labels in life that make us miss good things

options.text: 城市里的环境污染问题
options.pinyin: chéngshì lǐ de huánjìng wūrǎn wèntí
options.text_en: Environmental pollution problems in cities

options.pinyin: rénjì guānxi zhōng de zhàng'ài
options.text_en: Obstacles in interpersonal relationships

questions.explanation: The trash bin stands for any negative surface label that causes people to avoid something without evaluating its actual quality. Prejudgment by proximity.

title: Two Ways of Folding Laundry
text_zh: 我跟妻子叠衣服的分歧已经持续了九年。她的方法：每件T恤先对折袖子，再对折身体，最后再折一次，叠出来大小一模一样，整整齐齐码在衣柜里，像MUJI的展示柜。我的方法：分成「大致对折」和「揉成一团塞进去」两档，取决于当天的心情。我们为此吵过无数次。她认为我的叠法是对布料的犯罪。我认为衣服穿在身上没人能看出它是被精心叠过还是被扔进过洗衣机然后直接穿的。有一天我做了一个实验：我精心叠了三件T恤，同时揉了三件T恤，然后分别穿出门。我问了六个同事能不能看出区别。六个人都说看不出来。我拍照给妻子看，以为赢了。她看了一眼说：「我叠衣服不是给别人看的。」然后她叠完一摞衣服，站在衣柜前看了好几秒。那个眼神不是在检查整不整齐。那是一个在审视自己作品的人的眼神。那一刻我忽然明白了：她的生活在很多方面是失控的——工作压力、孩子生病、跟她妈的关系——但这个衣柜是她唯一能完全掌控的领地。我没有再说什么。从那以后我偶尔也会认真叠一次。叠得当然还是很丑。但她每次看了都笑。那种笑不是因为我叠得好，是因为我终于不再试图证明她是错的了。
text_pinyin: Wǒ gēn qīzi dié yīfu de fēnqí yǐjīng chíxù le jiǔ nián. Tā de fāngfǎ: měi jiàn T xù xiān duìzhé xiùzi, zài duìzhé shēntǐ, zuìhòu zài zhé yí cì, dié chūlái dàxiǎo yì mú yíyàng, zhěngzhěngqíqí mǎ zài yīguì lǐ, xiàng MUJI de zhǎnshìguì. Wǒ de fāngfǎ: fēn chéng "dàzhì duìzhé" hé "róu chéng yì tuán sāi jìnqù" liǎng dǎng, qǔjué yú dàngtiān de xīnqíng. Wǒmen wèi cǐ chǎo guò wúshù cì. Tā rènwéi wǒ de diéfǎ shì duì bùliào de fànzuì. Wǒ rènwéi yīfu chuān zài shēn shàng méi rén néng kàn chū tā shì bèi jīngxīn dié guò háishi bèi rēng jìn guò xǐyījī ránhòu zhíjiē chuān de. Yǒu yì tiān wǒ zuò le yí ge shíyàn: wǒ jīngxīn dié le sān jiàn T xù, tóngshí róu le sān jiàn T xù, ránhòu fēnbié chuān chūmén. Wǒ wèn le liù ge tóngshì néng bu néng kàn chū qūbié. Liù ge rén dōu shuō kàn bù chūlái. Wǒ pāizhào gěi qīzi kàn, yǐwéi yíng le. Tā kàn le yì yǎn shuō: "Wǒ dié yīfu bú shì gěi biéren kàn de." Ránhòu tā dié wán yì luò yīfu, zhàn zài yīguì qián kàn le hǎo jǐ miǎo. Nàge yǎnshén bú shì zài jiǎnchá zhěng bu zhěngqí. Nà shì yí ge zài shěnshì zìjǐ zuòpǐn de rén de yǎnshén. Nà yí kè wǒ hūrán míngbai le: tā de shēnghuó zài hěn duō fāngmiàn shì shīkòng de — gōngzuò yālì, háizi shēngbìng, gēn tā mā de guānxì — dàn zhège yīguì shì tā wéiyī néng wánquán zhǎngkòng de lǐngdì. Wǒ méiyǒu zài shuō shénme. Cóng nà yǐhòu wǒ ǒu'ěr yě huì rènzhēn dié yí cì. Dié de dāngrán háishi hěn chǒu. Dàn tā měi cì kàn le dōu xiào. Nà zhǒng xiào bú shì yīnwèi wǒ dié de hǎo, shì yīnwèi wǒ zhōngyú bú zài shìtú zhèngmíng tā shì cuò de le.
text_en: My wife and I have been in a laundry-folding dispute for nine years. Her method: sleeves folded, body folded, then once more — all identical, lined up like a MUJI display. My method: split between 'approximate fold' and 'crumple into a ball,' depending on mood. She considers my method a crime against fabric. I maintain nobody can tell whether a shirt was carefully folded or thrown in a dryer. One day I ran an experiment: folded three T-shirts carefully, crumpled three, wore each out. Six coworkers spotted no difference. I showed my wife the photos, thinking I'd won. She said: 'I don't fold laundry for other people to see.' Then she finished a stack and stood before the closet gazing at it. That look wasn't checking neatness. It was someone appraising their own work. Then I understood: her life is out of control in many ways — work, the kid getting sick, her mother — but this closet is the one territory she fully commands. Since then I occasionally fold carefully too. It still looks terrible. But she laughs every time. That laugh isn't because I fold well — it's because I've stopped trying to prove she's wrong.

questions.q_zh: 作者后来为什么不再嘲笑妻子叠衣服了？
questions.q_en: Why did the author stop making fun of his wife's folding?

options.text: 因为他学会了叠得更好
options.pinyin: yīnwèi tā xuéhuì le dié de gèng hǎo
options.text_en: Because he learned to fold better

options.text: 因为他理解了叠衣服对她来说是创造秩序和内心平静的方式
options.pinyin: yīnwèi tā lǐjiě le dié yīfu duì tā lái shuō shì chuàngzào zhìxù hé nèixīn píngjìng de fāngshì
options.text_en: Because he understood that folding was her way of creating order and inner peace

options.pinyin: yīnwèi tāmen dáchéng le tuǒxié
options.text_en: Because they reached a compromise

questions.explanation: He stopped mocking her method after realizing the folding was her way of imposing order on chaos—a meditative practice, not neurotic behavior.

questions.q_zh: 妻子看到作者认真叠的衣服时为什么笑了？
questions.q_en: Why did the wife laugh when she saw the author's carefully folded laundry?

options.pinyin: yīnwèi tā dié de háishi hěn chǒu
options.text_en: Because his folding was still ugly

options.text: 因为她觉得他在模仿她
options.pinyin: yīnwèi tā juéde tā zài mófǎng tā
options.text_en: Because she thought he was imitating her

options.pinyin: yīnwèi tā zhōngyú lǐjiě le tā
options.text_en: Because he'd finally understood her

questions.explanation: Seeing him fold carefully proved he'd grasped what the ritual meant to her. Her laughter was recognition that he finally understood.

title: The Dogs Who Wait
text_zh: 每天早上九点左右，我家楼下的超市门口会出现三四只狗。它们的主人进去买东西，把它们拴在门口的柱子上或者自行车架上。这些狗彼此不认识，但因为共同的处境——被留下来等——它们形成了一种临时的群体。我观察过它们的行为，发现非常有意思。刚被拴住的头几分钟，每只狗都会盯着超市的大门，耳朵竖起来，随时准备迎接主人出来。大约五分钟后，焦虑开始出现。有的狗会小声呜咽，有的会原地转圈，有的会躺下来但眼睛始终盯着门口。再过五分钟，大部分狗会进入一种「接受」状态：它们不再盯着门口，开始观察周围的环境——经过的行人、路过的其他狗、地上的落叶。有一只黄色的中华田园犬特别有意思。它被拴住之后，先焦虑了两分钟，然后就完全放松了，趴在地上晒太阳，偶尔抬头看看四周，一脸平静。它似乎完全信任它的主人一定会回来，所以不浪费精力在焦虑上。我想，也许人类应该向这只狗学习。等待一个你信任的人，最好的方式不是焦虑地盯着门口，而是安心地趴下来晒太阳。
text_pinyin: Měi tiān zǎoshang jiǔ diǎn zuǒyòu, wǒ jiā lóu xià de chāoshì ménkǒu huì chūxiàn sān sì zhī gǒu. Tāmen de zhǔrén jìnqù mǎi dōngxi, bǎ tāmen shuān zài ménkǒu de zhùzi shàng huòzhě zìxíngchē jià shàng. Zhèxiē gǒu bǐcǐ bú rènshi, dàn yīnwèi gòngtóng de chǔjìng——bèi liú xiàlái děng——tāmen xíngchéng le yī zhǒng línshí de qúntǐ. Wǒ guānchá guò tāmen de xíngwéi, fāxiàn fēicháng yǒu yìsi. Gāng bèi shuān zhù de tóu jǐ fēnzhōng, měi zhī gǒu dōu huì dīng zhe chāoshì de dàmén, ěrduo shù qǐlái, suíshí zhǔnbèi yíngjiē zhǔrén chūlái. Dàyuē wǔ fēnzhōng hòu, jiāolǜ kāishǐ chūxiàn. Yǒu de gǒu huì xiǎo shēng wūyè, yǒu de huì yuándì zhuǎnquān, yǒu de huì tǎng xiàlái dàn yǎnjīng shǐzhōng dīng zhe ménkǒu. Zài guò wǔ fēnzhōng, dà bùfen gǒu huì jìnrù yī zhǒng「jiēshòu」zhuàngtài: tāmen bú zài dīng zhe ménkǒu, kāishǐ guānchá zhōuwéi de huánjìng——jīngguò de xíngrén, lùguò de qítā gǒu, dì shàng de luòyè. Yǒu yī zhī huángsè de zhōnghuá tiányuán quǎn tèbié yǒu yìsi. Tā bèi shuān zhù zhīhòu, xiān jiāolǜ le liǎng fēnzhōng, ránhòu jiù wánquán fàngsōng le, pā zài dì shàng shài tàiyáng, ǒu'ěr tái tóu kàn kàn sìzhōu, yī liǎn píngjìng. Tā sìhū wánquán xìnrèn tā de zhǔrén yīdìng huì huílái, suǒyǐ bù làngfèi jīnglì zài jiāolǜ shàng. Wǒ xiǎng, yěxǔ rénlèi yīnggāi xiàng zhè zhī gǒu xuéxí. Děngdài yī gè nǐ xìnrèn de rén, zuì hǎo de fāngshì bú shì jiāolǜ de dīng zhe ménkǒu, ér shì ānxīn de pā xiàlái shài tàiyáng.
text_en: Every morning around nine, three or four dogs appear outside the supermarket below my apartment. Their owners have gone in to shop, tying them to the doorway pillars or bicycle racks. These dogs don't know each other, but their shared situation—being left behind to wait—forms them into a temporary group. I've observed their behavior and found it fascinating. During the first few minutes of being tied up, every dog stares at the supermarket door, ears pricked, ready to greet their returning owner. About five minutes later, anxiety sets in. Some whimper softly, some pace in circles, some lie down but keep their eyes fixed on the entrance. After another five minutes, most dogs enter an 'acceptance' state: they stop watching the door and begin observing their surroundings—passing pedestrians, other dogs, leaves on the ground. One yellow Chinese field dog was especially interesting. After being tied up, it was anxious for about two minutes, then completely relaxed—lying on the ground sunbathing, occasionally lifting its head to look around, perfectly calm. It seemed to completely trust that its owner would return, so it wasted no energy on anxiety. I think perhaps humans should learn from this dog. The best way to wait for someone you trust isn't anxiously watching the door, but peacefully lying down in the sun.

questions.q_zh: 狗被拴住后大约经历了哪几个阶段？
questions.q_en: What stages do dogs go through after being tied up?

options.pinyin: xīngfèn → shuìjiào → jiāolǜ
options.text_en: Excitement → sleeping → anxiety

options.text: 盯着门口→焦虑→接受并观察周围
options.pinyin: dīng zhe ménkǒu → jiāolǜ → jiēshòu bìng guānchá zhōuwéi
options.text_en: Watching the door → anxiety → acceptance and observing surroundings

options.pinyin: ānjìng → jiào → jìxù ānjìng
options.text_en: Quiet → barking → quiet again

questions.explanation: Tied-up dogs progress through three stages: watching the door, growing anxious, then accepting the wait and turning attention to their surroundings.

questions.q_zh: 作者认为那只黄色的狗能迅速放松的原因是什么？
questions.q_en: Why does the author think the yellow dog could relax so quickly?

options.pinyin: tā xìnggé běnlái jiù hěn lǎn
options.text_en: It was naturally lazy

options.text: 它完全信任主人一定会回来
options.pinyin: tā wánquán xìnrèn zhǔrén yīdìng huì huílái
options.text_en: It completely trusted its owner would return

options.text: 它已经习惯被拴在外面了
options.pinyin: tā yǐjīng xíguàn bèi shuān zài wàimiàn le
options.text_en: It was used to being tied up outside

questions.explanation: The yellow dog relaxed quickly because its trust in the owner's return was absolute—security came from confidence in the relationship, not the situation.

title: The Way Light Enters a Room
text_zh: 搬进新家之后，我花了大约一个月的时间才真正了解光线进入这个房间的方式。早上七点，太阳从东边窗户进来，先照到书架的上半部分，然后慢慢移到桌面上。到了九点，整个书桌都被阳光覆盖，这是一天中最适合阅读的时刻，因为光线充足但不刺眼。中午的时候，太阳绕到了南面，从阳台的落地窗射进来，在客厅地板上形成一个巨大的长方形光斑。这个光斑会随着太阳的移动缓缓滑过地板，像一把看不见的扫帚在慢慢扫地。下午三四点，光线开始变得柔和，从西边的小窗户进来，带着一种温暖的琥珀色调，这时候适合坐在沙发上发呆。到了五点半，最后一缕阳光从西窗的上沿斜射进来，刚好照在墙上的一幅画上，持续大约十分钟。这幅画是我在跳蚤市场买的一幅旧油画，平时看起来颜色有些暗淡，但在那十分钟里，它好像被重新点亮了。我没有刻意安排这一切。房子的朝向、窗户的位置、家具的摆放，这些因素碰巧组合在一起，创造了一个关于光的叙事。每天从日出到日落，光线在这个房间里讲述着一个完整的故事。而我，只是恰好住在这个故事里。
text_pinyin: Bān jìn xīn jiā zhīhòu, wǒ huā le dàyuē yī gè yuè de shíjiān cái zhēnzhèng liǎojiě guāngxiàn jìnrù zhège fángjiān de fāngshì. Zǎoshang qī diǎn, tàiyáng cóng dōngbiān chuānghu jìnlái, xiān zhào dào shūjià de shàng bàn bùfen, ránhòu mànmàn yí dào zhuōmiàn shàng. Dào le jiǔ diǎn, zhěnggè shūzhuō dōu bèi yángguāng fùgài, zhè shì yī tiān zhōng zuì shìhé yuèdú de shíkè, yīnwèi guāngxiàn chōngzú dàn bú cìyǎn. Zhōngwǔ de shíhou, tàiyáng rào dào le nánmiàn, cóng yángtái de luòdì chuāng shè jìnlái, zài kètīng dìbǎn shàng xíngchéng yī gè jùdà de chángfāngxíng guāng bān. Zhège guāng bān huì suí zhe tàiyáng de yídòng huǎnhuǎn huá guò dìbǎn, xiàng yī bǎ kàn bú jiàn de sǎozhou zài mànmàn sǎo dì. Xiàwǔ sān sì diǎn, guāngxiàn kāishǐ biàn de róuhé, cóng xībiān de xiǎo chuānghu jìnlái, dài zhe yī zhǒng wēnnuǎn de hǔpò sèdiào, zhè shíhou shìhé zuò zài shāfā shàng fādāi. Dào le wǔ diǎn bàn, zuìhòu yī lǚ yángguāng cóng xī chuāng de shàng yán xiéshè jìnlái, gānghǎo zhào zài qiáng shàng de yī fú huà shàng, chíxù dàyuē shí fēnzhōng. Zhè fú huà shì wǒ zài tiàozǎo shìchǎng mǎi de yī fú jiù yóuhuà, píngshí kàn qǐlái yánsè yǒuxiē àndàn, dàn zài nà shí fēnzhōng lǐ, tā hǎoxiàng bèi chóngxīn diǎn liàng le. Wǒ méiyǒu kèyì ānpái zhè yīqiè. Fángzi de cháoxiàng, chuānghu de wèizhì, jiājù de bǎifàng, zhèxiē yīnsù qiǎohǎo zǔhé zài yīqǐ, chuàngzào le yī gè guānyú guāng de xùshì. Měi tiān cóng rì chū dào rìluò, guāngxiàn zài zhège fángjiān lǐ jiǎngshù zhe yī gè wánzhěng de gùshì. Ér wǒ, zhǐshì qiǎohǎo zhù zài zhège gùshì lǐ.
text_en: After moving into my new home, it took about a month to truly understand how light enters this room. At seven in the morning, the sun comes through the east window, first hitting the upper half of the bookshelf, then slowly moving to the desk. By nine, the entire desk is bathed in sunlight—the best reading moment of the day, with ample but non-glaring light. At noon, the sun has moved to the south, pouring through the balcony's floor-to-ceiling window, forming a huge rectangular patch of light on the living room floor. This patch glides slowly across the floor as the sun moves, like an invisible broom sweeping. Around three or four in the afternoon, the light softens, entering through a small west-side window with a warm amber tone—perfect for sitting on the sofa and daydreaming. At five-thirty, the last slant of sunlight enters from the upper edge of the west window, landing precisely on a painting on the wall for about ten minutes. It's an old oil painting I bought at a flea market—normally its colors seem muted, but during those ten minutes it seems to be reilluminated. I didn't deliberately arrange any of this. The building's orientation, the windows' positions, the furniture placement—these factors happened to combine and create a narrative about light. Every day from sunrise to sunset, light tells a complete story in this room. And I simply happen to live inside that story.

questions.q_zh: 作者认为一天中最适合阅读的时刻是什么时候？
questions.q_en: When does the author consider the best reading moment of the day?

options.text: 早上九点左右，光线充足但不刺眼
options.pinyin: zǎoshang jiǔ diǎn zuǒyòu, guāngxiàn chōngzú dàn bú cìyǎn
options.text_en: Around 9 a.m., when light is ample but not glaring

options.pinyin: xiàwǔ sān sì diǎn, guāngxiàn róuhé
options.text_en: 3-4 p.m. when light is soft

options.pinyin: zhōngwǔ, yángguāng zuì qiáng de shíhou
options.text_en: Noon, when sunlight is strongest

questions.explanation: Around 9 a.m. the light is generous enough to read by but not harsh enough to glare—a narrow window of ideal natural illumination.

questions.q_zh: 文章最后说「我只是恰好住在这个故事里」，这表达了什么感受？
questions.q_en: What does the final sentence 'I simply happen to live inside that story' express?

options.text: 他对新家的光线设计很满意
options.pinyin: tā duì xīn jiā de guāngxiàn shèjì hěn mǎnyì
options.text_en: He's satisfied with his new home's lighting design

options.text: 一种对日常之美的偶然性和感恩
options.pinyin: yī zhǒng duì rìcháng zhī měi de ǒurán xìng hé gǎn'ēn
options.text_en: A sense of the serendipity and gratitude for everyday beauty

options.text: 他觉得自己只是房间的一部分
options.pinyin: tā juéde zìjǐ zhǐshì fángjiān de yī bùfen
options.text_en: He feels he is just part of the room

questions.explanation: The author doesn't pursue beauty; the light arrives on its own schedule, and he 'happens to live inside that story'—serendipity and quiet gratitude.

title: The Rhythm of a Crosswalk
text_zh: 每个城市的人行横道都有自己的节奏。在北京，绿灯亮起的时候，人群像决堤的水一样涌出去，步伐快而坚定，因为红灯时间长，大家都憋了一口气。在成都，过马路的速度明显慢一些，走到一半的人可能会因为接了个电话而放慢脚步，后面的人也不着急，绕过去就好了。在上海，人们走得快但有秩序，像一条条平行的溪流，很少有人走出自己的车道。我最喜欢的是一座南方小城的人行横道。那里没有红绿灯，只有地上画的白色斑马线。车让人，人也让车，彼此之间有一种不需要信号灯来调解的默契。有一次我看到一个老人推着自行车过马路，走到一半停下来系鞋带。三辆车安静地等着他，没有人按喇叭。他系完鞋带站起来，朝司机们点了点头，然后不慌不忙地走完了剩下的路。那个场景让我觉得，一个城市的文明程度，不在于它修了多少高楼和地铁，而在于一个老人能不能从容地在马路中间系鞋带。
text_pinyin: Měi gè chéngshì de rénxíng héngdào dōu yǒu zìjǐ de jiézòu. Zài Běijīng, lǜdēng liàng qǐ de shíhou, rénqún xiàng juédī de shuǐ yīyàng yǒng chūqù, bùfá kuài ér jiāndìng, yīnwèi hóngdēng shíjiān cháng, dàjiā dōu biē le yī kǒu qì. Zài Chéngdū, guò mǎlù de sùdù míngxiǎn màn yīxiē, zǒu dào yī bàn de rén kěnéng huì yīnwèi jiē le gè diànhuà ér fàngmàn jiǎobù, hòumian de rén yě bù zhāojí, rào guòqù jiù hǎo le. Zài Shànghǎi, rénmen zǒu de kuài dàn yǒu zhìxù, xiàng yī tiáo tiáo píngxíng de xīliú, hěn shǎo yǒu rén zǒu chū zìjǐ de chēdào. Wǒ zuì xǐhuan de shì yī zuò nánfāng xiǎo chéng de rénxíng héngdào. Nàlǐ méiyǒu hónglǜdēng, zhǐyǒu dì shàng huà de báisè bānmǎxiàn. Chē ràng rén, rén yě ràng chē, bǐcǐ zhī jiān yǒu yī zhǒng bù xūyào xìnhào dēng lái tiáojiě de mòqì. Yǒu yī cì wǒ kàn dào yī gè lǎorén tuī zhe zìxíngchē guò mǎlù, zǒu dào yī bàn tíng xiàlái jì xiédài. Sān liàng chē ānjìng de děng zhe tā, méiyǒu rén àn lǎba. Tā jì wán xiédài zhàn qǐlái, cháo sījīmen diǎn le diǎn tóu, ránhòu bù huāng bù máng de zǒu wán le shèng xià de lù. Nàge chǎngjǐng ràng wǒ juéde, yī gè chéngshì de wénmíng chéngdù, bú zàiyú tā xiū le duōshǎo gāolóu hé dìtiě, ér zàiyú yī gè lǎorén néng bù néng cóngróng de zài mǎlù zhōngjiān jì xiédài.
text_en: Every city's crosswalk has its own rhythm. In Beijing, when the light turns green, the crowd surges out like water breaking through a dam—steps fast and determined, because the red light lasts so long everyone has been holding their breath. In Chengdu, the pace of crossing is noticeably slower; someone halfway across might slow down to answer a phone call, and those behind aren't bothered—they just go around. In Shanghai, people walk fast but orderly, like parallel streams, rarely straying from their lane. My favorite is a crosswalk in a small southern city. There are no traffic lights, just white zebra stripes painted on the ground. Cars yield to people, people yield to cars—between them a mutual understanding that needs no signal light to mediate. Once I watched an elderly man push his bicycle across the street and stop halfway to tie his shoelace. Three cars waited quietly; no one honked. He finished tying his shoe, stood up, nodded to the drivers, then walked the rest of the way without hurrying.

questions.q_zh: 作者认为城市文明程度的标准是什么？
questions.q_en: What does the author think is the measure of a city's civilization?

options.pinyin: xiū le duōshǎo gāolóu hé dìtiě
options.text_en: How many skyscrapers and subways it's built

options.text: 老人能不能从容地在马路中间系鞋带
options.pinyin: lǎorén néng bù néng cóngróng de zài mǎlù zhōngjiān jì xiédài
options.text_en: Whether an old man can calmly tie his shoelace in the middle of the road

options.pinyin: jiāotōng xìnhào dēng shìfǒu wánshàn
options.text_en: Whether traffic signals are well-equipped

questions.explanation: Civilization isn't measured by speed but by patience: can an old man calmly tie his shoe mid-crossing without being honked at? That's the test.

questions.q_zh: 文中提到的南方小城的人行横道有什么特点？
questions.q_en: What is special about the crosswalk in the small southern city?

options.text: 有先进的智能红绿灯系统
options.pinyin: yǒu xiānjìn de zhìnéng hónglǜdēng xìtǒng
options.text_en: It has an advanced smart traffic light system

options.text: 没有红绿灯，靠车和人之间的默契
options.pinyin: méiyǒu hónglǜdēng, kào chē hé rén zhī jiān de mòqì
options.text_en: No traffic lights—it works on mutual understanding between cars and people

options.pinyin: xíngrén guò mǎlù sùdù hěn kuài
options.text_en: Pedestrians cross very quickly

questions.explanation: The small southern city's crosswalk has no traffic lights—cars and pedestrians negotiate passage through mutual reading of intention. Trust replaces regulation.

title: The Night Market's Geography
text_zh: 夜市摊位的排列不是随机的，而是几十年地盘争夺的均衡态。烧烤摊占据上风口，因为老陈三十年前就站在那儿，谁都别想动他。水果摊守入口，利润率高扛得住最贵的摊位费。面条摊藏在最深处——走到那里的人通常已经饿了，成交率最高。但夜市真正的风景不是这些，而是两个烧烤摊之间的冷战。老王和老刘，摊位紧挨，菜单几乎一模一样，二十年来从不说话。他们各自摆着相同的烤串、相同的啤酒、相同的塑料凳，价格永远差一毛钱——谁先降价，对方半小时内跟上。客人走错摊位，双方都假装没看见。我观察了一个月，从没见过他们有任何交流。直到有一天晚上城管来了。两个人同时收摊，动作配合之默契仿佛排练过一百次——先收招牌、再叠桌子、最后推车。城管走后，他们同时摆回去，先推车、再支桌子、最后挂招牌。然后继续假装对方不存在。
text_pinyin: Yèshì tānwèi de páiliè bú shì suíjī de, ér shì jǐ shí nián dìpán zhēngduó de jūnhéng tài. Shāokǎo tān zhànjù shàngfēngkǒu, yīnwèi Lǎo Chén sānshí nián qián jiù zhàn zài nàr, shéi dōu bié xiǎng dòng tā. Shuǐguǒ tān shǒu rùkǒu, lìrùnlǜ gāo kàng de zhù zuì guì de tānwèi fèi. Miàntiáo tān cáng zài zuì shēn chù — zǒu dào nàlǐ de rén tōngcháng yǐjīng è le, chéngjiāolǜ zuì gāo. Dàn yèshì zhēnzhèng de fēngjǐng bú shì zhèxiē, ér shì liǎng ge shāokǎo tān zhījiān de lěngzhàn. Lǎo Wáng hé Lǎo Liú, tānwèi jǐn āi, càidān jīhū yì mú yíyàng, èrshí nián lái cóng bù shuōhuà. Tāmen gèzì bǎi zhe xiāngtóng de kǎochuàn, xiāngtóng de píjiǔ, xiāngtóng de sùliào dèng, jiàgé yǒngyuǎn chā yì máo qián — shéi xiān jiàngjià, duìfāng bàn xiǎoshí nèi gēn shàng. Kèrén zǒu cuò tānwèi, shuāngfāng dōu jiǎzhuāng méi kànjiàn. Wǒ guānchá le yí ge yuè, cóng méi jiàn guò tāmen yǒu rènhé jiāoliú. Zhídào yǒu yì tiān wǎnshang chéngguǎn lái le. Liǎng ge rén tóngshí shōu tān, dòngzuò pèihé zhī mòqì fǎngfú páiliàn guò yì bǎi cì — xiān shōu zhāopai, zài dié zhuōzi, zuìhòu tuī chē. Chéngguǎn zǒu hòu, tāmen tóngshí bǎi huíqù, xiān tuī chē, zài zhī zhuōzi, zuìhòu guà zhāopai. Ránhòu jìxù jiǎzhuāng duìfāng bù cúnzài.
text_en: Night market stall placement isn't random—it's the equilibrium of decades of territorial negotiation. Barbecue stalls occupy the upwind position because Old Chen has been in that spot for thirty years and nobody dares move him. Fruit stalls hold the entrance—high margins absorb the highest stall fees. Noodle stalls hide in the back, where anyone who walks that far is already hungry. But the real scenery isn't the layout. It's the cold war between two barbecue stalls. Old Wang and Old Liu sit side by side, menus nearly identical, twenty years of not speaking. They each display the same skewers, the same beer, the same plastic stools. Their prices are always one mao apart—whoever drops first, the other matches within half an hour. When a customer sits at the wrong stall, both pretend not to notice. I watched for a month and never saw them exchange a word. Then one evening city inspectors arrived. Both packed up simultaneously, their coordination seamless as if rehearsed a hundred times—signs first, then tables, then carts. After the inspectors left, both set up again simultaneously—carts first, then tables, then signs. Then resumed pretending the other didn't exist.

questions.q_zh: 夜市摊位的排列有什么规律？
questions.q_en: What pattern governs the arrangement of night market stalls?

options.pinyin: wánquán suíjī páiliè
options.text_en: Completely random arrangement

options.text: 按照一种看不见的秩序：烧烤在上风口、水果在入口、小炒在最深处
options.pinyin: ànzhào yī zhǒng kàn bú jiàn de zhìxù: shāokǎo zài shàng fēngkǒu, shuǐguǒ zài rùkǒu, xiǎo chǎo zài zuì shēnchù
options.text_en: Following an invisible order: barbecue upwind, fruit at entrance, stir-fry at the back

options.pinyin: yóu zhèngfǔ tǒngyī guīhuà de
options.text_en: Uniformly planned by the government

questions.explanation: Stalls follow an invisible logic: barbecue goes upwind (smoke dispersal), fruit at the entrance (visual appeal), stir-fry at the back (seated eating).

questions.q_zh: 作者认为夜市证明了什么？
questions.q_en: What does the author think the night market proves?

options.text: 小商贩之间的竞争很激烈
options.pinyin: xiǎo shāngfàn zhī jiān de jìngzhēng hěn jīliè
options.text_en: Competition among small vendors is intense

options.text: 即使在商业关系中，人情味也可以是主要的粘合剂
options.pinyin: jíshǐ zài shāngyè guānxi zhōng, rénqíngwèi yě kěyǐ shì zhǔyào de niánhéjì
options.text_en: Even in commercial relationships, human warmth can be the primary adhesive

options.pinyin: chuántǒng yèshì zhèngzài xiāoshī
options.text_en: Traditional night markets are disappearing

questions.explanation: Despite being commercial relationships, the market runs on human warmth—banter, loyalty, familiarity—rather than contracts or regulations.

title: The Sound Map of a Neighborhood
text_zh: 如果给我住的这个街区画一张声音地图，它大概是这样的：东边是一所小学，每天早上七点半开始有孩子的笑声和读书声，上课铃声在八点整准时响起，像一个声音的分水岭，把喧闹和安静一刀切开。南边是一条商业街，从早到晚都有各种声音——店铺音箱播放的促销广播、外卖骑手的电动车鸣笛、快递员大喊「某某某你的快递到了」。西边是一片老旧的居民区，声音比较碎：锅铲炒菜的声音、电视剧的对白、偶尔从某个窗口飘出来的二胡声——那是退休的赵大爷在练琴。北边是一个小公园，它的声音最有层次：晨练老人的太极拳配乐、鸟叫、风吹过竹林的沙沙声、下午棋友下棋时的争论声。我住在这四种声音区域的交界处，根据听到的声音，不用看钟就大致知道现在是几点。如果听到小学的上课铃声和商业街的开门声同时出现，那就是早上八点。如果听到赵大爷的二胡和公园里的棋友在争论，那大概是下午三四点。声音是城市最真实的脉搏。你可以改变一条街的外观——拆了重建、刷上新漆——但你很难改变它的声音。声音是属于居民的，不属于规划者。
text_pinyin: Rúguǒ gěi wǒ zhù de zhège jiēqū huà yī zhāng shēngyīn dìtú, tā dàgài shì zhèyàng de: dōngbiān shì yī suǒ xiǎoxué, měi tiān zǎoshang qī diǎn bàn kāishǐ yǒu háizi de xiào shēng hé dúshū shēng, shàngkè língshēng zài bā diǎn zhěng zhǔnshí xiǎng qǐ, xiàng yī gè shēngyīn de fēnshuǐlǐng, bǎ xuānnào hé ānjìng yī dāo qiē kāi. Nánbiān shì yī tiáo shāngyè jiē, cóng zǎo dào wǎn dōu yǒu gè zhǒng shēngyīn——diànpù yīnxiāng bōfàng de cùxiāo guǎngbō, wàimài qíshǒu de diàndòngchē míngdí, kuàidì yuán dà hǎn「mǒumǒumǒu nǐ de kuàidì dào le」. Xībiān shì yī piàn lǎojiù de jūmínqū, shēngyīn bǐjiào suì: guōchǎn chǎo cài de shēngyīn, diànshìjù de duìbái, ǒu'ěr cóng mǒu gè chuāngkǒu piāo chūlái de èrhú shēng——nà shì tuìxiū de Zhào dàyé zài liàn qín. Běibiān shì yī gè xiǎo gōngyuán, tā de shēngyīn zuì yǒu céngcì: chénliàn lǎorén de tàijíquán pèiyuè, niǎo jiào, fēng chuī guò zhúlín de shāshā shēng, xiàwǔ qíyǒu xià qí shí de zhēnglùn shēng. Wǒ zhù zài zhè sì zhǒng shēngyīn qūyù de jiāojièchù, gēnjù tīng dào de shēngyīn, búyòng kàn zhōng jiù dàzhì zhīdào xiànzài shì jǐ diǎn. Rúguǒ tīng dào xiǎoxué de shàngkè língshēng hé shāngyè jiē de kāimén shēng tóngshí chūxiàn, nà jiùshì zǎoshang bā diǎn. Rúguǒ tīng dào Zhào dàyé de èrhú hé gōngyuán lǐ de qíyǒu zài zhēnglùn, nà dàgài shì xiàwǔ sān sì diǎn. Shēngyīn shì chéngshì zuì zhēnshí de màibó. Nǐ kěyǐ gǎibiàn yī tiáo jiē de wàiguān——chāi le chóngjiàn, shuā shàng xīn qī——dàn nǐ hěn nán gǎibiàn tā de shēngyīn. Shēngyīn shì shǔyú jūmín de, bù shǔyú guīhuà zhě.
text_en: If I were to draw a sound map of my neighborhood, it would look roughly like this: to the east is an elementary school—every morning at seven-thirty come children's laughter and reading aloud; the class bell rings at exactly eight, like an acoustic watershed cleaving noise from silence. To the south runs a commercial street, noisy from dawn to dusk—shop speakers blasting promotions, delivery riders honking electric scooters, couriers shouting 'So-and-so, your package is here!' To the west lies an old residential area with fragmented sounds: the clang of wok spatulas stir-frying, TV drama dialogue, and occasionally from some window the drifting notes of an erhu—retired Grandpa Zhao practicing his instrument. To the north is a small park with the most layered soundscape: tai chi music for morning exercisers, birdsong, the rustle of wind through bamboo groves, and afternoon arguments between chess partners. I live at the junction of these four sound zones and can roughly tell the time by what I hear, without looking at a clock. If the school bell and the commercial street's door-opening sounds appear simultaneously, it's eight a.m. If I hear Grandpa Zhao's erhu and the park chess players arguing, it's around three or four p.m. Sound is a city's truest pulse. You can change a street's appearance—tear it down, rebuild, slap on fresh paint—but you can hardly change its sound. Sound belongs to the residents, not to the planners.

questions.q_zh: 作者怎样不看钟就知道时间？
questions.q_en: How does the author tell time without looking at a clock?

options.text: 通过周围不同区域的声音组合来判断
options.pinyin: tōngguò zhōuwéi bùtóng qūyù de shēngyīn zǔhé lái pànduàn
options.text_en: By judging from the combination of sounds from different surrounding areas

options.pinyin: tōngguò tàiyáng de wèizhì
options.text_en: By the position of the sun

options.pinyin: tōngguò jiē shàng rén de duōshǎo
options.text_en: By the number of people on the street

questions.explanation: The author identifies time by layering sounds from different sources: school bells, kitchen clattering, mahjong tiles. Each hour has a distinct acoustic profile.

questions.q_zh: 作者为什么说「声音是属于居民的，不属于规划者」？
questions.q_en: Why does the author say 'sound belongs to the residents, not to the planners'?

options.text: 因为声音是居民日常生活自然产生的，无法被规划改变
options.pinyin: yīnwèi shēngyīn shì jūmín rìcháng shēnghuó zìrán chǎnshēng de, wúfǎ bèi guīhuà gǎibiàn
options.text_en: Because sound is naturally produced by residents' daily life and can't be changed by planning

options.text: 因为规划者不关心噪音问题
options.pinyin: yīnwèi guīhuà zhě bù guānxīn zàoyīn wèntí
options.text_en: Because planners don't care about noise issues

options.text: 因为居民会抗议城市改造
options.pinyin: yīnwèi jūmín huì kàngyì chéngshì gǎizào
options.text_en: Because residents protest urban renovation

questions.explanation: Sound emerges organically from residents' daily routines. Urban planners design spaces, but the acoustic character belongs to the people who live there.

title: The Corner Store That Never Changes
text_zh: 我家楼下的街角有一家小卖部，开了至少二十年。这些年来，周围的一切都变了：隔壁的理发店变成了奶茶店，对面的书报亭消失了，马路对面盖起了一栋商业中心。但这家小卖部还在，而且几乎没有变过。同样的玻璃柜台，同样的塑料凳子，同样的货架上摆着同样品牌的矿泉水、方便面和老式汽水。老板还是那个姓刘的大叔，只是头发白了一些，肚子大了一圈。我有时候觉得这家小卖部不是一个商店，而是一个时间锚点。当你从外面的世界回来，被各种变化搅得有点晕的时候，看到它还在那里，还是老样子，心里就会踏实一些。就像每个人心里都需要一个不会变的东西，来证明自己的记忆没有出错。有一次我跟刘大叔聊天，问他为什么不装修一下，也卖点时髦的东西。他想了想说：「我这种店就是给不想做选择的人准备的。他们下楼来买瓶水，不想面对二十种选择，就想要那种他们从小喝到大的，拿了就走。」我听完觉得他说得很对。在一个选择越来越多的世界里，不给你选择，本身就是一种服务。
text_pinyin: Wǒ jiā lóu xià de jiējiǎo yǒu yī jiā xiǎomàibù, kāi le zhìshǎo èrshí nián. Zhèxiē nián lái, zhōuwéi de yīqiè dōu biàn le: gébì de lǐfǎdiàn biànchéng le nǎichá diàn, duìmiàn de shūbàotíng xiāoshī le, mǎlù duìmiàn gài qǐ le yī dòng shāngyè zhōngxīn. Dàn zhè jiā xiǎomàibù hái zài, érqiě jīhū méiyǒu biàn guò. Tóngyàng de bōli guìtái, tóngyàng de sùliào dèngzi, tóngyàng de huòjià shàng bǎi zhe tóngyàng pǐnpái de kuàngquánshuǐ, fāngbiànmiàn hé lǎoshì qìshuǐ. Lǎobǎn háishi nàge xìng Liú de dàshū, zhǐshì tóufa bái le yīxiē, dùzi dà le yī quān. Wǒ yǒu shíhou juéde zhè jiā xiǎomàibù bú shì yī gè shāngdiàn, ér shì yī gè shíjiān máodiǎn. Dāng nǐ cóng wàimiàn de shìjiè huílái, bèi gè zhǒng biànhuà jiǎo de yǒudiǎn yūn de shíhou, kàn dào tā hái zài nàlǐ, háishi lǎo yàngzi, xīnlǐ jiù huì tāshi yīxiē. Jiù xiàng měi gè rén xīnlǐ dōu xūyào yī gè bú huì biàn de dōngxi, lái zhèngmíng zìjǐ de jìyì méiyǒu chū cuò. Yǒu yī cì wǒ gēn Liú dàshū liáotiān, wèn tā wèishéme bù zhuāngxiū yīxià, yě mài diǎn shímáo de dōngxi. Tā xiǎng le xiǎng shuō: 「Wǒ zhè zhǒng diàn jiùshì gěi bù xiǎng zuò xuǎnzé de rén zhǔnbèi de. Tāmen xià lóu lái mǎi píng shuǐ, bù xiǎng miànduì èrshí zhǒng xuǎnzé, jiù xiǎng yào nà zhǒng tāmen cóng xiǎo hē dào dà de, ná le jiù zǒu.」Wǒ tīng wán juéde tā shuō de hěn duì. Zài yī gè xuǎnzé yuè lái yuè duō de shìjiè lǐ, bù gěi nǐ xuǎnzé, běnshēn jiùshì yī zhǒng fúwù.
text_en: On the corner downstairs from my apartment is a convenience store that's been open at least twenty years. Everything around it has changed over the years: the barbershop next door became a milk tea shop, the newsstand across the way disappeared, a commercial center rose on the opposite side of the road. But this little store remains, virtually unchanged. Same glass counter, same plastic stools, same shelf brands of mineral water, instant noodles, and old-fashioned soda. The owner is still that Uncle Liu, just with whiter hair and a slightly larger belly. Sometimes I feel this store isn't a shop but a temporal anchor point. When you come back from the outside world, a bit dizzy from all the changes, seeing it still there, looking the same, settles your heart. It's as if everyone needs one unchanging thing to prove that their memory hasn't gone wrong. Once I chatted with Uncle Liu and asked why he didn't renovate and sell some trendy products. He thought about it and said: 'My kind of store is for people who don't want to make choices. They come downstairs to buy a bottle of water, don't want to face twenty options—they just want the kind they've been drinking since childhood, grab it and go.' I thought he was absolutely right.

questions.q_zh: 作者为什么把这家小卖部比作「时间锚点」？
questions.q_en: Why does the author compare this store to a 'temporal anchor point'?

options.text: 因为它在变化的环境中保持不变，让人心里踏实
options.pinyin: yīnwèi tā zài biànhuà de huánjìng zhōng bǎochí bú biàn, ràng rén xīnlǐ tāshi
options.text_en: Because it stays unchanged in a changing environment, giving people a sense of stability

options.text: 因为它卖的都是老式商品
options.pinyin: yīnwèi tā mài de dōu shì lǎoshì shāngpǐn
options.text_en: Because it sells old-fashioned products

options.pinyin: yīnwèi tā wèiyú jiējiǎo
options.text_en: Because it's located on the corner

questions.explanation: Surrounded by constant change, the unchanged store provides a temporal reference point—proof that not everything shifts, giving people psychological stability.

questions.q_zh: 刘大叔为什么不做任何改变？
questions.q_en: Why doesn't Uncle Liu make any changes?

options.pinyin: tā méiyǒu qián zhuāngxiū
options.text_en: He doesn't have money to renovate

options.text: 他的店是为不想做选择的人服务的
options.pinyin: tā de diàn shì wèi bù xiǎng zuò xuǎnzé de rén fúwù de
options.text_en: His store serves people who don't want to make choices

options.pinyin: tā jíjiāng tuìxiū
options.text_en: He's about to retire

questions.explanation: Uncle Liu's customers don't want options or novelty. His store serves people who find comfort in the absence of choice—simplicity as service.

title: The Bus Stop at Midnight
text_zh: 晚上十一点以后的公交车站是一个完全不同的空间。白天它是一个功能性的存在——人们来了又走，看站牌、查手机、等车、上车。但深夜的公交站变成了一个收容所，收容着所有还没有回家的人。等末班车的人很少说话。他们各自坐在候车椅上，或者靠着站牌柱子，做着各自的事。有人在看手机，屏幕的蓝光照着一张疲惫的脸。有人在抽烟，烟头的红点在夜里一明一灭。有人只是闭着眼睛，但不是在睡觉，更像是在屏蔽一天的噪音。我注意到一个有趣的现象：深夜的公交站里，人和人之间的距离会自动拉近。白天排队等车，人与人保持至少一臂的距离。但深夜的时候，两个陌生人可能会并肩坐在同一张椅子上，不交谈，但也不介意对方的存在。也许是因为深夜的城市让人感到微弱的不安全感，陌生人的存在反而变成了一种慰藉。「我不是唯一一个还没回家的人」——这个事实本身就够温暖了。末班车终于来了的时候，所有人都站起来，像一个临时的社团完成了它的使命。明天他们不会在白天的公交站认出彼此。午夜的连接，只属于午夜。
text_pinyin: Wǎnshang shíyī diǎn yǐhòu de gōngjiāo chēzhàn shì yī gè wánquán bùtóng de kōngjiān. Báitiān tā shì yī gè gōngnéng xìng de cúnzài——rénmen lái le yòu zǒu, kàn zhànpái, chá shǒujī, děng chē, shàng chē. Dàn shēnyè de gōngjiāo zhàn biànchéng le yī gè shōuróng suǒ, shōuróng zhe suǒyǒu hái méiyǒu huí jiā de rén. Děng mòbān chē de rén hěn shǎo shuōhuà. Tāmen gèzì zuò zài hòuchē yǐ shàng, huòzhě kào zhe zhànpái zhùzi, zuò zhe gèzì de shì. Yǒu rén zài kàn shǒujī, píngmù de lán guāng zhào zhe yī zhāng píbèi de liǎn. Yǒu rén zài chōuyān, yāntóu de hóng diǎn zài yè lǐ yī míng yī miè. Yǒu rén zhǐshì bì zhe yǎnjīng, dàn bú shì zài shuìjiào, gèng xiàng shì zài píngbì yī tiān de zàoyīn. Wǒ zhùyì dào yī gè yǒuqù de xiànxiàng: shēnyè de gōngjiāo zhàn lǐ, rén hé rén zhī jiān de jùlí huì zìdòng lā jìn. Báitiān páiduì děng chē, rén yǔ rén bǎochí zhìshǎo yī bì de jùlí. Dàn shēnyè de shíhou, liǎng gè mòshēng rén kěnéng huì bìngjiān zuò zài tóng yī zhāng yǐzi shàng, bù jiāotán, dàn yě bú jièyì duìfāng de cúnzài. Yěxǔ shì yīnwèi shēnyè de chéngshì ràng rén gǎndào wēiruò de bù ānquán gǎn, mòshēng rén de cúnzài fǎn'ér biànchéng le yī zhǒng wèijiè. 「Wǒ bú shì wéiyī yī gè hái méi huí jiā de rén」——zhège shìshí běnshēn jiù gòu wēnnuǎn le. Mòbān chē zhōngyú lái le de shíhou, suǒyǒu rén dōu zhàn qǐlái, xiàng yī gè línshí de shètuán wánchéng le tā de shǐmìng. Míngtiān tāmen bú huì zài báitiān de gōngjiāo zhàn rèn chū bǐcǐ. Wǔyè de liánjiē, zhǐ shǔyú wǔyè.
text_en: After eleven p.m., a bus stop becomes a completely different space. By day it's purely functional—people come and go, check the route sign, look at phones, wait, board. But at night the bus stop transforms into a shelter, harboring everyone who hasn't yet gone home. People waiting for the last bus rarely speak. They each sit on the waiting bench or lean against the stop's pillar, doing their own thing. Someone scrolls their phone, the screen's blue glow illuminating a weary face. Someone smokes, the cigarette's red tip flickering in the darkness. Someone simply closes their eyes—not sleeping, more like muting the day's noise. I noticed an interesting phenomenon: at a late-night bus stop, the distance between people naturally shrinks. During the day, people queuing for a bus maintain at least an arm's length. But late at night, two strangers might sit shoulder to shoulder on the same bench, not talking but not minding each other's presence. Perhaps the city at night creates a faint sense of insecurity, and a stranger's presence becomes solace instead. 'I'm not the only one who hasn't gone home'—that fact alone is warm enough. When the last bus finally arrives, everyone stands up, like a temporary society that has fulfilled its mission. Tomorrow they won't recognize each other at the daytime bus stop. Midnight's connection belongs only to midnight.

questions.q_zh: 深夜的公交站和白天相比有什么不同？
questions.q_en: How is the late-night bus stop different from the daytime one?

options.pinyin: rén gèng duō le
options.text_en: There are more people

options.text: 人与人之间的距离自动拉近，陌生人可以并肩而坐
options.pinyin: rén yǔ rén zhī jiān de jùlí zìdòng lā jìn, mòshēng rén kěyǐ bìngjiān ér zuò
options.text_en: The distance between people naturally shrinks; strangers can sit shoulder to shoulder

options.pinyin: rénmen gèng xǐhuan jiāotán
options.text_en: People are more likely to chat

questions.explanation: Late at night, personal-space rules relax. Strangers who'd maintain distance by day can sit shoulder to shoulder without discomfort.

questions.q_zh: 「午夜的连接只属于午夜」是什么意思？
questions.q_en: What does 'midnight's connection belongs only to midnight' mean?

options.text: 深夜陌生人之间的联系是短暂的，白天不会延续
options.pinyin: shēnyè mòshēng rén zhī jiān de liánxì shì duǎnzàn de, báitiān bú huì yánxù
options.text_en: The bond between strangers at night is brief and doesn't continue by day

options.text: 午夜的公交车比白天的更方便
options.pinyin: wǔyè de gōngjiāo chē bǐ báitiān de gèng fāngbiàn
options.text_en: Midnight buses are more convenient than daytime ones

options.pinyin: zhǐyǒu wǔyè cái yǒu mòbān chē
options.text_en: Only midnight has the last bus

questions.explanation: The connection formed between strangers at midnight is temporary and context-dependent—it exists only in that hour and doesn't carry over to daylight.

title: The Alley Cat's Territory
text_zh: 我住的这条巷子里有大约七八只流浪猫。经过长时间的观察，我发现它们对这条巷子进行了非常精确的领地划分。一只橘猫占据了巷口到第一个垃圾桶之间的区域，那里经过的行人最多，投食的概率最高。一只黑白花猫控制着两栋居民楼之间的夹缝，那里狭窄但安全，下雨也淋不到。一只灰色老猫盘踞在巷子尽头的一个废弃花坛上，居高临下，适合睡觉和监视整条巷子。这些领地的边界是流动的、模糊的，但猫们都心知肚明。偶尔会有越界事件发生——通常是新来的猫不了解规矩，走进了别人的地盘。这时候原住民会发出低沉的警告声，不是立刻攻击，而是给对方一个退出的机会。大部分时候，入侵者会识趣地退回去。我观察到最有意思的一幕是在下雨天。当暴雨来临，所有的领地概念都暂时失效了。猫们会挤在同一个屋檐下，彼此之间保持着最小的距离，谁也不发出威胁的声音。等雨一停，它们立刻各自回到自己的领地，仿佛刚才的亲密不曾发生。这跟人类社会何其相似——危机让我们团结，安全让我们划清界限。
text_pinyin: Wǒ zhù de zhè tiáo xiàngzi lǐ yǒu dàyuē qī bā zhī liúlàng māo. Jīngguò cháng shíjiān de guānchá, wǒ fāxiàn tāmen duì zhè tiáo xiàngzi jìnxíng le fēicháng jīngquè de lǐngdì huáfēn. Yī zhī jú māo zhànjù le xiàng kǒu dào dì yī gè lājī tǒng zhī jiān de qūyù, nàlǐ jīngguò de xíngrén zuì duō, tóu shí de gàilǜ zuì gāo. Yī zhī hēi bái huā māo kòngzhì zhe liǎng dòng jūmínlóu zhī jiān de jiāfèng, nàlǐ xiázhǎi dàn ānquán, xià yǔ yě lín bú dào. Yī zhī huīsè lǎo māo pánjù zài xiàngzi jìntóu de yī gè fèiqì huātán shàng, jūgāolínxià, shìhé shuìjiào hé jiānshì zhěng tiáo xiàngzi. Zhèxiē lǐngdì de biānjiè shì liúdòng de, móhu de, dàn māomen dōu xīnzhī dùmíng. Ǒu'ěr huì yǒu yuèjiè shìjiàn fāshēng——tōngcháng shì xīn lái de māo bù liǎojiě guīju, zǒu jìn le biéren de dìpán. Zhè shíhou yuánzhùmín huì fāchū dīchén de jǐnggào shēng, bú shì lìkè gōngjī, ér shì gěi duìfāng yī gè tuìchū de jīhuì. Dà bùfen shíhou, qīnlüè zhě huì shíqù de tuì huíqù. Wǒ guānchá dào zuì yǒu yìsi de yī mù shì zài xià yǔ tiān. Dāng bàoyǔ láilín, suǒyǒu de lǐngdì gàiniàn dōu zànshí shīxiào le. Māomen huì jǐ zài tóng yī gè wūyán xià, bǐcǐ zhī jiān bǎochí zhe zuìxiǎo de jùlí, shéi yě bù fāchū wēixié de shēngyīn. Děng yǔ yī tíng, tāmen lìkè gèzì huí dào zìjǐ de lǐngdì, fǎngfú gāngcái de qīnmì bù céng fāshēng. Zhè gēn rénlèi shèhuì héqí xiāngsì——wēijī ràng wǒmen tuánjié, ānquán ràng wǒmen huà qīng jièxiàn.
text_en: There are roughly seven or eight stray cats in my alley. After long observation, I discovered they've partitioned it with remarkable precision. An orange cat occupies the zone from the alley entrance to the first trash bin—the highest foot traffic and best odds of being fed. A black-and-white cat controls the narrow gap between two residential buildings—tight but safe, sheltered from rain. An old gray cat perches atop an abandoned flower bed at the alley's end—elevated, ideal for sleeping and surveilling the entire lane. These territorial boundaries are fluid and blurry, but the cats all know them by heart. Occasionally there are border crossings—usually a newcomer unfamiliar with the rules wandering into someone else's turf. The resident will issue a low warning sound—not an immediate attack, but giving the other a chance to withdraw. Most of the time, the intruder wisely retreats. The most interesting scene I observed was during rain. When a downpour arrives, all territorial concepts temporarily collapse. The cats squeeze under the same eave, maintaining minimal distance from each other, no one making threatening sounds. The moment the rain stops, they immediately return to their own territories, as if the closeness never happened.

questions.q_zh: 橘猫为什么占据巷口到垃圾桶之间的区域？
questions.q_en: Why does the orange cat occupy the area from the alley entrance to the trash bin?
