title: The Elderly Couple on the Bench
text_zh: 每天上午十点，公园入口旁边的长椅上总会出现一对老两口。他们来的时候，老先生走在前面，手里拎着一个布袋，老太太走在后面，步伐稍慢。到了长椅前，老先生会先用手帕把椅面擦一遍，然后扶着老太太坐下。他从布袋里拿出两个保温杯、一包花生和一份报纸。老太太喝茶，老先生看报。他们很少说话，偶尔老先生会念一段新闻给老太太听，老太太就嗯一声，表示知道了。有时候老太太会轻轻拍一下老先生的手背，指一指远处某棵开了花的树。老先生就抬头看看，点点头。整个过程安静得像是一幅画的两个局部在缓慢移动。十一点半的时候，老先生会把东西收进布袋，站起来拍拍裤子，然后伸出手。老太太握住他的手站起来，两个人按照来的时候相反的顺序走回去——老太太在前，老先生在后，走得更慢一些。我看了他们整整一个春天。一对七八十岁的老人，用一种几乎无声的方式彼此照顾，这种安静不是冷淡，而是经过了所有言语之后剩下的默契。最深的温柔，往往看起来最平淡。
text_pinyin: Měitiān shàngwǔ shí diǎn, gōngyuán rùkǒu pángbiān de chángyǐ shàng zǒng huì chūxiàn yí duì lǎo liǎng kǒu. Tāmen lái de shíhou, lǎo xiānsheng zǒu zài qiánmiàn, shǒu lǐ līn zhe yí gè bù dài, lǎo tàitai zǒu zài hòumiàn, bùfá shāo màn. Dào le chángyǐ qián, lǎo xiānsheng huì xiān yòng shǒupà bǎ yǐ miàn cā yí biàn, ránhòu fú zhe lǎo tàitai zuò xià. Tā cóng bù dài lǐ ná chū liǎng gè bǎowēn bēi, yì bāo huāshēng hé yí fèn bàozhǐ. Lǎo tàitai hē chá, lǎo xiānsheng kàn bào. Tāmen hěn shǎo shuōhuà, ǒu'ěr lǎo xiānsheng huì niàn yí duàn xīnwén gěi lǎo tàitai tīng, lǎo tàitai jiù ēn yì shēng, biǎoshì zhīdào le. Yǒu shíhou lǎo tàitai huì qīngqīng pāi yíxià lǎo xiānsheng de shǒubèi, zhǐ yi zhǐ yuǎnchù mǒu kē kāi le huā de shù. Lǎo xiānsheng jiù táitóu kàn kàn, diǎndiǎn tóu. Zhěnggè guòchéng ānjìng de xiàng shì yí fú huà de liǎng gè júbù zài huǎnmàn yídòng. Shíyī diǎn bàn de shíhou, lǎo xiānsheng huì bǎ dōngxi shōu jìn bù dài, zhàn qǐlái pāipai kùzi, ránhòu shēn chū shǒu. Lǎo tàitai wò zhù tā de shǒu zhàn qǐlái, liǎng gè rén ànzhào lái de shíhou xiāngfǎn de shùnxù zǒu huíqu — lǎo tàitai zài qián, lǎo xiānsheng zài hòu, zǒu de gèng màn yìxiē. Wǒ kàn le tāmen zhěngzhěng yí gè chūntiān. Yí duì qībāshí suì de lǎorén, yòng yì zhǒng jīhū wúshēng de fāngshì bǐcǐ zhàogù, zhè zhǒng ānjìng bú shì lěngdàn, ér shì jīngguò le suǒyǒu yányǔ zhīhòu shèng xià de mòqì. Zuì shēn de wēnróu, wǎngwǎng kàn qǐlái zuì píngdàn.
text_en: Every morning at ten, an elderly couple appears on the bench by the park entrance. When they arrive, the old gentleman walks ahead carrying a cloth bag, the old woman follows behind at a slightly slower pace. At the bench, the gentleman first wipes the seat with a handkerchief, then helps his wife sit down. He takes out two thermos cups, a bag of peanuts, and a newspaper from the cloth bag. She drinks tea; he reads the paper. They rarely speak. Occasionally the gentleman reads a piece of news to her, and she hums an acknowledgment. Sometimes she gently taps the back of his hand and points to some distant tree in bloom. He looks up, nods. The whole scene is as quiet as two parts of a painting slowly shifting. At eleven-thirty, the gentleman packs everything back into the bag, stands up, brushes off his trousers, and extends his hand. She takes it and stands. The two walk back in reverse order from when they came — she in front, he behind, a bit more slowly. I watched them for an entire spring. A couple in their seventies or eighties caring for each other in a nearly wordless way.

title: The Convenience Store at Three AM
text_zh: 凌晨三点的便利店像黑暗里一个发光的鱼缸，里面的人都在慢动作游泳。我失眠走进去的时候，只有两个客人。收银台旁坐着一个穿外卖服的小伙子，面前一碗关东煮，吃得极慢——不是品味，是在拖延回去接单的时间。角落里一个穿西装的中年男人，领带松了，靠着杂志架翻一本钓鱼杂志，翻的速度说明他只是需要一个动作来占据双手。店员面无表情地说了一句「欢迎光临」，声音带着已经说了三百遍的机械感。我在饮料柜前站了很久。拿起一罐咖啡，放下。拿起一罐奶茶，放下。最后选了一罐温热的咖啡牛奶——不是因为想喝，是因为罐子握在手里是温的。喝完咖啡，推门走入夜色。外卖小伙终于吃完了关东煮，正在系头盔。他朝我点了下头。我也点了下头。两个不想回家的人在荧光灯下交换了人类最简短的问候。
text_pinyin: Língchén sān diǎn de biànlìdiàn xiàng hēi'àn lǐ yí ge fāguāng de yúgāng, lǐmiàn de rén dōu zài màn dòngzuò yóuyǒng. Wǒ shīmián zǒu jìnqù de shíhou, zhǐyǒu liǎng ge kèrén. Shōuyíntái páng zuò zhe yí ge chuān wàimài fú de xiǎohuǒzi, miànqián yì wǎn guāndōngzhǔ, chī de jí màn — bú shì pǐnwèi, shì zài tuōyán huíqù jiē dān de shíjiān. Jiǎoluò lǐ yí ge chuān xīzhuāng de zhōngnián nánrén, lǐngdài sōng le, kào zhe zázhì jià fān yì běn diàoyú zázhì, fān de sùdù shuōmíng tā zhǐ shì xūyào yí ge dòngzuò lái zhànjù shuāngshǒu. Diànyuán miàn wú biǎoqíng de shuō le yí jù "huānyíng guānglín", shēngyīn dài zhe yǐjīng shuō le sān bǎi biàn de jīxièguǎn. Wǒ zài yǐnliào guì qián zhàn le hěn jiǔ. Ná qǐ yì guàn kāfēi, fàng xià. Ná qǐ yì guàn nǎichá, fàng xià. Zuìhòu xuǎn le yì guàn wēn rè de kāfēi niúnǎi — bú shì yīnwèi xiǎng hē, shì yīnwèi guànzi wò zài shǒu lǐ shì wēn de. Hē wán kāfēi, tuī mén zǒu rù yèsè. Wàimài xiǎohuǒ zhōngyú chī wán le guāndōngzhǔ, zhèngzài jì tóukuī. Tā cháo wǒ diǎn le xià tóu. Wǒ yě diǎn le xià tóu. Liǎng ge bù xiǎng huí jiā de rén zài yíngguāngdēng xià jiāohuàn le rénlèi zuì jiǎnduǎn de wènhòu.
text_en: A convenience store at three AM is a glowing fishbowl in the darkness, everyone inside swimming in slow motion. When I wandered in from insomnia, there were two customers. By the register sat a young man in a delivery uniform, bowl of oden in front of him, eating with extreme slowness—not savoring, but delaying his return to taking orders. In the corner, a middle-aged man in a suit, tie loosened, leaning against the magazine rack flipping through a fishing magazine at a speed that said he just needed something to do with his hands. The clerk said 'welcome' expressionlessly, her voice carrying the mechanical feel of having said it three hundred times. I stood before the drink cooler a long time. Picked up a coffee, put it back. Picked up a milk tea, put it back. Finally chose a warm can of cafe au lait—not because I wanted it, but because the can was warm in my hand. I finished the coffee and pushed through the door into the night. The delivery guy had finally finished his oden and was strapping on his helmet. He nodded at me. I nodded back. Two people who didn't want to go home, exchanging humanity's shortest greeting under fluorescent light.

options.text_en: A library

options.pinyin: Yí gè yīyuàn

title: The Neighbor's Piano
text_zh: 搬到这个小区之后，每天晚上七点到八点之间，我都能听到隔壁传来的钢琴声。弹的人水平不高，经常在同一个地方卡住，反反复复地练同一个小节。一开始我判断是个孩子在练琴，因为曲目都是初级的练习曲，而且速度忽快忽慢，明显缺乏控制力。但有一天晚上，钢琴声突然变了。不再是练习曲，而是一首我熟悉的老歌——邓丽君的《月亮代表我的心》。弹得并不流畅，但每一个音符都带着一种小心翼翼的深情，像是在用手指讲一个故事。之后的每个周末晚上，那个人都会弹一首老歌。有时是《恰似你的温柔》，有时是《甜蜜蜜》。平日里练基础，周末弹心事。后来有一次在电梯里，我遇到了隔壁的住户——一个头发花白的老先生。我忍不住问他是不是他在弹琴。他有些不好意思地说，退休之后开始学的，学了两年了。「年轻的时候没条件学，」他说，「现在学也不算晚吧。」他说他老伴生前最喜欢邓丽君的歌。我没有再问下去。回到家里，七点整，钢琴声准时响起，依旧磕磕绊绊，依旧认真。那些不完美的音符穿过墙壁传来，比任何完美的演奏都更动人。
text_pinyin: Bān dào zhège xiǎoqū zhīhòu, měitiān wǎnshang qī diǎn dào bā diǎn zhījiān, wǒ dōu néng tīng dào gébì chuán lái de gāngqín shēng. Tán de rén shuǐpíng bù gāo, jīngcháng zài tóng yí gè dìfang kǎ zhù, fǎnfǎn-fùfù de liàn tóng yí gè xiǎojié. Yì kāishǐ wǒ pànduàn shì gè háizi zài liàn qín, yīnwèi qǔmù dōu shì chūjí de liànxíqǔ, érqiě sùdù hū kuài hū màn, míngxiǎn quēfá kòngzhìlì. Dàn yǒu yì tiān wǎnshang, gāngqín shēng tūrán biàn le. Bú zài shì liànxíqǔ, ér shì yì shǒu wǒ shúxī de lǎo gē — Dèng Lìjūn de 《Yuèliang Dàibiǎo Wǒ de Xīn》. Tán de bìng bù liúchàng, dàn měi yí gè yīnfú dōu dài zhe yì zhǒng xiǎoxīn-yìyì de shēnqíng, xiàng shì zài yòng shǒuzhǐ jiǎng yí gè gùshi. Zhīhòu de měi gè zhōumò wǎnshang, nàge rén dōu huì tán yì shǒu lǎo gē. Yǒu shí shì 《Qiàsì Nǐ de Wēnróu》, yǒu shí shì 《Tiánmìmì》. Píngrì lǐ liàn jīchǔ, zhōumò tán xīnshì. Hòulái yǒu yí cì zài diàntī lǐ, wǒ yù dào le gébì de zhùhù — yí gè tóufa huābái de lǎo xiānsheng. Wǒ rěn bú zhù wèn tā shì bu shì tā zài tán qín. Tā yǒuxiē bù hǎoyìsi de shuō, tuìxiū zhīhòu kāishǐ xué de, xué le liǎng nián le. 「Niánqīng de shíhou méi tiáojiàn xué,」 tā shuō, 「xiànzài xué yě bú suàn wǎn ba.」 Tā shuō tā lǎobàn shēng qián zuì xǐhuan Dèng Lìjūn de gē. Wǒ méiyǒu zài wèn xiàqù. Huí dào jiā lǐ, qī diǎn zhěng, gāngqín shēng zhǔnshí xiǎng qǐ, yījiù kēkē-bànbàn, yījiù rènzhēn. Nàxiē bù wánměi de yīnfú chuān guò qiángbì chuán lái, bǐ rènhé wánměi de yǎnzòu dōu gèng dòngrén.
text_en: After moving to this neighborhood, every evening between seven and eight I can hear piano playing from next door. The player isn't very skilled, often getting stuck in the same spot, practicing the same passage over and over. At first I assumed it was a child practicing, since the pieces were beginner exercises and the tempo fluctuated, obviously lacking control. But one evening, the piano suddenly changed. No longer exercises, but an old song I recognized — Teresa Teng's 'The Moon Represents My Heart.' The playing wasn't smooth, but every note carried a careful, deep affection, as if telling a story with fingers. After that, every weekend evening, the person would play an old song. Sometimes 'Just Like Your Tenderness,' sometimes 'Tian Mi Mi.' Weekdays for fundamentals, weekends for what's in the heart. Later, I ran into the neighbor in the elevator — a grey-haired old gentleman. I couldn't help asking if he was the one playing. He said a little sheepishly that he'd started learning after retirement, two years ago. 'When I was young I didn't have the means to learn,' he said. 'It's not too late to start now, is it?' He said his late wife loved Teresa Teng's songs the most. I didn't ask further. Back in my apartment, at seven on the dot, the piano started up, still halting, still earnest. Those imperfect notes passing through the wall were more moving than any perfect performance.

title: The Underground Bookstore
text_zh: 在一栋写字楼的地下一层，藏着一家只有熟客才知道的书店。没有招牌，入口是一扇贴着「仓库」标志的铁门。推门进去，扑面而来的是旧书特有的纸香——一种混合了灰尘和时间的气味。书店的面积不大，大概六十平方米，但书架从地面一直延伸到天花板，中间的过道窄得只能侧身通过。老板是一个戴圆框眼镜的中年男人，总是坐在进门右手边的一张旧书桌后面，面前堆着几摞正在分类的书。他的分类方法很独特，不按出版社也不按作者，而是按照他自己发明的主题：「适合雨天读的书」「失恋时读的书」「辞职之前读的书」。他说这些分类来自二十年的观察，「人们找书的时候，其实不是在找一本具体的书，而是在找一种状态。」定价也很随意，他会翻开每本书看看有没有折痕和批注，如果上一个读者留下了认真的笔记，他反而会加价，因为「一本被认真读过的书，价值更高」。这家店不卖新书，不做网店，不接受移动支付。在这个一切都在追求效率的时代，它固执地保持着一种缓慢的、面对面的交易方式，像是对整个数字时代的温柔抵抗。
text_pinyin: Zài yí dòng xiězìlóu de dìxià yī céng, cáng zhe yì jiā zhǐ yǒu shúkè cái zhīdào de shūdiàn. Méiyǒu zhāopái, rùkǒu shì yí shàn tiē zhe 「cāngkù」 biāozhì de tiě mén. Tuī mén jìnqù, pūmiàn ér lái de shì jiù shū tèyǒu de zhǐxiāng — yì zhǒng hùnhé le huīchén hé shíjiān de qìwèi. Shūdiàn de miànjī bú dà, dàgài liùshí píngfāng mǐ, dàn shūjià cóng dìmiàn yìzhí yánshēn dào tiānhuābǎn, zhōngjiān de guòdào zhǎi de zhǐ néng cèshēn tōngguò. Lǎobǎn shì yí gè dài yuánkuàng yǎnjìng de zhōngnián nánrén, zǒng shì zuò zài jìn mén yòushǒu biān de yì zhāng jiù shūzhuō hòumiàn, miànqián duī zhe jǐ luò zhèngzài fēnlèi de shū. Tā de fēnlèi fāngfǎ hěn dútè, bú àn chūbǎnshè yě bú àn zuòzhě, ér shì ànzhào tā zìjǐ fāmíng de zhǔtí: 「shìhé yǔtiān dú de shū」「shīliàn shí dú de shū」「cízhí zhīqián dú de shū」. Tā shuō zhèxiē fēnlèi lái zì èrshí nián de guānchá, 「rénmen zhǎo shū de shíhou, qíshí bú shì zài zhǎo yì běn jùtǐ de shū, ér shì zài zhǎo yì zhǒng zhuàngtài.」 Dìngjià yě hěn suíyì, tā huì fānkāi měi běn shū kàn kàn yǒu méi yǒu zhéhén hé pīzhù, rúguǒ shàng yí gè dúzhě liú xià le rènzhēn de bǐjì, tā fǎn'ér huì jiājià, yīnwèi 「yì běn bèi rènzhēn dú guò de shū, jiàzhí gèng gāo」. Zhè jiā diàn bú mài xīn shū, bú zuò wǎngdiàn, bù jiēshòu yídòng zhīfù. Zài zhège yíqiè dōu zài zhuīqiú xiàolǜ de shídài, tā gùzhí de bǎochí zhe yì zhǒng huǎnmàn de, miànduìmiàn de jiāoyì fāngshì, xiàng shì duì zhěnggè shùzì shídài de wēnróu dǐkàng.
text_en: In the basement of an office building hides a bookstore known only to regulars. There is no sign; the entrance is an iron door marked 'Storage.' Push it open and you're met with the scent peculiar to old books — a smell mingling dust and time. The store is small, about sixty square meters, but bookshelves stretch from floor to ceiling, the aisles between them so narrow you must turn sideways to pass. The owner, a middle-aged man in round glasses, always sits behind an old desk to the right of the entrance, stacks of books being sorted piled before him. His categorization is unique — not by publisher or author, but by themes he invented himself: 'books to read on rainy days,' 'books to read after a breakup,' 'books to read before quitting your job.' He says these categories come from twenty years of observation: 'When people look for books, they're not really looking for a specific book — they're looking for a state of being.' His pricing is equally casual. He opens each book to check for dog-ears and annotations; if the previous reader left careful notes, he actually raises the price, because 'a book that has been read carefully is worth more.' This shop doesn't sell new books, doesn't have an online store, doesn't accept mobile payment.

title: The Hospital Cafeteria
text_zh: 医院的食堂和别的食堂不一样。这里的人吃饭时几乎不说话，即便说话也压低了声音，好像声音大一点就会惊扰到什么。穿白大褂的医生三五成群地坐在一起，用最快的速度把饭扒进嘴里，然后擦擦嘴就走了。他们的手术服上偶尔还能看到没来得及换下的手套痕迹。病人家属通常坐在靠窗的位置，面前的饭菜动了几口就不吃了，眼睛一直盯着手机，等着某个电话或者消息。有些人的眼圈是红的，但没有人哭出声来。食堂的菜品谈不上好吃，但分量很足，而且比外面便宜得多。打菜的阿姨似乎理解这里每个人的处境，手抖得格外慷慨，每次都多给半勺。墙上贴着「请勿浪费粮食」的标语，但没有人在意，因为在这里浪费食物通常不是因为挑剔，而是因为没有胃口。最安静的时刻是下午两三点，午饭的高峰过去了，食堂里只剩零星几个人。阳光从窗户斜斜地照进来，照在空荡荡的桌椅上。一个食堂，本来只是一个吃饭的地方，但在医院里，它承载着焦虑、疲惫和微弱的希望。人们在这里短暂地补充能量，然后回到各自的担忧中去。
text_pinyin: Yīyuàn de shítáng hé bié de shítáng bù yíyàng. Zhèlǐ de rén chīfàn shí jīhū bù shuōhuà, jíbiàn shuōhuà yě yādī le shēngyīn, hǎoxiàng shēngyīn dà yìdiǎn jiù huì jīngrǎo dào shénme. Chuān bái dàguà de yīshēng sānwǔ-chéngqún de zuò zài yìqǐ, yòng zuì kuài de sùdù bǎ fàn bā jìn zuǐ lǐ, ránhòu cā cā zuǐ jiù zǒu le. Tāmen de shǒushù fú shàng ǒu'ěr hái néng kàn dào méi lái de jí huàn xià de shǒutào hénjì. Bìngrén jiāshǔ tōngcháng zuò zài kào chuāng de wèizhì, miànqián de fàncài dòng le jǐ kǒu jiù bù chī le, yǎnjing yìzhí dīng zhe shǒujī, děng zhe mǒu gè diànhuà huòzhě xiāoxi. Yǒuxiē rén de yǎnquān shì hóng de, dàn méiyǒu rén kū chū shēng lái. Shítáng de càipǐn tán bú shàng hǎochī, dàn fènliàng hěn zú, érqiě bǐ wàimiàn piányi de duō. Dǎ cài de āyí sìhū lǐjiě zhèlǐ měi gè rén de chǔjìng, shǒu dǒu de géwài kāngkǎi, měi cì dōu duō gěi bàn sháo. Qiáng shàng tiē zhe 「qǐng wù làngfèi liángshi」 de biāoyǔ, dàn méiyǒu rén zàiyì, yīnwèi zài zhèlǐ làngfèi shíwù tōngcháng bú shì yīnwèi tiāoti, ér shì yīnwèi méiyǒu wèikǒu. Zuì ānjìng de shíkè shì xiàwǔ liǎng sān diǎn, wǔfàn de gāofēng guòqù le, shítáng lǐ zhǐ shèng língxīng jǐ gè rén. Yángguāng cóng chuānghù xiéxié de zhào jìnlái, zhào zài kōngdàngdàng de zhuōyǐ shàng. Yí gè shítáng, běnlái zhǐ shì yí gè chīfàn de dìfang, dàn zài yīyuàn lǐ, tā chéngzài zhe jiāolǜ, píbèi hé wēiruò de xīwàng. Rénmen zài zhèlǐ duǎnzàn de bǔchōng néngliàng, ránhòu huí dào gèzì de dānyōu zhōng qù.
text_en: A hospital cafeteria is different from other cafeterias. People here barely speak while eating, and when they do, they keep their voices low, as if any loudness might disturb something. Doctors in white coats sit in small groups, shoveling food down as fast as possible, then wiping their mouths and leaving. Occasionally you can still see glove marks on their surgical scrubs that they hadn't time to change out of. Patients' family members usually sit by the windows, picking at their food for a few bites before stopping, eyes fixed on their phones, waiting for some call or message. Some have red-rimmed eyes, but nobody cries aloud. The food isn't particularly good, but the portions are generous and much cheaper than outside. The serving auntie seems to understand everyone's situation here, her hand trembling with extra generosity, always giving an extra half-scoop. A sign on the wall reads 'Please don't waste food,' but nobody pays it mind, because here food goes to waste not from pickiness but from lack of appetite. The quietest moment is around two or three in the afternoon, after the lunch rush, when only a scattering of people remain. Sunlight slants through the windows onto the empty tables and chairs. A cafeteria is supposed to be just a place to eat, but in a hospital it carries anxiety, exhaustion, and fragile hope. People briefly refuel here, then return to their respective worries.

title: The Sound of Closing Shops at Night
text_zh: 每天晚上十点左右，我住的那条老街会进入一种独特的声音仪式。最先关门的总是卖文具的林阿姨。她拉下铁卷帘的时候，金属碰撞声在空荡的巷子里回响，像是一天疲惫的叹息。紧接着是隔壁的水果摊，老板把没卖完的西瓜搬进店里，塑料箱子拖过水泥地面发出低沉的摩擦声。然后是对面的裁缝铺，缝纫机终于停了，那种持续了一整天的嗡嗡声突然消失，反而让人觉得耳朵里空了一块。最后关门的永远是巷口的馄饨店。老板娘把最后一锅汤倒掉的声音，混合着水龙头冲洗锅具的哗哗声，构成了这条街的晚安曲。我曾经觉得这些声音是噪音，是阻碍入睡的干扰。但搬到新城区的高层公寓以后，夜晚安静得几乎令人窒息。双层隔音玻璃把一切都挡在了外面，连风声都听不到。我才意识到，那些关店的声音从来不是噪音——它们是一种陪伴，是这个街区在告诉你：今天结束了，大家都平安地收了工，你可以安心睡了。
text_pinyin: Měi tiān wǎnshang shí diǎn zuǒyòu, wǒ zhù de nà tiáo lǎo jiē huì jìnrù yī zhǒng dútè de shēngyīn yíshì. Zuì xiān guān mén de zǒng shì mài wénjù de Lín āyí. Tā lā xià tiě juǎnlián de shíhou, jīnshǔ pèngzhuàng shēng zài kōngdàng de xiàngzi lǐ huíxiǎng, xiàng shì yī tiān píbèi de tànxī. Jǐn jiēzhe shì gébì de shuǐguǒ tān, lǎobǎn bǎ méi mài wán de xīguā bān jìn diàn lǐ, sùliào xiāngzi tuō guò shuǐní dìmiàn fāchū dīchén de mócā shēng. Ránhòu shì duìmiàn de cáiféng pù, féngrènjī zhōngyú tíng le, nà zhǒng chíxù le yī zhěng tiān de wēngwēng shēng tūrán xiāoshī, fǎn'ér ràng rén juéde ěrduo lǐ kōng le yī kuài. Zuìhòu guān mén de yǒngyuǎn shì xiàngkǒu de húntun diàn. Lǎobǎnniáng bǎ zuìhòu yī guō tāng dào diào de shēngyīn, hùnhé zhe shuǐlóngtóu chōngxǐ guōjù de huāhuā shēng, gòuchéng le zhè tiáo jiē de wǎn'ān qǔ. Wǒ céngjīng juéde zhèxiē shēngyīn shì zàoyīn, shì zǔ'ài rùshuì de gānrǎo. Dàn bān dào xīn chéngqū de gāocéng gōngyù yǐhòu, yèwǎn ānjìng de jīhū lìng rén zhìxī. Shuāngcéng géyīn bōli bǎ yīqiè dōu dǎng zài le wàimiàn, lián fēngshēng dōu tīng bú dào. Wǒ cái yìshí dào, nàxiē guān diàn de shēngyīn cónglái bú shì zàoyīn——tāmen shì yī zhǒng péibàn, shì zhège jiēqū zài gàosù nǐ: jīntiān jiéshù le, dàjiā dōu píng'ān de shōu le gōng, nǐ kěyǐ ānxīn shuì le.
text_en: Every night around ten o'clock, the old street where I lived would enter a distinctive sonic ritual. The first to close was always Auntie Lin's stationery shop. When she pulled down the metal rolling shutter, the clash of metal echoed through the empty alley like the weary sigh of a long day. Right after came the fruit stand next door — the owner carrying unsold watermelons inside, plastic crates dragging across the concrete floor with a low grinding sound. Then the tailor's shop across the way, the sewing machine finally silenced, that all-day hum vanishing so suddenly it felt like a hollow space opened in your ears. The last to close was always the wonton shop at the mouth of the alley. The owner pouring out the final pot of broth, mingled with the rush of tap water rinsing the cookware — together they composed the street's goodnight song. I once thought these sounds were noise, disturbances that kept me from falling asleep. But after moving to a high-rise apartment in the new district, the nights were so quiet they felt almost suffocating. Double-paned soundproof glass blocked everything out; I couldn't even hear the wind. Only then did I realize those closing-shop sounds were never noise — they were companionship, the neighborhood telling you: the day is over, everyone has safely finished work, you can rest easy now.

title: Morning Fog over a River
text_zh: 小时候住在江边的外婆家，每天清晨我都会被一种奇特的安静叫醒——不是闹钟，不是鸡鸣，而是雾气降临时世界突然变得格外沉寂的那种感觉。推开窗户，江面上铺着一层乳白色的薄雾，对岸的房屋和树木只剩下模糊的轮廓，像一幅还没画完的水墨画。外婆说，雾是江水在呼吸。夜里水温比空气高，江面蒸腾出的水汽在冷空气中凝结，就形成了雾。她用最朴素的语言，无意中给了我一个相当准确的气象学解释。雾中的声音也会发生微妙的变化。平时能听到的对岸鸡叫声消失了，取而代之的是近处更细微的声响——露珠从屋檐滴落的声音，邻居家的猫踩在湿漉漉的石板上的脚步声，江水轻轻拍打岸边石头的节奏。雾像一层滤镜，把远处的声音吸收了，把近处的声音放大了。大概七八点钟，太阳升高以后，雾会在十几分钟内迅速散去。这个过程也很美：先是对岸的屋顶从雾中浮现，然后是树梢，最后整条江面像被揭开了面纱一样，突然变得清澈透亮。外婆去世以后我再没回过那个小镇。但每个起雾的清晨，无论我在哪个城市，我都会想起那条江，想起雾中那种温柔的、包裹一切的沉默。
text_pinyin: Xiǎo shíhou zhù zài jiāng biān de wàipó jiā, měi tiān qīngchén wǒ dōu huì bèi yī zhǒng qítè de ānjìng jiào xǐng——bú shì nàozhōng, bú shì jīmíng, ér shì wùqì jiànglín shí shìjiè tūrán biàn de géwài chénjì de nà zhǒng gǎnjué. Tuī kāi chuānghù, jiāng miàn shàng pū zhe yī céng rǔbái sè de báo wù, duì'àn de fángwū hé shùmù zhǐ shèng xia móhu de lúnkuò, xiàng yī fú hái méi huà wán de shuǐmò huà. Wàipó shuō, wù shì jiāng shuǐ zài hūxī. Yè lǐ shuǐwēn bǐ kōngqì gāo, jiāng miàn zhēngténg chū de shuǐqì zài lěng kōngqì zhōng níngjié, jiù xíngchéng le wù. Tā yòng zuì pǔsù de yǔyán, wúyì zhōng gěi le wǒ yī gè xiāngdāng zhǔnquè de qìxiàng xué jiěshì. Wù zhōng de shēngyīn yě huì fāshēng wēimiào de biànhuà. Píngshí néng tīng dào de duì'àn jī jiào shēng xiāoshī le, qǔ ér dài zhī de shì jìn chù gèng xìwēi de shēngxiǎng——lùzhū cóng wūyán dī luò de shēngyīn, línjū jiā de māo cǎi zài shīlùlù de shíbǎn shàng de jiǎobù shēng, jiāng shuǐ qīngqīng pāidǎ àn biān shítou de jiézòu. Wù xiàng yī céng lǜjìng, bǎ yuǎn chù de shēngyīn xīshōu le, bǎ jìn chù de shēngyīn fàngdà le. Dàgài qī bā diǎn zhōng, tàiyáng shēng gāo yǐhòu, wù huì zài shí jǐ fēnzhōng nèi xùnsù sàn qù. Zhège guòchéng yě hěn měi: xiān shì duì'àn de wūdǐng cóng wù zhōng fúxiàn, ránhòu shì shùshāo, zuìhòu zhěng tiáo jiāng miàn xiàng bèi jiē kāi le miànshā yīyàng, tūrán biàn de qīngchè tòu liàng. Wàipó qùshì yǐhòu wǒ zài méi huí guò nàge xiǎo zhèn. Dàn měi gè qǐ wù de qīngchén, wúlùn wǒ zài nǎge chéngshì, wǒ dōu huì xiǎng qǐ nà tiáo jiāng, xiǎng qǐ wù zhōng nà zhǒng wēnróu de, bāoguǒ yīqiè de chénmò.
text_en: When I was little, living at my grandmother's house by the river, every morning I was woken by a peculiar silence — not an alarm, not a rooster, but the feeling of the world growing still when fog descended. Opening the window, the river surface was draped in milky-white mist; houses and trees on the opposite bank were blurred silhouettes, like an unfinished ink wash painting. Grandmother said the fog was the river breathing. At night the water was warmer than the air, and rising vapor condensed to form fog. In the plainest language, she had given me an accurate meteorological explanation. Sounds in the fog changed too. The roosters across the bank disappeared, replaced by finer nearby sounds — dewdrops falling from eaves, the neighbor's cat stepping on damp stone, river water lapping shore rocks. Fog acted like a filter, absorbing distant sounds and amplifying near ones. Around seven or eight, after the sun climbed higher, the fog dissipated rapidly. First the rooftops emerged, then treetops, then the entire river surface turned clear and luminous. After Grandmother passed, I never returned to that town. But every foggy morning, in any city, I think of that river and that tender silence within the fog.

options.pinyin: wánquán tīng bù dǒng wàipó zài shuō shénme
options.text_en: Could not understand what Grandmother was saying at all

questions.explanation: Grandmother's plain-spoken explanation of the fog inadvertently matched the meteorological mechanism—folk wisdom arriving at scientific truth through observation.

questions.q_zh: 文章中把雾比作什么？
questions.q_en: What is the fog compared to in the passage?

options.pinyin: yī bǎ yǔsǎn

title: A Librarian's Favorite Corner
title_zh: 图书管理员最喜欢的角落
text_zh: 我在市图书馆工作了十七年，有些事情只有长年待在这里的人才会知道。比如，整座图书馆里最安静的地方并不是阅览室——阅览室有翻书声、有空调的嗡鸣、偶尔还有人压低嗓子打电话。真正最安静的地方是三楼东侧尽头的外文期刊区。那里常年光线昏暗，因为窗外有一棵巨大的银杏树，枝叶繁茂到几乎遮住了整面窗户。架子上陈列的是过期的法文和德文学术期刊，几乎没有人来翻阅。但正因如此，那个角落保留了一种珍贵的寂静——不是死寂，而是沉淀了无数个下午的、有厚度的宁静。我习惯在午休时间去那里坐一会儿。靠窗有一把旧藤椅，不知道是哪一任馆长留下的，坐垫已经被压出了一个人形的凹陷。秋天的时候，银杏叶变黄，午后的阳光透过金色的叶片洒进来，整个角落被染成琥珀色。那种光线有一种催眠的力量，让人在不知不觉中放松下来，思绪变得缓慢而透明。有同事问我为什么不申请调到新馆区去，那边设施更新、空间更大。我笑笑说：「新馆很好，但那里没有我的藤椅，没有我的银杏树。」有些东西是搬不走的，因为它们不属于任何人，只属于那个特定的角落和在那里度过的时间。
text_pinyin: Wǒ zài shì túshūguǎn gōngzuò le shíqī nián, yǒu xiē shìqíng zhǐyǒu cháng nián dāi zài zhèlǐ de rén cái huì zhīdào. Bǐrú, zhěng zuò túshūguǎn lǐ zuì ānjìng de dìfāng bìng bú shì yuèlǎn shì——yuèlǎn shì yǒu fān shū shēng, yǒu kōngtiáo de wēngmíng, ǒu'ěr hái yǒu rén yā dī sǎngzi dǎ diànhuà. Zhēnzhèng zuì ānjìng de dìfāng shì sān lóu dōng cè jìntóu de wàiwén qīkān qū. Nàlǐ cháng nián guāngxiàn hūn'àn, yīnwèi chuāng wài yǒu yī kē jùdà de yínxìng shù, zhīyè fánmào dào jīhū zhēzhù le zhěng miàn chuānghù. Jiàzi shàng chénliè de shì guòqī de Fǎwén hé Déwén xuéshù qīkān, jīhū méiyǒu rén lái fānyuè. Dàn zhèng yīn rúcǐ, nàge jiǎoluò bǎoliú le yī zhǒng zhēnguì de jìjìng——bú shì sǐjì, ér shì chéndiàn le wúshù gè xiàwǔ de, yǒu hòudù de níngjìng. Wǒ xíguàn zài wǔxiū shíjiān qù nàlǐ zuò yīhuìr. Kào chuāng yǒu yī bǎ jiù téng yǐ, bù zhīdào shì nǎ yī rèn guǎnzhǎng liú xià de, zuòdiàn yǐjīng bèi yā chū le yī gè rénxíng de āoxiàn. Qiūtiān de shíhou, yínxìng yè biàn huáng, wǔhòu de yángguāng tòu guò jīnsè de yèpiàn sǎ jìnlái, zhěnggè jiǎoluò bèi rǎn chéng hǔpò sè. Nà zhǒng guāngxiàn yǒu yī zhǒng cuīmián de lìliang, ràng rén zài bùzhībùjué zhōng fàngsōng xiàlái, sīxù biàn de huǎnmàn ér tòumíng. Yǒu tóngshì wèn wǒ wèishéme bù shēnqǐng diào dào xīn guǎn qū qù, nàbiān shèshī gēng xīn, kōngjiān gèng dà. Wǒ xiàoxiao shuō:「Xīn guǎn hěn hǎo, dàn nàlǐ méiyǒu wǒ de téng yǐ, méiyǒu wǒ de yínxìng shù.」Yǒu xiē dōngxi shì bān bù zǒu de, yīnwèi tāmen bù shǔyú rènhé rén, zhǐ shǔyú nàge tèdìng de jiǎoluò hé zài nàlǐ dùguò de shíjiān.
text_en: I have worked at the city library for seventeen years. The quietest spot is not the reading room — that has pages turning, the AC hum, the occasional lowered phone call. The truly quietest place is the foreign periodicals section at the far end of the third floor's east wing. Outside the window stands an enormous ginkgo tree blocking nearly all light. The shelves display expired French and German journals nobody reads. But this corner has preserved a precious stillness — not the dead silence of emptiness, but quiet with depth, settled through countless afternoons. I sit there during lunch breaks. By the window is an old rattan chair left by some former director, its cushion pressed into human shape. In autumn, when the ginkgo leaves turn yellow, afternoon sunlight filters through golden leaves and spills inside, dyeing the corner amber. That light has a hypnotic power: it lets you relax without realizing it, thoughts becoming slow and transparent. A colleague asked why I didn't transfer to the new branch — newer facilities, more space. I said: 'The new branch doesn't have my rattan chair, or my ginkgo tree.' Some things cannot be moved. They belong only to that corner and the time spent there.

options.text_en: Because the periodicals there are expired foreign academic journals that almost nobody reads

options.pinyin: yīnwèi nàlǐ jìnzhǐ shuōhuà

options.pinyin: yīnwèi xīn guǎn qū gōngzī gèng dī

options.pinyin: yīnwèi xīn guǎn qū lí jiā tài yuǎn
options.text_en: Because the new branch is too far from home

options.text: 因为那个角落的藤椅和银杏树是无法搬走的，它们属于那个特定的空间和时间

options.text_en: Because the rattan chair and ginkgo tree of that corner cannot be moved; they belong to that specific space and time

title: The Warmth of a Stranger's Nod
title_zh: 陌生人点头之间的温暖
text_zh: 在我住的小区里，有一个遛狗的老先生，我不知道他姓什么，不知道他住哪栋楼，甚至不知道他养的那条柴犬叫什么名字。但每天早上七点半左右，我出门去地铁站的路上，总会在小区花园的石子路上遇到他。我们之间的全部交流，就是彼此点一下头。不说「早上好」，不说「今天天气不错」，只是微微颔首，然后各走各的路。这种默契持续了大概三年。奇怪的是，这个极其简短的仪式却给了我一种难以言喻的安定感。好像只要早上出门能看到他，今天就会是正常的、平安的一天。有一次我出差两周回来，第一天早上出门又在老地方看到他，他照常点了一下头。那一刻我心里涌起一股温暖——不是那种热烈的情感，而是一种低温的、持续的、像恒温器一样稳定的安心。后来有一阵子我连续好几天没看到他，心里竟然隐隐有些担忧。第四天早上他又出现了，身边换了一条黑色的拉布拉多。他朝我点头的时候，我发现他的眼神比以前柔和了一些，带着某种我读不懂的东西。也许那条柴犬离开了。我们依然没有说话。但那天的点头比平时慢了半拍，好像彼此都在这个微小的动作里多停留了一瞬。我想，人与人之间最珍贵的联结，有时候恰恰不需要语言。
text_pinyin: Zài wǒ zhù de xiǎoqū lǐ, yǒu yī gè liù gǒu de lǎo xiānsheng, wǒ bù zhīdào tā xìng shénme, bù zhīdào tā zhù nǎ dòng lóu, shènzhì bù zhīdào tā yǎng de nà tiáo cháiquǎn jiào shénme míngzì. Dàn měi tiān zǎoshang qī diǎn bàn zuǒyòu, wǒ chū mén qù dìtiě zhàn de lùshang, zǒng huì zài xiǎoqū huāyuán de shízǐ lù shàng yù dào tā. Wǒmen zhījiān de quánbù jiāoliú, jiùshì bǐcǐ diǎn yīxià tóu. Bù shuō「zǎoshang hǎo」, bù shuō「jīntiān tiānqì búcuò」, zhǐshì wēiwēi hànshǒu, ránhòu gè zǒu gè de lù. Zhè zhǒng mòqì chíxù le dàgài sān nián. Qíguài de shì, zhège jíqí jiǎnduǎn de yíshì què gěi le wǒ yī zhǒng nányǐ yányù de āndìng gǎn. Hǎoxiàng zhǐyào zǎoshang chū mén néng kàn dào tā, jīntiān jiù huì shì zhèngcháng de, píng'ān de yī tiān. Yǒu yī cì wǒ chūchāi liǎng zhōu huílái, dì yī tiān zǎoshang chū mén yòu zài lǎo dìfāng kàn dào tā, tā zhàocháng diǎn le yīxià tóu. Nà yī kè wǒ xīnlǐ yǒng qǐ yī gǔ wēnnuǎn——bú shì nà zhǒng rèliè de qínggǎn, ér shì yī zhǒng dīwēn de, chíxù de, xiàng héngwēnqì yīyàng wěndìng de ānxīn. Hòulái yǒu yī zhènzi wǒ liánxù hǎo jǐ tiān méi kàn dào tā, xīnlǐ jìngrán yǐnyǐn yǒu xiē dānyōu. Dì sì tiān zǎoshang tā yòu chūxiàn le, shēnbiān huàn le yī tiáo hēisè de lābùlāduō. Tā cháo wǒ diǎntóu de shíhou, wǒ fāxiàn tā de yǎnshén bǐ yǐqián róuhé le yīxiē, dài zhe mǒu zhǒng wǒ dú bù dǒng de dōngxi. Yěxǔ nà tiáo cháiquǎn líkāi le. Wǒmen yīrán méiyǒu shuōhuà. Dàn nà tiān de diǎntóu bǐ píngshí màn le bàn pāi, hǎoxiàng bǐcǐ dōu zài zhège wēixiǎo de dòngzuò lǐ duō tíngliú le yī shùn. Wǒ xiǎng, rén yǔ rén zhījiān zuì zhēnguì de liánjié, yǒu shíhou qiàqià bú xūyào yǔyán.
text_en: In my compound, there is an old gentleman who walks his dog. I don't know his surname, his building, or his Shiba Inu's name. But every morning around seven-thirty, on my way to the subway, I see him on the gravel path through the garden. Our communication is a mutual nod. No 'good morning,' no 'nice weather' — just a slight inclination of the head, then each going our way. This has continued for three years. This brief ritual gave me a stability difficult to put into words. As long as I could see him when I stepped out, today would be a normal, safe day. Once I returned from a two-week business trip. The first morning back, I saw him at the usual spot; he nodded as always. A warmth welled up — not ardent emotion, but a low-temperature, thermostat-like steadiness. Later he was absent for several consecutive days, and a faint worry crept in. On the fourth morning he reappeared, this time with a black Labrador beside him. When he nodded, his gaze had softened, carrying something I couldn't read. Perhaps the Shiba had gone. We still didn't speak. But that day's nod was half a beat slower, as if we had both lingered an extra instant within that tiny gesture.

options.pinyin: xiàng héngwēnqì yīyàng wěndìng de, dīwēn de, chíxù de ānxīn
options.text_en: Like a thermostat — stable, low-temperature, sustained reassurance

options.pinyin: xiàng tūrán zhòngjiǎng de jīngxǐ
options.text_en: Like the surprise of suddenly winning a prize

questions.explanation: The warmth is compared to a thermostat: stable, low-temperature, and sustained—not a burst of friendliness but a reliable, calibrated reassurance.

questions.q_zh: 老先生消失几天后重新出现时，有什么变化？
questions.q_en: When the old gentleman reappeared after being absent for several days, what had changed?

options.text: 他开始主动跟作者说话了
options.pinyin: tā kāishǐ zhǔdòng gēn zuòzhě shuōhuà le
options.text_en: He started actively talking to the author

options.text: 他身边的狗换成了黑色拉布拉多，眼神比以前柔和
options.pinyin: tā shēnbiān de gǒu huàn chéng le hēisè lābùlāduō, yǎnshén bǐ yǐqián róuhé
options.text_en: His dog had changed to a black Labrador, and his gaze was softer than before

title: Tea Leaves Unfurling
text_zh: 父亲泡茶有一套固定的流程，几十年来从未改变。先用沸水烫壶，倒掉，再放入茶叶，注水至七分满，盖上壶盖，等待。他说泡茶最关键的不是水温，不是茶叶的品质，而是等待的耐心。太急着倒出来，茶叶还没有完全舒展，滋味是浅的、涩的；等得太久，又会苦。恰到好处的那个时刻，需要经验，也需要直觉。小时候我不理解他为什么每天花那么多时间在泡茶上。一杯茶而已，速溶的不是也能喝吗？他没有反驳，只是把泡好的茶递给我一杯。我喝了一口，确实跟速溶的不一样——不是简单的好喝或不好喝的区别，而是一种质感上的差异，像手工织布和机器织布的区别。后来我自己开始泡茶，才慢慢理解了父亲的那套仪式。泡茶的过程本身就是目的。当你站在那里等待茶叶在热水中缓缓舒展的时候，你被迫慢下来。你的注意力从纷乱的思绪中抽离出来，集中在眼前这个小小的壶里正在发生的变化上。卷缩的叶片一点点打开，颜色从深褐变成碧绿，水的颜色也渐渐变深。这个过程安静、缓慢、不可逆，像某种微型的四季更迭。父亲去年走了。现在每天早上泡茶的时候，我用的是他留下的那把紫砂壶。壶壁上积了几十年的茶垢，据说这样的壶即使只加白水也能泡出淡淡的茶香。我不知道这是不是真的，但我愿意相信。
text_pinyin: Fùqīn pào chá yǒu yī tào gùdìng de liúchéng, jǐ shí nián lái cóng wèi gǎibiàn. Xiān yòng fèishuǐ tàng hú, dào diào, zài fàng rù cháyè, zhù shuǐ zhì qī fēn mǎn, gài shàng hú gài, děngdài. Tā shuō pào chá zuì guānjiàn de bú shì shuǐwēn, bú shì cháyè de pǐnzhì, ér shì děngdài de nàixīn. Tài jí zhe dào chūlái, cháyè hái méiyǒu wánquán shūzhǎn, zīwèi shì qiǎn de, sè de; děng de tài jiǔ, yòu huì kǔ. Qià dào hǎo chù de nàge shíkè, xūyào jīngyàn, yě xūyào zhíjué. Xiǎo shíhou wǒ bù lǐjiě tā wèishéme měi tiān huā nàme duō shíjiān zài pào chá shàng. Yī bēi chá éryǐ, sùróng de bú shì yě néng hē ma? Tā méiyǒu fǎnbó, zhǐshì bǎ pào hǎo de chá dì gěi wǒ yī bēi. Wǒ hē le yī kǒu, quèshí gēn sùróng de bù yīyàng——bú shì jiǎndān de hǎo hē huò bù hǎo hē de qūbié, ér shì yī zhǒng zhìgǎn shàng de chāyì, xiàng shǒugōng zhī bù hé jīqì zhī bù de qūbié. Hòulái wǒ zìjǐ kāishǐ pào chá, cái mànman lǐjiě le fùqīn de nà tào yíshì. Pào chá de guòchéng běnshēn jiùshì mùdì. Dāng nǐ zhàn zài nàlǐ děngdài cháyè zài rè shuǐ zhōng huǎnhuǎn shūzhǎn de shíhou, nǐ bèi pò màn xiàlái. Nǐ de zhùyì lì cóng fēnluàn de sīxù zhōng chōulí chūlái, jízhōng zài yǎnqián zhège xiǎo xiǎo de hú lǐ zhèngzài fāshēng de biànhuà shàng. Juǎnsuō de yèpiàn yīdiǎndiǎn dǎkāi, yánsè cóng shēn hè biàn chéng bìlǜ, shuǐ de yánsè yě jiànjiàn biàn shēn. Zhège guòchéng ānjìng, huǎnmàn, bùkě nì, xiàng mǒu zhǒng wēixíng de sìjì gēngdié. Fùqīn qùnián zǒu le. Xiànzài měi tiān zǎoshang pào chá de shíhou, wǒ yòng de shì tā liú xià de nà bǎ zǐshā hú. Hú bì shàng jī le jǐ shí nián de chá gòu, jùshuō zhèyàng de hú jíshǐ zhǐ jiā bái shuǐ yě néng pào chū dàndàn de chá xiāng. Wǒ bù zhīdào zhè shì bú shì zhēn de, dàn wǒ yuànyì xiāngxìn.
text_en: Father had a fixed routine for brewing tea that never changed over decades. First scald the pot, pour it out, add leaves, fill to seven-tenths, lid on, wait. He said the most crucial thing isn't water temperature or leaf quality, but patience. Pour too hastily and the leaves haven't unfurled — the flavor is shallow, astringent. Wait too long and it turns bitter. That moment of just right requires experience and intuition alike. As a child I didn't understand why he spent so much time on tea. He didn't argue, just handed me a cup. I took a sip and it was different from instant — not simply tastes-good-or-not, but a difference in texture, like hand-woven versus machine-woven cloth. Later, when I started brewing myself, I understood his ritual. The process is the purpose. When you stand waiting for leaves to unfurl in hot water, you are forced to slow down. Attention pulls free from tangled thoughts and focuses on the small changes inside that pot. Curled leaves open, color shifting from dark brown to jade green, the water gradually deepening. The process is quiet, slow, irreversible — like some miniature turning of the seasons. Father passed away last year. Each morning I brew tea in the purple clay pot he left behind. Decades of tea stains line the walls; they say such a pot yields faint fragrance even with plain water. I choose to believe it.

title: A Letter Never Sent
text_zh: 整理母亲遗物的时候，我在她梳妆台的抽屉最深处发现了一封信。信封上写着一个地址，贴了邮票，但从未被投进邮筒。收信人是一个我从没听过的名字：周若兰。信已经被折叠得很整齐，纸张泛黄，墨迹微微褪色，估计写了至少二十年。我犹豫了很久，最终还是打开了。信的内容出乎我的意料。不是什么惊天动地的秘密，只是一封非常平淡的近况报告——母亲告诉周若兰她最近在学做糕点，丈夫升了职，女儿（也就是我）开始上小学了。信的结尾写道：「我时常想起我们在宿舍里彻夜聊天的日子。那时候觉得未来很远，现在回头看，才发现那些夜晚才是最珍贵的。希望你一切都好。」我试着查了那个地址，早已拆迁。我在网上搜索周若兰这个名字，没有任何结果。母亲为什么写了这封信却没有寄出？也许是地址不确定，怕寄不到。也许是写完以后重新读了一遍，觉得措辞不够好，打算改了再寄，结果一拖就忘了。也许她根本就没打算寄——写信本身就是目的，像是对着一个遥远的影子说话，不需要回应，只需要倾诉。我把信重新折好，放回了抽屉。
text_pinyin: Zhěnglǐ mǔqīn yíwù de shíhou, wǒ zài tā shūzhuāng tái de chōuti zuì shēn chù fāxiàn le yī fēng xìn. Xìnfēng shàng xiě zhe yī gè dìzhǐ, tiē le yóupiào, dàn cóng wèi bèi tóu jìn yóutǒng. Shōuxìn rén shì yī gè wǒ cóng méi tīng guò de míngzì: Zhōu Ruòlán. Xìn yǐjīng bèi zhédié de hěn zhěngqí, zhǐzhāng fàn huáng, mòjì wēiwēi tuìsè, gūjì xiě le zhìshǎo èrshí nián. Wǒ yóuyù le hěn jiǔ, zuìzhōng háishì dǎkāi le. Xìn de nèiróng chūhū wǒ de yìliào. Bú shì shénme jīngtiān dòngdì de mìmì, zhǐshì yī fēng fēicháng píngdàn de jìnkuàng bàogào——mǔqīn gàosù Zhōu Ruòlán tā zuìjìn zài xué zuò gāodiǎn, zhàngfu shēng le zhí, nǚ'ér (yě jiùshì wǒ) kāishǐ shàng xiǎoxué le. Xìn de jiéwěi xiě dào:「Wǒ shícháng xiǎng qǐ wǒmen zài sùshè lǐ chèyè liáotiān de rìzi. Nà shíhou juéde wèilái hěn yuǎn, xiànzài huítóu kàn, cái fāxiàn nàxiē yèwǎn cái shì zuì zhēnguì de. Xīwàng nǐ yīqiè dōu hǎo.」Wǒ shì zhe chá le nàge dìzhǐ, zǎo yǐ chāiqiān. Wǒ zài wǎng shàng sōusuǒ Zhōu Ruòlán zhège míngzì, méiyǒu rènhé jiéguǒ. Mǔqīn wèishéme xiě le zhè fēng xìn què méiyǒu jì chū? Yěxǔ shì dìzhǐ bú quèdìng, pà jì bú dào. Yěxǔ shì xiě wán yǐhòu chóngxīn dú le yī biàn, juéde cuòcí búgòu hǎo, dǎsuàn gǎi le zài jì, jiéguǒ yī tuō jiù wàng le. Yěxǔ tā gēnběn jiù méi dǎsuàn jì——xiě xìn běnshēn jiùshì mùdì, xiàng shì duì zhe yī gè yáoyuǎn de yǐngzi shuōhuà, bú xūyào huíyìng, zhǐ xūyào qīngsù. Wǒ bǎ xìn chóngxīn zhé hǎo, fàng huí le chōuti. Yǒu xiē huà, yěxǔ shuō chūlái jiù gòu le, bú yīdìng fēi yào bèi tīng dào.
text_en: Sorting through Mother's belongings, I found a letter at the back of her vanity table drawer. The envelope bore an address and stamp, but had never been mailed. The addressee was a name I'd never heard: Zhou Ruolan. The paper was yellowed, ink faded — written at least twenty years ago. I hesitated but opened it. The contents were ordinary: Mother telling Zhou Ruolan she'd been learning to bake, that her husband had been promoted, that her daughter had started elementary school. The letter ended: 'I often think of those nights we stayed up talking in the dormitory. The future seemed so far away then; looking back, those nights were the most precious. I hope all is well with you.' I tried the address — demolished. The name — no results. Why had Mother written but never sent it? Perhaps the address was uncertain. Perhaps she reread it, planned to revise, and let procrastination win. Perhaps she never intended to send it — writing was the purpose, like speaking to a distant shadow, needing no reply. I refolded the letter and placed it back.

title: The Rhythm of a Calligrapher's Brush
text_zh: 楼下的赵老先生每天清晨在小区广场上用水写书法。他用的是一支特制的海绵笔，蘸清水，在灰色的水泥地面上写大字。字写完以后，随着水分蒸发，笔画会在几分钟内逐渐消失。他写了几十年，没有留下过一个字。我有一次站在旁边看了整整一个小时。他写字的节奏非常独特——不是匀速的，而是有呼吸感的。起笔的时候很慢，笔尖在地面上停顿了一瞬，好像在酝酿力量。然后行笔加速，流畅地划过，像河水遇到了下坡。到了转折处，又突然减速，甚至完全停住，然后以一个精确的角度拐弯。收笔的时候，笔尖缓缓提起，在空中画出一个看不见的弧线。每一个字都像一次完整的呼吸：吸气、屏息、呼气。我问他为什么不用墨写在纸上，至少可以留下来。他停下笔，看着地上正在消失的字，说：「你看，这个『永』字的最后一笔快要不见了。你觉得它消失了吗？」我说看不见了应该算消失了。他摇摇头：「你刚才看到了。你记住了。它就没有消失。书法的意义不在于那个字最终留在哪里，而在于写的那一刻，你的心、你的手、你的呼吸和那个字之间达成的默契。那个瞬间是永恒的，不需要纸来证明。」
text_pinyin: Lóu xià de Zhào lǎo xiānsheng měi tiān qīngchén zài xiǎoqū guǎngchǎng shàng yòng shuǐ xiě shūfǎ. Tā yòng de shì yī zhī tèzhì de hǎimián bǐ, zhàn qīng shuǐ, zài huīsè de shuǐní dìmiàn shàng xiě dà zì. Zì xiě wán yǐhòu, suízhe shuǐfèn zhēngfā, bǐhuà huì zài jǐ fēnzhōng nèi zhújiàn xiāoshī. Tā xiě le jǐ shí nián, méiyǒu liú xià guò yī gè zì. Wǒ yǒu yī cì zhàn zài pángbiān kàn le zhěngzhěng yī gè xiǎoshí. Tā xiě zì de jiézòu fēicháng dútè——bú shì yúnsù de, ér shì yǒu hūxī gǎn de. Qǐ bǐ de shíhou hěn màn, bǐ jiān zài dìmiàn shàng tíngdùn le yī shùn, hǎoxiàng zài yùnniàng lìliang. Ránhòu xíng bǐ jiāsù, liúchàng de huà guò, xiàng héshuǐ yù dào le xià pō. Dào le zhuǎnzhé chù, yòu tūrán jiǎnsù, shènzhì wánquán tíng zhù, ránhòu yǐ yī gè jīngquè de jiǎodù guǎiwān. Shōu bǐ de shíhou, bǐ jiān huǎnhuǎn tí qǐ, zài kōngzhōng huà chū yī gè kàn bú jiàn de húxiàn. Měi yī gè zì dōu xiàng yī cì wánzhěng de hūxī: xīqì, bǐngxī, hūqì. Wǒ wèn tā wèishéme bú yòng mò xiě zài zhǐ shàng, zhìshǎo kěyǐ liú xiàlái. Tā tíng xià bǐ, kàn zhe dì shàng zhèngzài xiāoshī de zì, shuō:「Nǐ kàn, zhège 'yǒng' zì de zuìhòu yī bǐ kuài yào bú jiàn le. Nǐ juéde tā xiāoshī le ma?」Wǒ shuō kàn bú jiàn le yīnggāi suàn xiāoshī le. Tā yáoyao tóu:「Nǐ gāngcái kàn dào le. Nǐ jì zhù le. Tā jiù méiyǒu xiāoshī. Shūfǎ de yìyì bú zàiyú nàge zì zuìzhōng liú zài nǎlǐ, ér zàiyú xiě de nà yī kè, nǐ de xīn, nǐ de shǒu, nǐ de hūxī hé nàge zì zhījiān dáchéng de mòqì. Nàge shùnjiān shì yǒnghéng de, bú xūyào zhǐ lái zhèngmíng.」
text_en: Old Mr. Zhao downstairs practices calligraphy with water on the plaza every morning. He uses a sponge brush, dips it in clear water, and writes large characters on gray concrete. Within minutes the strokes disappear as the water evaporates. He has written for decades without leaving a single character behind. Once I watched for a full hour. His rhythm was distinctive — not uniform, but breathing. The opening stroke was slow, the brush tip pausing as if gathering force. Then it accelerated, gliding smoothly, like a river meeting a slope. At turning points it decelerated, even stopped, then turned at a precise angle. At the closing stroke, the tip rose slowly, tracing an invisible arc through air. Every character was one complete breath: inhale, hold, exhale. I asked why he didn't use ink on paper, so something would remain. He looked at the vanishing characters and said: 'The last stroke of this yong is about to vanish. Do you think it has disappeared?' I said if you can't see it, it should count as gone. He shook his head: 'You just saw it. You remember it. Then it hasn't disappeared. Calligraphy's meaning isn't where the character rests, but the moment of writing — the accord between heart, hand, breath, and character. That instant is eternal. It doesn't need paper to prove it.'

title: Moonlight through Paper Screens
text_zh: 京都的旅馆房间里有一扇障子门——就是那种用木框架糊上和纸的日式拉门。白天的时候，阳光透过纸面洒进来，光线被纸张过滤成一种柔和的、均匀的漫射光，不刺眼，也不阴暗，恰好处于让人舒适的中间地带。但真正让我震动的是夜里。凌晨两点多我醒来，房间一片漆黑，但障子门上映着一片银白色的光。月光。它穿过庭院里的枫树，被枝条切割成碎片，再经过和纸的过滤，变成了一层朦胧的、流动的光影。风吹过的时候，枫树枝轻轻摇晃，纸门上的光影也随之缓缓移动，像一场无声的默片在白色的银幕上放映。我躺在榻榻米上，看了很久。这种光让我想起一个词：「幽玄」——日本美学中对那种隐约的、暗示性的、不完全呈现的美的概括。月光本身已经很美了，但经过纸的过滤和树影的切割之后，它变成了另一种东西：一种被削减了亮度却增加了层次感的美。就像一首诗，最好的意象不是直接说出来的那个，而是被留白和暗示包裹着的那个。第二天退房的时候，前台的老太太问我住得怎么样。我说：「月光很好。」她笑了，好像在说：你看到了。
text_pinyin: Jīngdū de lǚguǎn fángjiān lǐ yǒu yī shàn zhàngzi mén——jiùshì nà zhǒng yòng mù kuàngjià hú shàng hé zhǐ de Rìshì lā mén. Báitiān de shíhou, yángguāng tòu guò zhǐ miàn sǎ jìnlái, guāngxiàn bèi zhǐzhāng guòlǜ chéng yī zhǒng róuhé de, jūnyún de mànshè guāng, bú cìyǎn, yě bù yīn'àn, qiàhǎo chǔyú ràng rén shūshì de zhōngjiān dìdài. Dàn zhēnzhèng ràng wǒ zhèndòng de shì yè lǐ. Língchén liǎng diǎn duō wǒ xǐng lái, fángjiān yī piàn qīhēi, dàn zhàngzi mén shàng yìng zhe yī piàn yínbái sè de guāng. Yuèguāng. Tā chuān guò tíngyuàn lǐ de fēngshù, bèi zhītiáo qiēgē chéng suìpiàn, zài jīngguò hé zhǐ de guòlǜ, biàn chéng le yī céng ménglóng de, liúdòng de guāngyǐng. Fēng chuī guò de shíhou, fēngshù zhī qīngqīng yáohuàng, zhǐ mén shàng de guāngyǐng yě suí zhī huǎnhuǎn yídòng, xiàng yī chǎng wúshēng de mòpiàn zài báisè de yínmù shàng fàngyìng. Wǒ tǎng zài tàtàmǐ shàng, kàn le hěn jiǔ. Zhè zhǒng guāng ràng wǒ xiǎng qǐ yī gè cí:「yōuxuán」——Rìběn měixué zhōng duì nà zhǒng yǐnyuē de, ànshì xìng de, bù wánquán chéngxiàn de měi de gàikuò. Yuèguāng běnshēn yǐjīng hěn měi le, dàn jīngguò zhǐ de guòlǜ hé shùyǐng de qiēgē zhīhòu, tā biàn chéng le lìng yī zhǒng dōngxi: yī zhǒng bèi xuējiǎn le liàngdù què zēngjiā le céngcì gǎn de měi. Jiù xiàng yī shǒu shī, zuì hǎo de yìxiàng bú shì zhíjiē shuō chūlái de nàge, ér shì bèi liúbái hé ànshì bāoguǒ zhe de nàge. Dì èr tiān tuì fáng de shíhou, qiántái de lǎo tàitai wèn wǒ zhù de zěnmeyàng. Wǒ shuō:「Yuèguāng hěn hǎo.」Tā xiào le, hǎoxiàng zài shuō: nǐ kàn dào le.
text_en: The Kyoto inn room had a shoji door — wooden frame covered in washi paper. During the day, sunlight passed through, filtered into soft, even diffused light. But what moved me was the night. I woke past two in the morning; the room was dark, but on the shoji appeared a wash of silver-white light. Moonlight. It had passed through courtyard maples, been cut into fragments by branches, then filtered through washi into hazy, flowing light and shadow. When the wind blew, maple branches swayed and the patterns shifted slowly, like a silent film projected on a white screen. I lay on the tatami watching for a long time. This light reminded me of yugen — the Japanese aesthetic of beauty that is suggestive, never fully revealed. Moonlight is already beautiful, but filtered through paper and carved by tree shadows, it becomes something else: diminished in brightness, deepened in layers. As in a poem, the finest image is not stated directly but wrapped in negative space. When I checked out, the elderly woman at the front desk asked how my stay was. I said: 'The moonlight was wonderful.' She smiled, as if to say: you saw it.

questions.q_zh: 作者用「幽玄」这个概念来形容什么？
questions.q_en: What does the author use the concept of 'yugen' to describe?

options.text: 月光经过纸和树影过滤后形成的隐约的、不完全呈现的美
options.pinyin: yuèguāng jīngguò zhǐ hé shùyǐng guòlǜ hòu xíngchéng de yǐnyuē de, bù wánquán chéngxiàn de měi
options.text_en: The suggestive, not fully revealed beauty formed by moonlight filtered through paper and tree shadows

options.pinyin: Jīngdū lǚguǎn de jiànzhú fēnggé
options.text_en: The architectural style of the Kyoto inn

options.pinyin: Rìběn liàolǐ de bǎi pán fāngshì
options.text_en: The plating style of Japanese cuisine

questions.explanation: Yugen describes the suggestive, partially concealed beauty created when moonlight filters through paper screens and tree shadows—not full revelation but implication.

questions.q_zh: 作者退房时说「月光很好」，前台老太太的反应说明了什么？
questions.q_en: When the author said 'the moonlight was wonderful' at checkout, what did the elderly receptionist's reaction imply?

options.pinyin: tā juéde kèrén hěn qíguài
options.text_en: She thought the guest was strange

options.text: 她知道那扇纸门上的月光是这间旅馆特有的美，客人领悟到了
options.pinyin: tā zhīdào nà shàn zhǐ mén shàng de yuèguāng shì zhè jiān lǚguǎn tèyǒu de měi, kèrén lǐngwù dào le
options.text_en: She knew the moonlight on the paper door was a beauty unique to this inn, and the guest had perceived it

options.text: 她只是出于礼貌在微笑
options.pinyin: tā zhǐshì chūyú lǐmào zài wēixiào
options.text_en: She was just smiling out of politeness

questions.explanation: The receptionist's knowing expression implied she understood the moonlight on the paper door was the inn's hidden treasure, and the guest had perceived it.

title: The Last Page of a Notebook
text_zh: 我有一个习惯：每用完一本笔记本，在翻到最后一页的时候，总会有一种奇怪的仪式感。不是因为最后一页写了什么特别的内容——通常它和前面的页面一样，记着会议纪要或者零散的想法。这种仪式感来自于「结束」本身。一本笔记本从空白到写满，通常需要三到四个月。这段时间里发生的事，有些被记录了下来，有些没有。被记录的部分构成了一个片面的、不完整的日常档案。你翻回去看，会发现三个月前困扰你的那个问题，现在已经完全忘了；上个月某次会议记的要点，当时觉得至关重要，现在读起来平淡无奇。时间就是这样一个奇妙的编辑器，它不断地重新排列事情的优先级，把你以为重要的东西推到边缘，把你忽略的细节提到前面。我最珍惜的往往不是正式的笔记内容，而是页边的涂鸦——开会走神时画的小方块、等电话时随手写的一串无意义的曲线、某天心情很好时在角落画的一朵花。这些痕迹比文字更诚实，因为它们是无意识留下的，没有经过任何编辑和筛选。写完最后一页以后，我会把笔记本合上，放进书架最高层的一个纸箱里。那里已经有二十多本了，按时间顺序排列。我从来不翻旧的本子，但知道它们在那里，就觉得安心。好像过去的时间虽然回不来了，但至少没有完全消散——它被压缩在那些泛黄的纸页之间，安静地等着。
text_pinyin: Wǒ yǒu yī gè xíguàn: měi yòng wán yī běn bǐjìběn, zài fān dào zuìhòu yī yè de shíhou, zǒng huì yǒu yī zhǒng qíguài de yíshì gǎn. Bú shì yīnwèi zuìhòu yī yè xiě le shénme tèbié de nèiróng——tōngcháng tā hé qiánmiàn de yèmiàn yīyàng, jì zhe huìyì jìyào huòzhě língsǎn de xiǎngfǎ. Zhè zhǒng yíshì gǎn láizì yú「jiéshù」běnshēn. Yī běn bǐjìběn cóng kòngbái dào xiě mǎn, tōngcháng xūyào sān dào sì gè yuè. Zhè duàn shíjiān lǐ fāshēng de shì, yǒu xiē bèi jìlù le xiàlái, yǒu xiē méiyǒu. Bèi jìlù de bùfen gòuchéng le yī gè piànmiàn de, bù wánzhěng de rìcháng dǎng'àn. Nǐ fān huíqù kàn, huì fāxiàn sān gè yuè qián kùnrǎo nǐ de nàge wèntí, xiànzài yǐjīng wánquán wàng le; shàng gè yuè mǒu cì huìyì jì de yàodiǎn, dāngshí juéde zhìguān zhòngyào, xiànzài dú qǐlái píngdàn wúqí. Shíjiān jiùshì zhèyàng yī gè qímiào de biānjí qì, tā búduàn de chóngxīn páiliè shìqíng de yōuxiān jí, bǎ nǐ yǐwéi zhòngyào de dōngxi tuī dào biānyuán, bǎ nǐ hūlüè de xìjié tí dào qiánmiàn. Wǒ zuì zhēnxī de wǎngwǎng bú shì zhèngshì de bǐjì nèiróng, ér shì yè biān de túyā——kāi huì zǒushén shí huà de xiǎo fāngkuài, děng diànhuà shí suíshǒu xiě de yī chuàn wú yìyì de qūxiàn, mǒu tiān xīnqíng hěn hǎo shí zài jiǎoluò huà de yī duǒ huā. Zhèxiē hénjì bǐ wénzì gèng chéngshí, yīnwèi tāmen shì wúyìshí liú xià de, méiyǒu jīngguò rènhé biānjí hé shāixuǎn. Xiě wán zuìhòu yī yè yǐhòu, wǒ huì bǎ bǐjìběn hé shàng, fàng jìn shūjià zuì gāo céng de yī gè zhǐxiāng lǐ. Nàlǐ yǐjīng yǒu èrshí duō běn le, àn shíjiān shùnxù páiliè. Wǒ cónglái bù fān jiù de běnzi, dàn zhīdào tāmen zài nàlǐ, jiù juéde ānxīn. Hǎoxiàng guòqù de shíjiān suīrán huí bù lái le, dàn zhìshǎo méiyǒu wánquán xiāosàn——tā bèi yāsuō zài nàxiē fàn huáng de zhǐ yè zhījiān, ānjìng de děng zhe.
text_en: I have a habit: finishing a notebook's last page always brings a sense of ceremony. Not because the last page contains anything special — usually meeting notes or scattered thoughts. The ceremony comes from ending itself. A notebook fills in three to four months. Of what happened, some things were recorded, some not. Flipping back, the problem troubling you three months ago has been forgotten; key points from last month's meeting now read as unremarkable. Time is a marvelous editor — continually reshuffling priorities, pushing what felt important to the margins, elevating overlooked details. What I treasure most is not formal notes but margin doodles — squares drawn while zoning out in a meeting, curves scrawled while waiting for a call, a flower sketched when I was in a good mood. These traces are more honest than written words because they were left unconsciously, without editing. After the last page, I close the notebook and place it in a cardboard box on my bookcase's highest shelf. Over twenty are in there, in chronological order. I never go back through old ones, but knowing they're there brings peace. As if the past, though it can't return, has not completely dissipated — compressed between yellowing pages, quietly waiting.

questions.q_zh: 作者为什么说页边的涂鸦比正式笔记更诚实？
questions.q_en: Why does the author say the margin doodles are more honest than formal notes?

options.pinyin: yīnwèi túyā huà de gèng piàoliang
options.text_en: Because the doodles are prettier

options.text: 因为涂鸦是无意识留下的，没有经过编辑和筛选
options.pinyin: yīnwèi túyā shì wúyìshí liú xià de, méiyǒu jīngguò biānjí hé shāixuǎn
options.text_en: Because doodles are left unconsciously, without editing or filtering

options.text: 因为正式笔记写得太潦草
options.pinyin: yīnwèi zhèngshì bǐjì xiě de tài liáocǎo
options.text_en: Because formal notes are too messy

questions.explanation: Margin doodles are unconscious and unedited—the hand moves without the mind censoring. Formal notes are curated; doodles bypass that filter.

questions.q_zh: 作者把时间比喻成什么？
questions.q_en: What does the author compare time to?

options.text: 一个奇妙的编辑器，不断重新排列事情的优先级
options.pinyin: yī gè qímiào de biānjí qì, búduàn chóngxīn páiliè shìqíng de yōuxiān jí
options.text_en: A marvelous editor that continually reshuffles the priority of things

options.text: 一条河流，永远不会停下
options.pinyin: yī tiáo héliú, yǒngyuǎn bú huì tíng xià
options.text_en: A river that never stops

options.pinyin: yī běn yǒngyuǎn xiě bù wán de shū
options.text_en: A book that can never be finished

questions.explanation: Time acts as an editor, continually reordering what matters. What seemed trivial when written may later prove to be the notebook's most important content.

title: Autumn Leaves in a Courtyard
text_zh: 我家老宅的院子里有一棵梧桐树，据说是祖父那一辈种下的。每年秋天，梧桐叶变黄脱落，整个院子会被铺上一层金色的地毯。小时候，父亲每天早上都会拿着扫帚把落叶扫成一堆，装进竹篓里。我问他为什么不等叶子全落完了再扫——反正扫完了第二天又会落一层。他说：「扫落叶不是为了让院子干净。院子从来不会真正干净，因为树还活着，还在落叶。扫落叶是为了跟这棵树打招呼——告诉它，我看到了，我知道又是一天了。」这个回答当时让我觉得莫名其妙。但现在我自己住在这个院子里，每天早上拿起扫帚的时候，终于明白了他的意思。扫落叶确实不是一个关于清洁的动作，而是一个关于时间的仪式。每一片叶子的形状、颜色、干燥程度都不一样。刚落下来的叶子还带着柔软的弧度和残存的绿意，已经落了几天的叶子则变得卷曲、干脆，踩上去会发出清脆的碎裂声。你可以通过观察落叶来感受季节的推进：九月的叶子只是边缘微微泛黄，十月的叶子整片金黄，十一月的叶子则开始发褐，带着一种即将归于泥土的沉稳。扫帚划过石板地面的沙沙声，加上偶尔从树上旋转飘下的新叶，构成了一个安静而完整的秋天的早晨。父亲没有教过我任何关于美学或者哲学的东西，但他教会了我扫落叶。现在想来，这也许是同一件事。
text_pinyin: Wǒ jiā lǎo zhái de yuànzi lǐ yǒu yī kē wútóng shù, jùshuō shì zǔfù nà yī bèi zhǒng xià de. Měi nián qiūtiān, wútóng yè biàn huáng tuōluò, zhěnggè yuànzi huì bèi pū shàng yī céng jīnsè de dìtǎn. Xiǎo shíhou, fùqīn měi tiān zǎoshang dōu huì ná zhe sàozhou bǎ luòyè sǎo chéng yī duī, zhuāng jìn zhúlǒu lǐ. Wǒ wèn tā wèishéme bù děng yèzi quán luò wán le zài sǎo——fǎnzhèng sǎo wán le dì èr tiān yòu huì luò yī céng. Tā shuō:「Sǎo luòyè bú shì wèile ràng yuànzi gānjìng. Yuànzi cónglái bú huì zhēnzhèng gānjìng, yīnwèi shù hái huó zhe, hái zài luòyè. Sǎo luòyè shì wèile gēn zhè kē shù dǎ zhāohu——gàosù tā, wǒ kàn dào le, wǒ zhīdào yòu shì yī tiān le.」Zhège huídá dāngshí ràng wǒ juéde mòmíng qímiào. Dàn xiànzài wǒ zìjǐ zhù zài zhège yuànzi lǐ, měi tiān zǎoshang ná qǐ sàozhou de shíhou, zhōngyú míngbai le tā de yìsi. Sǎo luòyè quèshí bú shì yī gè guānyú qīngjié de dòngzuò, ér shì yī gè guānyú shíjiān de yíshì. Měi yī piàn yèzi de xíngzhuàng, yánsè, gānzào chéngdù dōu bù yīyàng. Gāng luò xiàlái de yèzi hái dài zhe róuruǎn de húdù hé cáncún de lǜyì, yǐjīng luò le jǐ tiān de yèzi zé biàn de juǎnqū, gāncuì, cǎi shàngqù huì fāchū qīngcuì de suìliè shēng. Nǐ kěyǐ tōngguò guānchá luòyè lái gǎnshòu jìjié de tuījìn: jiǔ yuè de yèzi zhǐshì biānyuán wēiwēi fàn huáng, shí yuè de yèzi zhěng piàn jīnhuáng, shíyī yuè de yèzi zé kāishǐ fā hè, dài zhe yī zhǒng jíjiāng guī yú nítǔ de chénwěn. Sàozhou huà guò shíbǎn dìmiàn de shāshā shēng, jiā shàng ǒu'ěr cóng shù shàng xuánzhuǎn piāo xià de xīn yè, gòuchéng le yī gè ānjìng ér wánzhěng de qiūtiān de zǎochén. Fùqīn méiyǒu jiāo guò wǒ rènhé guānyú měixué huòzhě zhéxué de dōngxi, dàn tā jiāo huì le wǒ sǎo luòyè. Xiànzài xiǎng lái, zhè yěxǔ shì tóng yī jiàn shì.
text_en: In the courtyard of our old family home stands a parasol tree, planted by my grandfather's generation. Every autumn the leaves turn yellow, carpeting the courtyard in gold. When I was small, Father would sweep the fallen leaves each morning into a bamboo basket. I asked why he didn't wait until all leaves had fallen — a new layer would fall the next day. He said: 'Sweeping leaves isn't about keeping the courtyard clean. The courtyard will never be clean, because the tree is alive, still shedding. Sweeping is about greeting this tree — telling it: I see you, I know another day has come.' That answer baffled me then. But now I live in this courtyard, and each morning I pick up the broom and understand. Sweeping leaves is not cleaning but a ritual of time. Every leaf differs in shape, color, dryness. Fresh leaves carry a soft curve and lingering green; leaves down several days have curled crisp, crunching underfoot. You can feel the season's advance: September's leaves are faintly yellow at edges, October's fully golden, November's begin to brown. The swish of the broom across stone paving, joined by a new leaf spiraling down, composes a quiet autumn morning. Father never taught me aesthetics or philosophy, but he taught me to sweep fallen leaves. Looking back, perhaps those are the same thing.

title: The Weight of a Well-Used Key
title_zh: 一把用旧的钥匙的分量
text_zh: 搬家的时候，我从抽屉里翻出了一把旧钥匙。不是现在住的房子的，也不是上一套的——是我大学毕业后租的第一间房子的钥匙。房东当时多给了我一把备用的，退租的时候我还了一把，另一把不知怎么一直留在了身边，跟着我搬了四次家。这把钥匙比普通钥匙重一些。不是因为材质不同，而是因为用得太久，边缘被磨得光滑圆润，握在手里有一种温润的质感，像一块被河水冲刷了很久的鹅卵石。齿痕也变浅了，如果真的拿去开门，恐怕已经不太灵了。但我一直舍不得扔。那间房子在城南的一栋老楼里，二楼，朝北，冬天很冷，夏天倒还凉快。二十平米，一张床、一张桌子、一个衣柜，塞得满满的。厨房在走廊尽头，和隔壁的邻居共用。水管经常漏水，房东永远在说「下周来修」。但那是我第一次拥有属于自己的空间——不是父母的家，不是学校的宿舍，而是一个我用自己赚的钱租来的、可以随时锁上门把全世界关在外面的小房间。每天晚上下班回来，把钥匙插进锁孔、听到「咔嗒」一声的那个瞬间，就是一天中最安心的时刻。那个声音意味着：外面的一切暂时跟你无关了，这里是你的。现在我住的房子大了很多，用的是指纹锁，开门无声无息。方便是方便了，但总觉得少了点什么。
text_pinyin: Bān jiā de shíhou, wǒ cóng chōuti lǐ fān chū le yī bǎ jiù yàoshi. Bú shì xiànzài zhù de fángzi de, yě bú shì shàng yī tào de——shì wǒ dàxué bìyè hòu zū de dì yī jiān fángzi de yàoshi. Fángdōng dāngshí duō gěi le wǒ yī bǎ bèiyòng de, tuì zū de shíhou wǒ huán le yī bǎ, lìng yī bǎ bù zhī zěnme yīzhí liú zài le shēnbiān, gēn zhe wǒ bān le sì cì jiā. Zhè bǎ yàoshi bǐ pǔtōng yàoshi zhòng yīxiē. Bú shì yīnwèi cáizhì bùtóng, ér shì yīnwèi yòng de tài jiǔ, biānyuán bèi mó de guānghuá yuánrùn, wò zài shǒu lǐ yǒu yī zhǒng wēnrùn de zhìgǎn, xiàng yī kuài bèi héshuǐ chōngshuā le hěn jiǔ de éluǎnshí. Chǐhén yě biàn qiǎn le, rúguǒ zhēn de ná qù kāi mén, kǒngpà yǐjīng bú tài líng le. Dàn wǒ yīzhí shě bù de rēng. Nà jiān fángzi zài chéng nán de yī dòng lǎo lóu lǐ, èr lóu, cháo běi, dōngtiān hěn lěng, xiàtiān dào hái liángkuai. Èrshí píngmǐ, yī zhāng chuáng, yī zhāng zhuōzi, yī gè yīguì, sāi de mǎnmǎn de. Chúfáng zài zǒuláng jìntóu, hé gébì de línjū gòngyòng. Shuǐguǎn jīngcháng lòushuǐ, fángdōng yǒngyuǎn zài shuō「xià zhōu lái xiū」. Dàn nà shì wǒ dì yī cì yōngyǒu shǔyú zìjǐ de kōngjiān——bú shì fùmǔ de jiā, bú shì xuéxiào de sùshè, ér shì yī gè wǒ yòng zìjǐ zhuàn de qián zū lái de, kěyǐ suíshí suǒ shàng mén bǎ quán shìjiè guān zài wàimiàn de xiǎo fángjiān. Měi tiān wǎnshang xià bān huílái, bǎ yàoshi chā jìn suǒ kǒng, tīng dào「kādā」yī shēng de nàge shùnjiān, jiùshì yī tiān zhōng zuì ānxīn de shíkè. Nàge shēngyīn yìwèi zhe: wàimiàn de yīqiè zànshí gēn nǐ wúguān le, zhèlǐ shì nǐ de. Xiànzài wǒ zhù de fángzi dà le hěn duō, yòng de shì zhǐwén suǒ, kāi mén wúshēng wúxī. Fāngbiàn shì fāngbiàn le, dàn zǒng juéde shǎo le diǎn shénme. Yěxǔ shǎo de jiùshì nà yī shēng「kādā」——nàge xiǎo xiǎo de, quèrèn nǐ dào jiā le de shēngyīn.
text_en: While moving, I dug out an old key from a drawer. Not for my current place, nor the one before — the key to my first apartment after graduating college. The landlord gave me a spare; when I moved out I returned one, the other somehow stayed, following me through four moves. This key is slightly heavier than ordinary. Not from different material, but from use — edges worn smooth and round, sitting in the hand with a warm, polished texture like a river pebble. The teeth have grown shallow; it probably wouldn't open anything now. But I've never thrown it away. That apartment was in an old building in the south of the city. Twenty square meters: bed, desk, wardrobe, all crammed in. Shared kitchen at the corridor's end. Pipes leaked constantly; the landlord forever promising repairs next week. But it was the first space that was mine — not parents' home, not dormitory, but a room rented with money I earned, where I could lock the door and shut the world outside. Every evening, the instant I inserted the key and heard that 'click' was the most reassuring moment of the day. That sound meant: everything out there is temporarily none of your concern. The house I live in now is larger, with a fingerprint lock that opens silently. Convenient, but something is missing.

questions.q_zh: 作者为什么说这把旧钥匙比普通钥匙重？
questions.q_en: Why does the author say this old key is heavier than an ordinary key?

options.text: 因为钥匙是用特殊金属制成的
options.pinyin: yīnwèi yàoshi shì yòng tèshū jīnshǔ zhì chéng de
options.text_en: Because the key is made of a special metal

options.text: 因为用得太久，边缘被磨得光滑圆润，手感像河水冲刷过的鹅卵石
options.pinyin: yīnwèi yòng de tài jiǔ, biānyuán bèi mó de guānghuá yuánrùn, shǒugǎn xiàng héshuǐ chōngshuā guò de éluǎnshí
options.text_en: Because it was used so long the edges wore smooth, feeling like a river-polished pebble

options.text: 因为钥匙上挂了很多装饰品
options.pinyin: yīnwèi yàoshi shàng guà le hěn duō zhuāngshì pǐn
options.text_en: Because many decorations were hung on the key

questions.explanation: Years of use wore the key's edges smooth, giving it the feel of a river-polished pebble. Physical wear added tactile weight and character.

questions.q_zh: 作者觉得现在的指纹锁少了什么？
questions.q_en: What does the author feel is missing from the current fingerprint lock?

options.pinyin: ānquán xìng búgòu gāo
options.text_en: It's not secure enough

options.pinyin: kāi mén tài màn le
options.text_en: It's too slow to open

options.text: 少了钥匙转动锁孔时「咔嗒」一声确认到家的声音
options.pinyin: shǎo le yàoshi zhuǎndòng suǒ kǒng shí 'kādā' yī shēng quèrèn dào jiā de shēngyīn
options.text_en: The 'click' sound of the key turning in the lock that confirmed arriving home

questions.explanation: The fingerprint lock opens silently. The old key's 'click' confirmed arrival home—an acoustic ritual of return that the new technology eliminated.
